results.title_inferred: Illustrated Plates Describing Ancient Egyptian and Nubian Pathological Specimens
results.original_text: Compound fracture of both bones of the left forearm in a patient who died fifty centuries ago within a few hours of the injury. Palm fibre had been pushed through the skin wound into the broken ulna (see the left-hand fragment) to stop the hemorrhage. This may have been the source of the infection that proved fatal. The broken bones were set in bark splints which formed a tube tied up with linen. This is one of a pair of the earliest splints discovered, the other set having been used for setting the broken thigh of a boy buried in the same cemetery. PLATE VII — A very early stage in the development of tubercular caries of the spine—a small mass of caseous tubercular material is still present in the eroded cavity. (Nubia, 2,000 B.C.). LIST OF ILLUSTRATIONS PLATE VIII — Osteosarcoma of the femur from a tomb of the Fifth Dynasty. PLATE IX — The base of the skull of an ancient Egyptian showing the extensive deficiency caused by cancer of the throat. PLATE X — The top of the head of Rameses the Great showing calcified arteries. PLATE XI — Hands of an early Syrian Christian (circa 6th century A.D.) from Assouan, suffering from leprosy. PLATE XII — The mummified liver of a Priestess of Amon (Twenty-first Dynasty) showing the gall bladder packed with stones. PLATE XIII — The mummy of the patient whose liver is shown in Plate XII. PLATE XIV — The jaw of a Predynastic Egyptian showing the wearing down of the teeth with exposure of the pulp cavities. From the alveolar abscess around the roots of the second and third molars inflammation extended upon the surface of the jaw around the inferior dental foramen which transmits the nerve to the skin of the chin-region. PLATE XV — Skull of Pharaoh Akhenaton, showing slight hydrocephalus and indications of acromegaly. PLATE XVI — Skull of a decapitated Nubian whose head was cut off by a Roman [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off].
results.translation:
Compound fracture of both bones of the left forearm in a patient who died fifty centuries ago within a few hours of the injury. Palm fibre had been pushed through the skin wound into the broken ulna (see the left-hand fragment) in order to stop the hemorrhage. This may have been the source of the infection that proved fatal. The broken bones were set in bark splints which formed a tube tied up with linen. This is one of a pair of the earliest splints discovered; the other set was used for setting the broken thigh of a boy buried in the same cemetery.

PLATE VII: A very early stage in the development of tubercular caries [Pott's disease] of the spine — a small mass of caseous tubercular material is still present in the eroded cavity. (Nubia, 2,000 B.C.)

PLATE VIII: Osteosarcoma of the femur from a tomb of the Fifth Dynasty.

PLATE IX: The base of the skull of an ancient Egyptian showing the extensive bony deficiency caused by cancer of the throat.

PLATE X: The top of the head of Rameses the Great showing calcified arteries.

PLATE XI: Hands of an early Syrian Christian (circa 6th century A.D.) from Assouan, suffering from leprosy.

PLATE XII: The mummified liver of a Priestess of Amon (Twenty-first Dynasty) showing the gall bladder packed with stones [cholelithiasis].

PLATE XIII: The mummy of the patient whose liver is shown in Plate XII.

PLATE XIV: The jaw of a Predynastic Egyptian showing the wearing down of the teeth with exposure of the pulp cavities. From the alveolar abscess around the roots of the second and third molars, inflammation extended over the surface of the jaw around the inferior dental foramen, which transmits the nerve to the skin of the chin region.

PLATE XV: Skull of Pharaoh Akhenaton, showing slight hydrocephalus and indications of acromegaly.

PLATE XVI: Skull of a decapitated Nubian whose head was cut off by a Roman [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off].

Several OCR artefacts were silently corrected (e.g., 'Iche' → 'The'; 'PLATE XX' → 'PLATE X', which fits the sequential numbering; leading dashes and extraneous characters removed). The final entry for Plate XVI is truncated mid-sentence. Plate numbering anomaly: 'PLATE XX' in the source almost certainly represents a misprint or OCR error for 'PLATE X', as the sequence runs VII through XVI without gap.

results.original_text: sliced off part of the right temple bone. PAGE 173 173 175 175 175 177 179 179 179 INTRODUCTION It is often assumed even by the most learned historians that the history of medicine began with the Greeks, and that before the time of Hippocrates there was little or nothing that can be called a science of medicine. Yet for more than thirty centuries before the emancipation of human reason in Ionia, numerous practitioners had been attempting to diagnose and treat disease in Egypt, Mesopotamia, and elsewhere. The Papyrus Ebers is the longest and the most famous of the documents relating to this more ancient practice of medicine. The Papyrus, although written about 1500 B.C., is really a collection of bits and pieces of folk-lore, much of it five and some of it probably twenty centuries older. It is expressed in archaic phraseology, so that many of the terms relating to the diseases and drugs are impossible to identify. In the face of these insuperable difficulties, it is not surprising that most scholars have refrained from risking their reputation for scholarship by pretending to translate a technical document in which many of the technical terms are at present untranslatable. An heroic attempt to provide a translation in German was made, however, many years ago by Dr. H. Joachim. This attempt naturally provoked many criticisms, especially from those scholars who had the philological knowledge to recognize the solecisms. But if the time is not yet ripe for an adequate translation, the Papyrus is so interesting that no excuse should be necessary for providing a rendering in English of the only version at present available for those who cannot read the scripts of Ancient Egypt. Hence Dr. Cyril Bryan is to be commended for giving us in English an interpretation based upon Dr. Joachim's translation, though not slavishly following it. INTRODUCTION

It is often assumed, even by the most learned historians, that the history of medicine began with the Greeks, and that before the time of Hippocrates there was little or nothing that could be called a science of medicine. Yet for more than thirty centuries before the emancipation of human reason in Ionia, numerous practitioners had been attempting to diagnose and treat disease in Egypt, Mesopotamia, and elsewhere. The Papyrus Ebers is the longest and most famous of the documents relating to this more ancient practice of medicine. The Papyrus, although written around 1500 B.C., is in reality a collection of fragments of folk-lore, much of it five centuries older and some of it probably twenty centuries older still. It is expressed in archaic phraseology, so that many of the terms relating to diseases and drugs are impossible to identify. In the face of these insuperable difficulties, it is not surprising that most scholars have refrained from risking their scholarly reputations by undertaking to translate a technical document in which many of the technical terms are at present untranslatable. A heroic attempt to provide a translation into German was made, however, many years ago by Dr. H. Joachim. This attempt naturally provoked many criticisms, especially from those scholars who had the philological knowledge to recognize its solecisms [grammatical errors or improprieties]. But if the time is not yet ripe for an adequate translation, the Papyrus is so interesting that no apology should be necessary for providing a rendering into English of the only version currently available to those who cannot read the scripts of Ancient Egypt. Hence Dr. Cyril Bryan is to be commended for giving us in English an interpretation based upon Dr. Joachim's translation, though not following it slavishly. In his book, Magician and Leech (1929), Mr. Warren R. Dawson has given a very useful summary of the history of the [text truncated]

results.title_inferred: Introduction to the Ebers Papyrus: Character, Contents, and Difficulties of Interpretation
results.original_text: miscellaneous collection of extracts and jottings collected from at least forty different sources. It consists mainly of a large collection of prescriptions for a number of named ailments, specifying the names of the drugs, the quantities of each, and the method of administration. A few sections deal with diagnosis and symptoms, another passage is physiological in character and describes the action of the heart and its vessels, and the concluding portion is surgical, being concerned with the treatment of wounds and suppurating sores. Freely interspersed amongst these elements are spells and incantations. The text covers 110 large columns (each of 22 lines on the average) in the original roll, which a modern editor has conveniently divided into 877 numbered sections of varying length. In considering the pathology of the papyri, the student is at once confronted with a host of difficulties. In the first place, the texts are full of philological and lexicographical problems, and they are written in a specialized, concise and aphoristic style abounding in syntactical difficulties. Many of the passages, as already noted, have been copied from older books and show evidence of textual corruption. But the greatest difficulty of all is the impossibility of translating into English the names of a great number of the maladies mentioned, and the names of most of the drugs. In the identification of the diseases with which the ancient physicians were trying to cope, and of the drugs and other measures they were using for therapeutic purposes, the study of folk-lore, both of the Egyptians and other people, and the survival of ancient remedies, opens up great possibilities. results.translation:
This segment is already in English and constitutes the introduction to a scholarly edition or commentary on the Ebers Papyrus. The text reads as follows:

The Ebers Papyrus is a miscellaneous collection of extracts and jottings gathered from at least forty different sources. It consists mainly of a large collection of prescriptions for a number of named ailments, specifying the names of the drugs, the quantities of each, and the method of administration. A few sections deal with diagnosis and symptoms; another passage is physiological in character and describes the action of the heart and its vessels; and the concluding portion is surgical, being concerned with the treatment of wounds and suppurating sores. Freely interspersed among these elements are spells and incantations. The text covers 110 large columns (each averaging 22 lines) in the original roll, which a modern editor has conveniently divided into 877 numbered sections of varying length.

In considering the pathology of the papyri, the student is at once confronted with a host of difficulties. In the first place, the texts are full of philological and lexicographical problems, and they are written in a specialized, concise, and aphoristic style abounding in syntactical difficulties. Many of the passages, as already noted, have been copied from older books and show evidence of textual corruption. But the greatest difficulty of all is the impossibility of translating into English the names of a great number of the maladies mentioned, and the names of most of the drugs. In the identification of the diseases with which the ancient physicians were trying to cope, and of the drugs and other measures they were using for therapeutic purposes, the study of folklore—both of the Egyptians and of other peoples—and the survival of ancient remedies, opens up great possibilities. It is the aim of Dr. Bryan's book to disseminate such information of ancient Egyptian medicine as we have and in particular to reveal the lacunae in our knowledge. For our hope of gaining a fuller insight into the real meaning of these cryptic papyri depends largely upon the evidence which [text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: Ebers Papyrus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ebers Papyrus (Papyrus Ebers), c. 1550 BCE, now held at the University of Leipzig; one of the oldest and most comprehensive ancient Egyptian medical documents
entities_mentioned.notes: The papyrus is described as comprising 110 columns divided into 877 numbered sections by a modern editor. It is noted as a composite document drawn from at least 40 sources.

entities_mentioned.name: heart and its vessels
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cardiovascular system; the passage likely refers to the ancient Egyptian concept of the 'metu' (channels or vessels) emanating from the heart
entities_mentioned.notes: The physiological passage describing the heart and vessels is a well-known section of the Ebers Papyrus, often cited in histories of cardiology and anatomy.

entities_mentioned.name: wounds and suppurating sores
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: General traumatic wounds and purulent (infected) lesions
entities_mentioned.notes: The surgical section of the Ebers Papyrus deals with practical wound management, distinct from the better-known Edwin Smith Papyrus which is more exclusively surgical.

entities_mentioned.name: Dr. Bryan's book
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely: Cyril P. Bryan, 'Ancient Egyptian Medicine: The Papyrus Ebers' (1930), a translation and commentary
entities_mentioned.notes: The reference to 'Dr. Bryan' and the goal of revealing lacunae in knowledge of ancient Egyptian medicine is consistent with C. P. Bryan's 1930 publication.

results.translator_notes: This segment is an English-language scholarly introduction (not a primary-language source requiring translation per se), likely from the prefatory matter of a 20th-century edition or commentary on the Ebers Papyrus, possibly Bryan (1930) or a related work. OCR errors corrected: 'collec- tion' (line break hyphen), 'xvi INTRODUCTION' (running header intruded into body text, silently removed from translation), 'lacune' corrected to 'lacunae', and 'whic' (truncated word at end) noted as a cut-off rather than an OCR error. The segment ends mid-sentence, indicating the source chunk is incomplete. No non-English primary source text is present in this chunk.

results.title_inferred: Egyptian Pharmacopoeia, the Ebers Papyrus, and the Relationship Between Ancient Egyptian and Old Testament Medicine
results.original_text: that although the merits of many of the ancient materials used in the most ancient pharmacopoeias are entirely illusory, at the same time the Egyptians did discover not a few substances whose virtues are recognized to-day as being quite genuine. For example many centuries before the Ebers Papyrus was written, the Egyptians had discovered and were using Castor Oil; they had recognized certain antiseptic virtues in Onions; and their belief in the virtues of a good many other substances, vegetable, animal, and mineral, which they used, has been shown to have a large measure of justification. Another source of information has recently been tapped and promises to refresh a department of archeology which was threatened with sterility. It adds enormously to the interest and importance of such documents as the Ebers Papyrus and also provides a new and fruitful method for interpreting them. In his treatise Die Sprache des Pentateuch in Ihren Beziehungen zum Ägyptischen, Professor A. S. Yahuda has recently emphasized the extent to which the earlier parts of the Old Testament—and the medical lore it contains—was inspired by Egypt. Both in language and subject-matter the Five Books of Moses reveal the intimacy of the Egyptian influence, and he concludes that contrary to modern ideas the Pentateuch could have been compiled only at the time of the Exodus, when the writer's mind was saturated with the language and ideas of Ancient Egypt. Moses himself was 'the Child of the Nile' and the medical information in the Old Testament is essentially identical with that of the Ebers Papyrus. A great deal of the discussion which has taken place with regard to ancient medicine and in particular with what is recorded in Egyptian papyri has been concerned with the contrast between Rational Medicine and Magic; and no little obscurity has been introduced into the interpretation of the ancient practice of medicine as a result of these discussions. Hence it is worth
results.translation: Although the merits of many of the substances used in the most ancient pharmacopoeias [dispensatories] are entirely illusory, the Egyptians nonetheless discovered a considerable number of materials whose virtues are recognized today as genuinely effective. For example, many centuries before the Ebers Papyrus was written, the Egyptians had discovered and were using Castor Oil; they had recognized certain antiseptic properties in Onions; and their confidence in the virtues of a great many other substances—vegetable, animal, and mineral—has been shown to have substantial justification. Another source of information has recently been opened up and promises to reinvigorate a branch of archaeology that had been in danger of becoming unproductive. It greatly increases the interest and importance of documents such as the Ebers Papyrus and also provides a new and productive method for interpreting them. In his treatise 'Die Sprache des Pentateuch in Ihren Beziehungen zum Ägyptischen' [The Language of the Pentateuch in its Relations to Egyptian], Professor A. S. Yahuda has recently emphasized the degree to which the earlier portions of the Old Testament—and the medical knowledge it contains—were inspired by Egypt. Both in language and subject matter, the Five Books of Moses reveal the depth of Egyptian influence, and he concludes that, contrary to modern opinion, the Pentateuch could only have been compiled at the time of the Exodus, when the author's mind was thoroughly saturated with the language and ideas of Ancient Egypt. Moses himself was 'the Child of the Nile,' and the medical information in the Old Testament is essentially identical with that of the Ebers Papyrus. Much of the discussion concerning ancient medicine—and in particular what is recorded in Egyptian papyri—has centered on the contrast between Rational Medicine and Magic; and considerable obscurity has been introduced into the interpretation of ancient medical practice as a result of these discussions. It is therefore worth [continued]

entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a purgative and topical remedy in ancient Egyptian medicine; well attested in the Ebers Papyrus.

entities_mentioned.name: Onions
entities_mentioned.notes: Recognized by Egyptians for antiseptic properties; modern research confirms antimicrobial compounds such as allicin in Allium species.

entities_mentioned.name: Ebers Papyrus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ancient Egyptian medical papyrus, c. 1550 BCE
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the oldest and most extensive known medical documents from ancient Egypt; now housed at the University of Leipzig.

results.translator_notes: The segment is part of an introduction to a translated or annotated edition of the Ebers Papyrus. The OCR rendered 'pharmacopeeias' (corrected to 'pharmacopoeias'), 'Afgyptischen' (corrected to 'Ägyptischen'), 'Yahouda' (corrected to 'Yahuda', referring to Abraham Shalom Yahuda, 1877–1951), and an extraneous 'B XVill' (a page/header artifact, silently removed from cleaned text). The sentence ends mid-thought, indicating the chunk is cut off. The reference to the work 'Die Sprache des Pentateuch in Ihren Beziehungen zum Ägyptischen' is retained in its original German with a bracketed English translation provided.

results.title_inferred: Introduction: Rational Origins of Magical Therapeutics in the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text: subtle mysterious disturbances, concerning the cause of many of which, even to-day, we are ignorant—just as we are ignorant of the reasons underlying the manner in which the disturbance of health manifests itself in certain definite symptoms. In early times, however, such constitutional diseases, of which no obvious cause could be found, were regarded as an interference with the life process—some condition which imperilled the existence of life in the body and for which the rational treatment was the addition to the vital substance of something which would increase its quantity and so avert the peril to existence. The point that I specially want to emphasize is the fact that there was originally no conflict between the rational and the magical—that the things we call magical to-day were originally inspired by rational considerations, for which the people who originally invented these devices believed they had valid reasons for putting faith in them. Just as the early people believed that as blood was the substance of life and that the virtue in blood depended upon its redness, so that the red colour and any red substance could protect the body from the risk of death, so a vast number of other materials, ideas, and even words, acquired the merit of being life-giving, and therefore were rational means of treating any condition which seemed to imperil life, in other words, to remove the vital elements from the body. But it may be protested that many of the therapeutic measures adopted in the Ebers Papyrus are not simply drugs which had acquired a reputation for which there was no adequate justification, but were in the nature of spells and incantations. Nevertheless, these different rites which Dr. Alan Gardiner has distinguished as 'manual' and 'oral'—the doing something with the hands or saying something with the mouth to cure disease—were clearly inspired in the beginning by rational considerations. In the beginning there was no distinction
results.translation: Subtle, mysterious disturbances—the cause of many of which remains unknown even today—are paralleled by our ignorance of the reasons underlying the way in which disturbances of health manifest in certain definite symptoms. In early times, however, such constitutional diseases, for which no obvious cause could be found, were regarded as an interference with the life process: some condition that imperiled the existence of life within the body, and for which the rational treatment was the addition to the vital substance of something that would increase its quantity and thereby avert the peril to existence. The point I wish especially to emphasize is that there was originally no conflict between the rational and the magical—that the things we call magical today were originally inspired by rational considerations, for which the people who first invented these devices believed they had valid reasons to place faith in them. Just as early peoples believed that blood was the substance of life and that the virtue of blood depended upon its redness—so that red color and any red substance could protect the body from the risk of death—so too did a vast number of other materials, ideas, and even words acquire the merit of being life-giving, and therefore became rational means of treating any condition that seemed to imperil life, that is, to remove the vital elements from the body. It may be objected, however, that many of the therapeutic measures found in the Ebers Papyrus are not simply drugs that had acquired a reputation without adequate justification, but are rather in the nature of spells and incantations. Nevertheless, these different rites which Dr. Alan Gardiner has distinguished as 'manual' and 'oral'—the doing of something with the hands, or the saying of something with the mouth, to cure disease—were clearly inspired at the outset by rational considerations. In the beginning there was no distinction

results.title_inferred: Introduction: On the Rational and Magical Origins of Ancient Egyptian Medical Practices
results.original_text: it is; but this is not incompatible with the fact that originally this practice was devised on purely rational grounds, and it has become magical only because we have learned that the reasons which dictated the original use of mice as drugs are invalid. With regard to the use of spells and incantations, in the early development of religion the oral rites came to acquire a great reputation for power; the recitation of certain statements at the opening of the mouth of a mummy were supposed to confer life upon it. The statements of certain kings and deities were supposed to be able to confer life and to protect the lives of their subjects. It was part of a firm belief in the superhuman power of the king to do things simply by expressing a wish that they should be done. The XXII INTRODUCTION principle is expressed very wittily by Gilbert in the opera, The Mikado, when the Lord High Executioner explains to the Mikado that when he ordered a certain person to be executed the execution was as good as done, and if it was as good as done it was done, and if it was done why not say so? This verbal juggling really illustrates the sort of reasoning which occurred among primitive people, and enables us the better to understand what was involved in spells and incantations as potent influences in the treatment of disease. People who had not learned that death itself was the inevitable fate of every human being, but that when it did happen it was the result of some injury to the body inflicted by some other living creature, naturally assumed that interference with the normal health of a person which was not due to any observable cause, might be produced by some creature having obtained possession of the body and requiring to be driven out in order to free the body from the element which was disturbing it. Once people got the idea that these living beings, whether we call them spirits or not matters little, occupied the body and caused these disturbances, it was perhaps not irr[ational to suppose...]
results.translation: This is already in English. The passage is an introductory scholarly commentary discussing the rational versus magical origins of ancient Egyptian medical practices, including: (1) the use of mice as medicinal drugs, originally conceived on rational grounds but later regarded as magical once those grounds were discredited; (2) the role of oral rites, spells, and incantations in early religious and medical contexts, including the 'Opening of the Mouth' ceremony performed on mummies; (3) the belief in the superhuman authority of kings and deities to confer life or protection through spoken decree; (4) the author's use of Gilbert and Sullivan's opera The Mikado as a witty analogy for the kind of verbal-magical reasoning found in ancient cultures; and (5) a proto-demonological explanation of disease, in which illness without observable cause was attributed to a spirit or living entity possessing the body, which then needed to be expelled to restore health.

results.title_inferred: Introduction: Primitive Medicine, Magic, and the Ebers Papyrus — Theory of Disease and Paleopathological Evidence
results.original_text: the current theory of the etiology of the disease. Once this idea is realized that what we call magic is simply the persistence of fallacies which were rational INTRODUCTION XXIII applications of a general theory, we will recognize that the principles underlying such primitive medicine, as recorded in the Ebers Papyrus, are not essentially different from those which inspire the treatment of disease in modern times. In both ages there were general theories of the nature of disease; the treatment was inspired by a rational application of the knowledge in the light of this general theory. In medicine at the present time there are no doubt all sorts of fallacies both in interpreting the etiology of disease and in diagnosing it and in attempting to treat it rationally—attempts which fifty years hence may in many cases be regarded in the same way as we regard the magical practices of the past. If the growth of knowledge eliminates error, at the same time it creates magic, magic which the next generation will learn to discover and to exorcise. Hence to understand the vast accumulation of nonsensical methods of diagnosis and treatment such as those of the Ebers Papyrus, we must try to put ourselves in the position of those men thirty centuries ago who lacked the knowledge and experience our ancestors have gained in the intervening centuries, and try to sympathize with their attempts to penetrate into the elusive mysteries of constitutional disease and discover a rational means of treating it. Leaving aside such attempts to explain Egyptian ideas, what at the moment it is more useful to do as a help towards identifying the diseases which are discussed in the Ebers Papyrus is to call attention to the actual evidence provided by the dead bodies of the XXIV INTRODUCTION ancient Egyptians of the ailments that afflicted them in the times when these ancient documents were written; and perhaps I can best do this in the form of a personal narrative by giving a brief sketch of
results.translation: The current theory of the etiology of disease. Once it is appreciated that what we call magic is simply the persistence of fallacies that were rational applications of a general theory [page marker: INTRODUCTION XXIII], we will recognise that the principles underlying such primitive medicine, as recorded in the Ebers Papyrus, are not essentially different from those which inspire the treatment of disease in modern times. In both eras there were general theories regarding the nature of disease; treatment was inspired by a rational application of knowledge in the light of that general theory. In medicine at the present day there are doubtless all manner of fallacies, both in interpreting the etiology of disease and in diagnosing it and in attempting to treat it rationally—attempts which, fifty years hence, may in many cases be regarded in the same light as we now regard the magical practices of the past. If the growth of knowledge eliminates error, it simultaneously creates [new] magic—magic which the next generation will learn to identify and to dispel. In order, therefore, to understand the vast accumulation of seemingly nonsensical methods of diagnosis and treatment found in works such as the Ebers Papyrus, we must endeavour to place ourselves in the position of those men thirty centuries ago who lacked the knowledge and experience that our ancestors have accumulated in the intervening centuries, and to sympathise with their efforts to penetrate the elusive mysteries of constitutional disease and to discover a rational means of treating it. Setting aside such attempts to elucidate Egyptian ideas, what is more immediately useful as an aid to identifying the diseases discussed in the Ebers Papyrus is to draw attention to the direct evidence furnished by the bodies of [page marker: XXIV INTRODUCTION] ancient Egyptians regarding the ailments that afflicted them at the time these ancient documents were composed; and perhaps I can best do this in the form of a personal narrative, by giving a brief account of

results.title_inferred: Paleopathological Findings from Ancient Egypt and Nubia: Fractures, Executions, Tuberculosis, Malignancy, and Other Diseases

Altogether a great variety of injuries was found, which, however, I shall not attempt to enumerate. XXVI INTRODUCTION Two of them are of particular interest. At Shellal (First Cataract), a trench outside a Roman Fort was found to contain 104 bodies of Nubians, all of whom had been executed. Most of them had been hanged, and it is interesting to note that the noose of the rope did not catch the neck as it usually does in modern hanging but the head, the bones of which were forced apart by the shock. One of these Nubians had had his head cut off with a sword. Evidence of tubercular disease was extremely rare. Only about a dozen typical examples of tubercular caries were found in Egypt and Nubia, of which the most ancient (circa 2000 B.C.), and the earliest stage in the development of the disease, is shown in Plate vii. Malignant disease was also very rare. Several cases of osteosarcoma (Plate viii) were found, three of them in the cemetery of the Fifth Dynasty at the Giza Pyramids. Evidence of only two other types of malignant disease was found—cancer of the pharynx and cancer of the rectum, both of them more than twenty centuries later than the Pyramid Age. The cancer of the throat, the former of the cases just mentioned, destroyed a large part of the base of the skull (Plate ix). Calcareous disease of the arteries was common in all ages. The head of Rameses the Great (Plate x) shows calcified temporal arteries and also a mysterious sign painted upon the top of the scalp. Only one case of leprosy was discovered (Plate xi) and that in a cemetery of Syrian Christians at the First Cataract. One instance of gall-stones was found (Plate xii) in INTRODUCTION XXVII the mummy of a Priestess of Amon (Plate xiii), who died at Thebes about 1000 B.C. results.translation:
A thousand years later, an example of the splints used for treating fractures of the forearm (Plate VI). Altogether a great variety of injuries was found, which, however, I shall not attempt to enumerate.

INTRODUCTION (XXVI)

Two of them are of particular interest. At Shellal (First Cataract), a trench outside a Roman fort was found to contain 104 bodies of Nubians, all of whom had been executed. Most of them had been hanged, and it is interesting to note that the noose of the rope did not catch the neck as it usually does in modern hanging, but rather the head, the bones of which were forced apart by the shock. One of these Nubians had had his head severed with a sword.

Evidence of tubercular disease was extremely rare. Only about a dozen typical examples of tubercular caries [spinal or bony tuberculosis] were found in Egypt and Nubia, of which the most ancient (circa 2000 B.C.), representing the earliest stage in the development of the disease, is shown in Plate VII.

Malignant disease was also very rare. Several cases of osteosarcoma [malignant bone tumour] (Plate VIII) were found, three of them in the cemetery of the Fifth Dynasty at the Giza Pyramids. Evidence of only two other types of malignant disease was found—cancer of the pharynx and cancer of the rectum—both of them dating to more than twenty centuries after the Pyramid Age. The cancer of the throat, the first of the two cases just mentioned, destroyed a large part of the base of the skull (Plate IX).

Calcareous [calcified/arteriosclerotic] disease of the arteries was common in all ages. The head of Ramesses the Great (Plate X) shows calcified temporal arteries and also a mysterious sign painted upon the top of the scalp.

Only one case of leprosy was discovered (Plate XI), and that in a cemetery of Syrian Christians at the First Cataract. One instance of gallstones was found (Plate XII) in INTRODUCTION (XXVII) the mummy of a Priestess of Amon (Plate XIII), who died at Thebes about 1000 B.C.

When I first visited Dr. Reisner at Naga-ed-Der, he asked my advice on a curious controversy that was agitating the m[text breaks off — see next chunk]

results.title_inferred: Dental Disease in Ancient Egypt and the Royal Mummies Controversy
results.original_text: learned luxury. In the cemetery of the time of the ancient Empire excavated by the Hearst Expedition at the Giza Pyramids, more than five hundred skeletons of aristocrats of the time of the pyramid-builders (circa 2800 BC.) were brought to light, and in these bodies it was found that tartar-formation, dental caries, and alveolar abscesses were at least as common as they are in modern Europe to-day. At every subsequent period of Egyptian history one finds the same thing—the wide prevalence of every form of dental disease among the wealthy people who indulge in a luxurious diet, and the relative immunity from such ailments among the poorer people who lived mainly on a coarse uncooked vegetable diet. There is in no case the slightest suggestion that any operative measures were adopted in order to cope with dental trouble, and in spite of frequent statements to the contrary, tooth-stopping was never practised in ancient Egypt. Even the mummy of Amenophis III, often called The Magnificent, who reigned at a time when Egyptian power and luxury had attained their fullest expression, revealed no trace of any attempt to deal with the extreme condition of caries and alveolar inflammation found in his jaws. The most remarkable controversy that developed out of the study of the Royal mummies is of special interest and even now, nearly twenty years after the event, is not yet settled. It was concerned with the heretic king, Akhenaton. The late Mr. Theodore Davis, who financed the excavations in the Valley of the Tombs of the Kings near Thebes from 1903 to 1912, often stated that his chief ambition was to discover the tomb of the famous Queen Tiy, the wife of the Pharaoh Amenophis III of the Eighteenth Dynasty. In 1907 the announcement was made (by Professor Sayce) in a letter to The Times that Mr. Davis had found the tomb of the famous Queen. I knew nothing more about this matter until about three months later, when, in July 1907, the Inspector [text continues]

results.translation: After he was twenty. If this is the true history, there is no reason why he might not, between the ages of fifteen and twenty, have been the father of these children whom he obviously treated as his own offspring. I am not competent to express an authoritative opinion on the symptoms and results of this dystrophy. Some years ago, however, before venturing to suggest it as the only possible explanation I could discover to eliminate the apparent conflict between the anatomical and the historical evidence, I consulted the full report on the history and manifestations of dystrophia adiposo-genitalis [adiposo-genital dystrophy], written by Dr. Harvey G. Beck in Professor Llewellyn F. Barker's Endocrinology and Metabolism (Volume I, page 859, New York, 1922). It seems to me that the actual cases reported by Dr. Beck afford a possible explanation of all we know of Akhenaton's symptoms, and also an answer to the objections that have been raised against the suggested diagnosis of adiposo-genital dystrophy as the solution of the enigma his case presents. Sterility is not an invariable result of this dystrophy; in certain sufferers, either the excessive development of fat or the genital disability may be present without the other. [Page XXXIV — INTRODUCTION] In these notes I have attempted to summarize what little we know of the incidence of disease in ancient Egypt — not merely such complaints as arthritis and mastoid disease, which are mentioned in the Ebers Papyrus, but also other ailments that have not been recognized in the writings. It is now coming to be recognized that studies in Egyptian philology alone are not likely to resolve the difficulties of interpretation of the medical papyri. The survival in Egypt and elsewhere of ancient archaic terms and ancient remedies is pointing the way toward an understanding of much that would otherwise be obscure in the Egyptian texts. As I have already stated, the comparative study of folk-lore and herbals, and the investigation of medieval leechdom [medical practice] and pharmacopoeias, is proving [to be valuable in this regard — text ends mid-sentence].

results.title_inferred: Introduction to the Ebers Papyrus: Context, Scholarship, and the Edwin Smith Surgical Papyrus
results.original_text:
solve problems of exceptional interest that appeal to the deepest desires of every human being to safeguard health and life itself. Since the foregoing paragraphs have been printed Professor James Henry Breasted's sumptuous monograph on the Edwin Smith Surgical Papyrus—the INTRODUCTION XXXV result of ten years' intensive investigation—has been published by the University of Chicago Press. Hence it is no longer correct to say that no Ancient Egyptian treatise on Medicine has been translated into English. Moreover Professor Breasted's work is the most ambitious attempt that has ever been made to interpret an ancient medical treatise, for he has summoned to his aid all the resources of Egyptian philology and used his superb scholarship and insight to discover the subtle meaning underlying the cryptic phraseology. He has provided all the material on the Egyptian side so that the way is now ready to institute comparisons with Hebrew and Greek literature and, using the technique of Professor Yahouda's method, bring illumination to the writings of these three ancient peoples, to whom in different ways our own civilization is so deeply indebted. The issue of Professor Breasted's treatise does not minimize the need for such works of popularization as Dr. Bryan's. On the contrary the increased interest the former will provoke should extend the demand for such guide-books as the latter. G. Elliot Smith. University College, London. August, 1930

AUTHOR'S FOREWORD

Little more than fifty years ago there came into the possession of a German Egyptologist a roll of papyrus that was destined to prove the oldest book the world possesses. It could not have fallen into worthier hands, for within three years there issued in two beautiful volumes not only a facsimile of the whole papyrus but, as well, a German translation and a Hieroglypho-Latin glossary: the whole work constituting a monument to German scholarship that commands the admiration of all time.
results.translation:
...to solve problems of exceptional interest that appeal to the deepest desires of every human being to safeguard health and life itself. Since the foregoing paragraphs were printed, Professor James Henry Breasted's sumptuous monograph on the Edwin Smith Surgical Papyrus—the result of ten years' intensive investigation (Introduction, p. xxxv)—has been published by the University of Chicago Press. It is therefore no longer accurate to say that no ancient Egyptian medical treatise has been translated into English. Furthermore, Professor Breasted's work is the most ambitious attempt ever made to interpret an ancient medical treatise: he brought to bear all the resources of Egyptian philology, and employed his superb scholarship and insight to uncover the subtle meanings underlying the cryptic phrasing. He has supplied all necessary material on the Egyptian side, so that the way is now clear for comparisons with Hebrew and Greek literature, and—using the analytical technique of Professor Yahouda's method—for casting light upon the writings of these three ancient peoples, to whom our own civilization owes so deep and varied a debt. The publication of Professor Breasted's treatise does not diminish the need for works of popularization such as Dr. Bryan's. On the contrary, the heightened interest that the former will provoke should only increase demand for guide-books such as the latter.

G. Elliot Smith
University College, London
August, 1930

AUTHOR'S FOREWORD

Little more than fifty years ago, a roll of papyrus came into the possession of a German Egyptologist—a roll destined to prove the oldest book the world possesses. It could not have fallen into worthier hands, for within three years there appeared, in two beautiful volumes, not only a facsimile of the entire papyrus, but also a German translation and a Hieroglyphic-Latin glossary: the whole constituting a monument to German scholarship that commands admiration for all time.

results.title_inferred: Author's Foreword: On the Challenges of Translating the Papyrus Ebers into English
results.original_text: has come to us from out the very womb of time. It is not that the Papyrus is inaccessible. As fresh and as perfect as when it was penned some four thousand years ago it reposes to-day in the library of the University of Leipzig, while every library of note throughout the civilized world boasts at least one of those beautiful facsimile editions which we owe to the discoverer's unquenchable energy. And it is not that its value has decreased with the years, for it still remains the most ancient complete book in the world. Can it be that the task is too great? * * * To be sure the preparation of a complete English edition will be no easy task. One of our greatest Egyptologists, F. L. Griffith, writing in 1893, has put on record his opinion of the sort of man who, only, could cope successfully with it. In the first place he must be a Physician: for a knowledge of Anatomy, Physiology, and Nosology, is a sine qua non. Then he must be familiar with the Egyptian language and in sympathy with the ideas and methods of these ancient peoples; he must have a knowledge of the substances likely to be available and used as medicinal compounds; he must be conversant not only with Savage and Primitive Medicine but with Classical and Medieval Practice as well, since they were so interwoven. Lastly, he must bring to bear on the work a knowledge of modern Egyptian diseases and their native treatment. Then, having so admirably arrayed the qualifications needed for the task, Griffith bluntly proceeded to declare that no one person possessed them all. * * * But even were that true—and it probably was, and is, true—that constitutes no warrant for shirking the responsibility. Though there be no one person to combine in himself all the qualifications that would go to a perfect interpretation into English of the letter and the spirit of the Papyrus Ebers, it is not very difficult to imagine that the scientific world could pr[ovide a solution].
results.translation: This knowledge has come to us from out the very womb of time. It is not that the Papyrus is inaccessible. As fresh and as perfect as when it was written some four thousand years ago, it reposes today in the library of the University of Leipzig, while every library of note throughout the civilized world boasts at least one of those beautiful facsimile editions which we owe to the discoverer's inexhaustible energy. Nor has its value decreased with the years, for it still remains the most ancient complete book in the world. Can it be that the task of translating it is too great? To be sure, the preparation of a complete English edition will be no easy task. One of our greatest Egyptologists, F. L. Griffith, writing in 1893, placed on record his opinion of the kind of person who alone could successfully undertake it. In the first place, such a person must be a Physician, for a knowledge of Anatomy, Physiology, and Nosology [the classification of diseases] is a sine qua non [an indispensable requirement]. Then he must be familiar with the Egyptian language and sympathetic to the ideas and methods of these ancient peoples; he must have a knowledge of the substances likely to have been available and used as medicinal compounds; he must be conversant not only with Primitive and Savage Medicine but with Classical and Medieval Practice as well, since these traditions were so interwoven. Lastly, he must bring to bear upon the work a knowledge of modern Egyptian diseases and their native treatment. Having so admirably set out the qualifications needed for the task, Griffith then bluntly declared that no one person possessed them all. But even were that true—and it probably was, and still is, true—that constitutes no warrant for shirking the responsibility. Though there may be no single person who combines in himself all the qualifications that would go toward a perfect rendering into English of the letter and spirit of the Papyrus Ebers, it is not very difficult to imagine that the scientific world could pr[ovide a collective solution].

results.title_inferred: Author's Foreword and Chapter I: Age of the Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text: it is a distinct loss—especially when we observe that same scholar's translations of those fragments of medical papyri in the British Museum, dating back a thousand years earlier than the Papyrus Ebers. But he has not done it, neither he nor those others whose life-long studies and researches should have fitted them for the task. Until they do so, the English-speaking student is offered this little volume, a rendition into English of German and other translations of the Egyptian original. * * * One word more. Most Ancient of Books is the claim made for the Papyrus Ebers; not, be it noted, Most Ancient of Papyri. Not (by more than two thousand years) can it claim the latter distinction. Indeed, scattered about the museums and libraries of Europe there are in point of fact a score or more of papyri, all of them centuries older than the Papyrus Ebers. But these papyri are not books, any more than a leaf or several leaves torn from a modern volume constitute the volume itself. For the most part they are scraps of papyrus, torn and bedraggled and much the worse for wear by their passage through the ages, and only partly legible. The papyrus discovered by Ebers, however, complete to the most minute detail, and legible from first to last, is a book, a real book, and nothing but a book, and, as clearly on this ground as on the score of age, justifies the claim made for it to be the Most Ancient Book in the World. Cyril Bryan. Whilst excavating in the vicinity of Thebes he was one day approached by a wealthy Egyptian, who, so he discovered, had made the journey from Luxor expressly to see him. When however the Egyptian produced nothing more exciting than a practically worthless papyrus and a modern statue of Osiris, Ebers's suspicions were aroused.
results.translation:
It is a distinct loss—especially when we observe that same scholar's translations of those fragments of medical papyri in the British Museum, dating back a thousand years earlier than the Papyrus Ebers. But he has not done it, neither he nor those others whose life-long studies and researches should have fitted them for the task. Until they do so, the English-speaking student is offered this little volume, a rendition into English of German and other translations of the Egyptian original.

One word more. 'Most Ancient of Books' is the claim made for the Papyrus Ebers; not, be it noted, 'Most Ancient of Papyri.' Not (by more than two thousand years) can it claim the latter distinction. Indeed, scattered about the museums and libraries of Europe there are in point of fact a score or more of papyri, all of them centuries older than the Papyrus Ebers. But these papyri are not books, any more than a leaf or several leaves torn from a modern volume constitute the volume itself. For the most part they are scraps of papyrus, torn and bedraggled and much the worse for their passage through the ages, and only partly legible. The papyrus discovered by Ebers, however, complete to the most minute detail, and legible from first to last, is a book—a real book, and nothing but a book—and, as clearly on this ground as on the score of age, justifies the claim made for it to be the Most Ancient Book in the World.

—Cyril Bryan

THE PAPYRUS EBERS
Chapter I: Age of the Papyrus

It was in the winter of 1872 that the German Egyptologist Georg Ebers came into possession of the papyrus which, according to the usual custom, bears his name. Whilst excavating in the vicinity of Thebes, he was one day approached by a wealthy Egyptian who, as he discovered, had made the journey from Luxor expressly to see him. When, however, the Egyptian produced nothing more exciting than a practically worthless papyrus and a modern statue of Osiris, Ebers's suspicions were aroused.

results.title_inferred: Historical Context of the Ebers Papyrus and the Hermetic Books of Ancient Egypt
results.original_text: both of whom have identified the King in whose reign it was transcribed as Amen-Hotep I, a Pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty, who reigned in Egypt exactly two hundred years before the now familiar Tut-Ankh-Amen came to the throne. But Ebers was not content with merely fixing the date: he claimed that his Papyrus was nothing less than one of the 'Hermetic Books' of the Ancient Egyptians, in fact the only one then known. Clement of Alexandria writing in A.D. 200, and again Jamblichus in A.D. 363, recorded that the Egyptian priests possessed forty-two books in which was contained the sum of Human Knowledge. These books were called by the Greeks the 'Hermetic Books,' because their authorship was ascribed to the god Hermes: Hermes being the Greek name for the god who in the Egyptian Pantheon was known as Thoth, God of the Healing Art. But it was not as a god that Thoth had gathered the sum of Human Knowledge into these so-called Hermetic Books; on the contrary it was because he had as an ordinary (or extraordinary!) mortal done this great service that he was acclaimed a god by a grateful people and raised to the Pantheon as the God of the Healing Art. Exactly who he was in his mortal state is not clear. He is identified by various authorities with Athothis, 3,400 B.C., who besides being a mighty king was learned in all the arts and sciences; and with Imhotep, who also excelled in every branch of learning, especially in the mysteries of the healing art. Both these personages were deified because of their excellent qualities, and it is quite within the realm of possibility that both were one and the same person. The point, however, does not concern us here. Of these forty-two books, Clement tells us, thirty-six dealt with Philosophy and General Knowledge, whilst the last six were devoted to the Healing Art under the following heads: Book 37. Anatomy. Book 38. Diseases. Book 39. Surgery. Book 40. Remedies. Book 41. Diseases of the Eye. Book 42. Diseases [of the Women].
results.translation: Both scholars have identified the king in whose reign the papyrus was transcribed as Amen-Hotep I [Amenhotep I], a Pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty, who reigned in Egypt exactly two hundred years before the now well-known Tutankhamun came to the throne. But Ebers was not satisfied with merely establishing the date: he claimed that his Papyrus was nothing less than one of the 'Hermetic Books' of the Ancient Egyptians — in fact the only one known at that time. Clement of Alexandria, writing in A.D. 200, and again Jamblichus in A.D. 363, recorded that the Egyptian priests possessed forty-two books in which was contained the sum of Human Knowledge. These books were called by the Greeks the 'Hermetic Books,' because their authorship was ascribed to the god Hermes — Hermes being the Greek name for the deity known in the Egyptian Pantheon as Thoth, God of the Healing Art. It was not, however, in his divine capacity that Thoth had gathered the sum of Human Knowledge into these so-called Hermetic Books; on the contrary, it was because he had — as an ordinary (or extraordinary!) mortal — rendered this great service that he was acclaimed a god by a grateful people and elevated to the Pantheon as the God of the Healing Art. Exactly who he was in his mortal life is not clear. Various authorities identify him with Athothis (c. 3,400 B.C.), who, besides being a mighty king, was learned in all the arts and sciences; and also with Imhotep, who likewise excelled in every branch of learning, especially in the mysteries of the healing art. Both of these figures were deified on account of their outstanding qualities, and it is entirely within the realm of possibility that both were one and the same person. This point, however, does not concern us here. Of these forty-two books, Clement informs us, thirty-six dealt with Philosophy and General Knowledge, whilst the last six were devoted to the Healing Art under the following headings: Book 37. Anatomy. Book 38. Diseases. Book 39. Surgery. Book 40. Remedies. Book 41. Diseases of the Eye. Book 42. Diseases [of Women — text ends abruptly].

Throughout the manuscript the headings of the different chapters, the names of the diseases, the directions for treatment, and in many cases the weights and dosages of the drugs are written in a vivid red which holds its colour to-day every whit as fast as does the black in the text. An understanding of the Papyrus Ebers, or of any Egyptian papyrus, involves an excursion into the domain of Philology. A most interesting excursion it is. The Papyrus Ebers is of course written in the Hieratic, which is a cursive script evolved in course of time from the Hieroglyphics that excite our curiosity on the ancient Egyptian monuments. These Hieroglyphics were eminently suited for chiselling out of stone, but when it came to writing them down on paper, or rather papyri, it was a vastly different matter. To trace minutely on to papyrus the quaint birds and beasts and other objects which went to make up the Hieroglyphic vocabulary, entailed not only great patience, it must have involved an appalling amount of time. Our imagination can easily and with practical certainty bridge over the gap between Hieroglyphics and Hieratic. At first the fine points of the Hieroglyphics, unconsciously perhaps, were slurred over; and this first step being taken the rest was only a matter of time. But this evolution did not stop at the Hieratic stage, which was in fact the script of the Priests, or learned class. results.translation: One might think that the practice of using Rubrics [headings written in red ink] grew, like many other things, out of the Middle Ages, but a glance at almost any of the ancient Egyptian papyri quickly dispels that idea. Throughout the manuscript, the headings of the different chapters, the names of the diseases, the directions for treatment, and in many cases the weights and dosages of the drugs are written in a vivid red ink which retains its colour today just as well as the black ink used in the body of the text. An understanding of the Papyrus Ebers, or of any Egyptian papyrus, requires an excursion into the domain of Philology — and a most interesting excursion it is. The Papyrus Ebers is of course written in Hieratic, which is a cursive script evolved over time from the Hieroglyphics that arouse our curiosity on ancient Egyptian monuments. These Hieroglyphics were admirably suited for being chiselled out of stone, but when it came to writing them down on paper — or rather on papyrus — it was a vastly different matter. To trace minutely onto papyrus the elaborate birds, beasts, and other objects that made up the Hieroglyphic vocabulary entailed not only great patience but must have required an enormous amount of time. Our imagination can easily and with reasonable certainty bridge the gap between Hieroglyphics and Hieratic. At first the fine details of the Hieroglyphics were, perhaps unconsciously, glossed over; and once that first step was taken, the rest was only a matter of time. The simplification became deliberate, was recognized as inevitable, and finally the Scribes found themselves in possession of a flowing cursive style of writing that we know today as Hieratic. But this evolution did not stop at the Hieratic stage, which was in fact the script of the Priests, or learned class. The final stage was reached apparently around the Eighth Century B.C., when we find the common people in possession of a popular script called Demotic, which from its presence [text continues]...

For the most part the Papyrus Ebers is devoted to the medicinal treatment of diseases, surgical interference being recommended in only about a score of cases. results.translation:
The key [to decipherment of Egyptian writing] lay in the fact that Hieroglyphic characters were inscribed upon [the Rosetta Stone] together with their Demotic and Greek equivalents, and it only awaited the arrival of the English physicist and physician Thomas Young, the great French Egyptologist Champollion, and others, to reveal to us an intimate knowledge of that ancient world which until then had meant little more to us than Pyramids, Sphinxes, and Tombs. To return to our Papyrus: since Hieratic script was not inscribed on the Rosetta Stone, it was first necessary to transliterate the Hieratic of the Papyrus into Hieroglyphics. Thence, by means of the Demotic and Greek versions, its meaning was drawn out of the obscurity in which it had lain for thousands of years. On the plates illustrated in this volume, this rendering of Hieratic into Hieroglyphics — depicting men, birds, and beasts — can be plainly traced in the Hieratic script (written, it should be noted, from right to left), and the study will prove an education in itself, well repaying the time devoted to it.

Chapter III: CONTENTS OF THE PAPYRUS

For the most part, the Papyrus Ebers is devoted to the medicinal treatment of diseases, with surgical intervention recommended in only about a score of cases. The pathological conditions described range from the sting of a wasp to the bite of a crocodile, from a trivial ache to the most loathsome disease. The anatomy, physiology, and pathology of the heart and circulatory system are addressed in a dedicated chapter; morbid conditions of the alimentary [digestive] and urinary tracts are examined very thoroughly; obstetrics and gynecology are by no means neglected; and the skin, teeth, ears, and especially the eyes receive due attention. In addition, a considerable amount of space is devoted to cosmetics. Remedies are recorded for driving out wrinkles and making the face smooth, for removing moles and beautifying the body, for altering the colour of the skin and for dyeing the hair and eyebrows, for promoting hair growth, and for correcting 'squint' [strabismus]. Then in all s[...] [TEXT ENDS MID-SENTENCE]

results.translator_notes: The source text is already in English (a scholarly introduction/commentary on the Ebers Papyrus), so the 'translation' here represents a lightly edited and clarified rendering rather than a translation from another language. The segment ends abruptly mid-sentence ('Then in all s'), indicating the chunk boundary cuts off the text. OCR errors corrected: 'ren- dering' (line break artifact) consolidated to 'rendering'; 'well re- , paying' corrected to 'well repaying'; 'theskin' corrected to 'the skin'. The passage is descriptive/introductory and does not itself describe specific preparation methods; it catalogues the categories of medical, surgical, and cosmetic content found in the Papyrus Ebers.

results.title_inferred: Summary of Pathological Conditions in the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text: OF THE PAPYRUS II away fleas, lice, and mice, to soothe a crying baby, to prognosticate the fate of a new-born child, to distinguish spoiled milk, to sweeten the breath—these and numerous others being rounded off by a remedy for sweaty feet! The following summary of the pathological conditions described or dealt with in the Papyrus will afford a better idea as to its scope, an attempt having been made to group them in some sort of order. To Joachim even more than to Ebers are we indebted for the fact that it is possible to prepare such a list. A scholar, an Egyptologist, and a physician, he bent his whole mind to the task of giving to the world in a modern tongue, not only a word-for-word translation of the precious document but of elucidating many passages which without such elucidation would be to us so much gibberish; and it was only after diligent study and research extending over years that he was enabled to diagnose the actual complaints from the descriptions often so cryptically supplied by the Scribe: Headache, Migraine, Giddiness, Constipation, Diarrhoea, Indigestion, Colic, Dysentery, Melena, Piles, Inflammations of the Anus, Tumours and Inflammations of the Abdomen, Tapeworms, Roundworms, Guinea-worm, Hookworm, Polyuria, Frequency of Micturition, Accumulation and Obstruction of Urine, Cystitis, Enlarged Prostate, Stricture, Stone, Cardiac pain and weakness, Palpitation, Disorderly Action of the Heart, Atheroma, Debility, Diseases of the Liver, Glandular swellings, Tumours innocent and malignant, Fat Tumours, Skin Tumours, Tumours of Nerves and Vessels, Baldness, Alopecia, Scurf, Eczema, Impetigo, Scabies, Stings of Wasps and Tarantula, Bite of a Crocodile, Burns of the First, Second, Third, Fourth, and Fifth Day, Wounds, Abscesses, Gangrene, Pustules and Suppurations, Menstrual irregularities, Amenorrhoea, Leucorrhoea, Aids to Delivery, Abortion, and Lactation, Diseases of the Breasts, Falling of the Womb, Ulcers and Diseases of the Female Genitalia.
results.translation: OF THE PAPYRUS [Chapter II]: [remedies to] drive away fleas, lice, and mice; to soothe a crying baby; to prognosticate the fate of a new-born child; to distinguish spoiled milk; to sweeten the breath—these and numerous others being rounded off by a remedy for sweaty feet. The following summary of the pathological conditions described or dealt with in the Papyrus will afford a better idea as to its scope, an attempt having been made to group them in some sort of order. To Joachim even more than to Ebers are we indebted for the fact that it is possible to prepare such a list. A scholar, an Egyptologist, and a physician, he devoted his whole mind to the task of giving to the world in a modern tongue not only a word-for-word translation of the precious document, but also of elucidating many passages which, without such elucidation, would be to us so much gibberish; and it was only after diligent study and research extending over years that he was able to diagnose the actual complaints from the descriptions so often cryptically supplied by the Scribe: Headache; Migraine; Giddiness; Constipation; Diarrhoea; Indigestion; Colic; Dysentery; Melena [black, tarry stool indicative of gastrointestinal bleeding]; Piles [haemorrhoids]; Inflammations of the Anus; Tumours and Inflammations of the Abdomen; Tapeworms; Roundworms; Guinea-worm; Hookworm; Polyuria; Frequency of Micturition [urinary frequency]; Accumulation and Obstruction of Urine; Cystitis; Enlarged Prostate; Stricture [urethral]; Stone [urinary calculus]; Cardiac pain and weakness; Palpitation; Disorderly Action of the Heart [arrhythmia]; Atheroma [arterial or cystic fatty deposit]; Debility; Diseases of the Liver; Glandular swellings; Tumours, innocent and malignant; Fat Tumours [lipomas]; Skin Tumours; Tumours of Nerves and Vessels; Baldness; Alopecia; Scurf [dandruff/seborrhoea]; Eczema; Impetigo; Scabies; Stings of Wasps and Tarantula; Bite of a Crocodile; Burns of the First, Second, Third, Fourth, and Fifth Day [burns classified by days of progression or severity]; Wounds; Abscesses; Gangrene; Pustules and Suppurations; Menstrual irregularities; Amenorrhoea; Leucorrhoea; Aids to Delivery, Abortion, and Lactation; Diseases of the Breasts; Falling of the Womb [uterine prolapse]; Ulcers and Diseases of the Female Genitalia.

entities_mentioned.name: Atheroma

entities_mentioned.name: Falling of the Womb

entities_mentioned.name: Burns of the First, Second, Third, Fourth, and Fifth Day
entities_mentioned.notes: The Ebers Papyrus classifies burns by the number of days since injury rather than by depth; this may correspond loosely to modern burn degree classifications or reflect a sequential treatment protocol.

entities_mentioned.name: Leucorrhoea

entities_mentioned.name: Amenorrhoea

results.translator_notes: This segment is a secondary English-language commentary on the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE), not a direct translation from the original hieratic Egyptian. It summarises the scholarly contributions of Georg Ebers (who published the facsimile edition in 1875) and H. Joachim (whose annotated German translation, 'Papyros Ebers,' 1890, is credited here). OCR errors corrected include: 'Tarantule' → 'Tarantula'; 'Amenorrhcea' → 'Amenorrhoea'; 'Leucorrhcea' → 'Leucorrhoea'; 'Joa- chim' (hyphenated line break) → 'Joachim'. The classification of burns by 'day' (First through Fifth Day) is a notable ancient Egyptian staging system distinct from modern burn-depth classification and warrants further scholarly attention. The list of conditions treated reflects the broad scope of the Ebers Papyrus, encompassing internal medicine, parasitology, urology, cardiology, dermatology, surgery, and gynaecology.

results.title_inferred: Pharmacopoeia of the Papyrus Ebers: Castor Oil and Its Uses
results.original_text: gargles; draughts, confections, and pills; fumigations, suppositories, and enemata. A few of the prescriptions are extremely simple, only one substance being directed to be taken, or applied as a plaster or poultice, or rubbed in. The majority, however, are more complex and run from half a dozen to a dozen or more drugs; while one on Plate 83 consists of thirty-seven ingredients. Many of the preparations, even if we cannot credit them with any therapeutic effect, appear at least to be harmless; other remedies such as the application of Raw Meat to a Black Eye present a more up-to-date touch; but there are over many which can only be described as curious in the highest degree. A glance through the pharmacopoeia on which the Papyrus Ebers is based is not to be undertaken lightly. To be sure we encounter there a number of drugs that figure in our pharmacopoeia to-day, but they are dwarfed by the weird and wonderful substances that the Scribe gravely transcribes. It is here that we first make acquaintance with that God-given drug, Castor Oil, which besides finding itself in a hundred prescriptions has the honour of a special mention in the body of the book: MEMORANDUM ON THE USE OF THE CASTOR OIL TREE (AS FOUND IN THE ANCIENT WRITINGS OF THE WISE MEN) When a Person rubs its Stalk in Water and applies it to a Head which is Diseased, he will immediately become as if he had never been ill. When a Person who suffers from Constipation chews a little of its Berry along with Beer, then the Disease will be driven out of the Sick One's body. Also, a Woman's Hair will increase in growth by using the Berries. She crushes them, makes them into one, puts them in Oil, and anoints her Head therewith. Furthermore, the Oil from its Berries is pressed out as an Ointment for the use of any Person who has the uha-abscess-with-stinking-matter. Lo, the Evil will fly as though he had suffered nothing! For Ten Days he anoints himself afresh daily in the morning.
results.translation:
Gargles; draughts [liquid doses], confections [prepared compound medicines], and pills; fumigations, suppositories, and enemata [enemas]. A few of the prescriptions are extremely simple, directing only one substance to be taken, or applied as a plaster or poultice, or rubbed in. The majority, however, are more complex and range from half a dozen to a dozen or more drugs; while one prescription on Plate 83 consists of thirty-seven ingredients. Many of the preparations, even if we cannot credit them with any therapeutic effect, appear at least to be harmless; other remedies, such as the application of Raw Meat to a Black Eye, present a more modern character; but there are far too many which can only be described as curious in the highest degree. A survey of the pharmacopoeia on which the Papyrus Ebers is based is not to be undertaken lightly. To be sure, we encounter there a number of drugs that figure in our pharmacopoeia today, but they are overshadowed by the strange and remarkable substances that the Scribe gravely transcribes. It is here that we first make acquaintance with that God-given drug, Castor Oil, which, besides appearing in a hundred prescriptions, has the honour of a special mention in the body of the book:

MEMORANDUM ON THE USE OF THE CASTOR OIL TREE (AS FOUND IN THE ANCIENT WRITINGS OF THE WISE MEN)

When a Person rubs its Stalk in Water and applies it to a Head which is Diseased, he will immediately become as if he had never been ill.

When a Person who suffers from Constipation chews a little of its Berry along with Beer, then the Disease will be driven out of the Sick One's body.

Also, a Woman's Hair will increase in growth by using the Berries. She crushes them, combines them into one [preparation], puts them in Oil, and anoints her Head therewith.

Furthermore, the Oil pressed from its Berries is used as an Ointment for any Person who has the uha-abscess-with-stinking-matter [an Egyptian medical term for a suppurating abscess]. Lo, the Evil will flee as though he had suffered nothing! For Ten Days he anoints himself afresh daily in the morning.

entities_mentioned.name: Castor Oil Tree (Berries, Stalk, Oil)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in multiple forms: stalk rubbed in water applied to diseased head; berries chewed with beer for constipation; crushed berries in oil for hair growth; pressed berry oil as ointment for suppurating abscess (uha-abscess-with-stinking-matter). One of the most prominently featured drugs in the Papyrus Ebers, appearing in approximately one hundred prescriptions.

entities_mentioned.name: Raw Meat
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied to a black eye; the author notes this has a 'more up-to-date touch,' suggesting it was still recognizable as a folk remedy at the time of writing.

entities_mentioned.name: Beer
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ancient Egyptian beer (fermented barley/emmer wheat beverage)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle for administering castor berries in the treatment of constipation.

entities_mentioned.name: uha-abscess-with-stinking-matter
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Suppurating or infected abscess (precise clinical identity uncertain)
entities_mentioned.notes: An Egyptian medical term preserved in transliteration; likely refers to a fetid, purulent abscess. Treated with castor oil pressed from berries, applied topically as an ointment for ten consecutive days each morning.

results.translator_notes: This segment is an English-language scholarly commentary on the Papyrus Ebers (not a direct translation from the original Egyptian), interspersed with translated excerpts from the papyrus itself. The 'Memorandum on the Use of the Castor Oil Tree' section represents a translated Egyptian primary source embedded within the commentary. The term 'uha-abscess-with-stinking-matter' is a direct transliteration/translation of an Egyptian medical designation and has been preserved as such. The text is truncated at the end ('in the mor-'), with the full phrase reconstructed as 'in the morning' based on context. The symbol '©' appearing after 'Castor' in the original OCR is a scanning artifact and has been silently removed.

results.title_inferred: Overview of Ancient Egyptian Pharmacopoeia: Ingredients, Administration, and Vehicles
results.original_text: reached as high as the heavens, actually prescribing Excrement-of-the-Gods; and as low as—well, as low as the following pages will reveal. They laid heavy hands on the animal world, on the PHARMACOPOEIA OF ANCIENT EGYPT mineral world, on the plant world, and not least on the World of Fancy; and having got a substance which they imagined possessed the slightest therapeutic virtue they prescribed it internally and externally, raw and cooked, ripe and unripe; in some cases over-ripe and actually rotting. They would take a plant or a tree and strip it of its blossoms and its flowers, its fruit and its nuts, its leaves and its bark, its thorns if it had any, its stem, its root, and the bark of its root, its sap, its resin, and its fat. Even then they were not finished with it; they collected any splinters of it that survived and used them (will it be believed?) as a poultice for their nerves! And when the plant was capable of no further dismemberment they directed that the particular one used should come from the North or from the South as their fancy moved them, from Upper or from Lower Egypt, from the Hills or from the Delta, from the Fields or from the Meadows or from the Marshes. Their ingenuity not yet exhausted, they turned their attention to the administration of the remedies, exhibiting again a versatility in every way comparable to their pharmaceutical efforts. Thus, we read of the mother who was instructed to spit the remedy into the child's mouth, of the woman who was ordered to take her medicine while she sat cross-legged, and of that other lady who, after chanting the Magic Formula, was told to have coitus before swallowing the medicament. A final reference must be made to the vehicles by which it was sought to make the nauseous mixtures and objectionable messes which passed for poultices less nauseous and less objectionable. These vehicles, Beer, Milk, and Water, were employed with well-balanced impartiality; they were oft—
results.translation: The ancient Egyptian pharmacopoeia reached as high as the heavens—literally prescribing 'Excrement-of-the-Gods' [a known ingredient in Egyptian medical texts]—and descended to the lowest substances, as the following pages will reveal. Egyptian physicians drew heavily upon the animal kingdom, the mineral kingdom, the plant kingdom, and not least upon the realm of imagination. Once they believed a substance possessed even the slightest therapeutic virtue, they prescribed it internally and externally, raw and cooked, ripe and unripe, and in some cases overripe and even putrefying. When working with a plant or tree, they would make use of its blossoms, flowers, fruit, nuts, leaves, bark, thorns (where present), stem, root, root-bark, sap, resin, and fat. Even then they were not finished: any surviving splinters were collected and applied—incredible as it may seem—as a poultice for the nerves. When the plant could be dismembered no further, they specified that the specimen used must come from the North or the South as inclination dictated, from Upper or Lower Egypt, from the hills or the Delta, from the fields, the meadows, or the marshes. Their ingenuity still not exhausted, they turned to the manner of administering remedies, displaying a versatility fully comparable to their pharmaceutical inventiveness. We read, for instance, of a mother instructed to spit a remedy into her child's mouth; of a woman directed to take her medicine while seated cross-legged; and of another lady who, after reciting the prescribed Magic Formula, was told to engage in coitus before swallowing the medicament. A final note must be made regarding the vehicles used to render the nauseating mixtures and objectionable preparations that served as poultices somewhat more tolerable. These vehicles—Beer, Milk, and Water—were employed with evenhanded impartiality; they were frequently—[text continues]
