entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly frankincense, bdellium, or a symbolic/ritual ingredient; some scholars identify it with certain resinous or fermented substances
entities_mentioned.notes: A known ingredient category in Egyptian medical papyri; likely a euphemistic or ritual name for a specific substance, possibly of resinous or mineral origin. Exact identification remains debated.

entities_mentioned.name: Beer
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the three principal vehicles (carriers) used in Egyptian pharmaceutical preparations to mask or deliver medicaments.

entities_mentioned.name: Milk
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Animal milk, most commonly from cows or humans in Egyptian texts
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a pharmaceutical vehicle alongside beer and water in Ebers Papyrus prescriptions.

entities_mentioned.name: Water
entities_mentioned.notes: The third standard vehicle for Egyptian pharmaceutical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Magic Formula
entities_mentioned.notes: Ritual incantations were frequently prescribed alongside physical remedies in Egyptian medical texts; the Ebers Papyrus contains numerous such spells.

results.translator_notes: The source text is an English-language scholarly commentary on the Ebers Papyrus (not a direct translation of the hieratic original), so this JSON represents a cleaned and structured rendering of the commentary itself. The word 'zerves' in the OCR source is an obvious OCR error for 'nerves,' corrected silently. The header 'PHARMACOPG:IA' is an OCR corruption of 'PHARMACOPOEIA,' corrected silently. The text is mid-sentence at both the opening and closing, indicating this is a chunk extracted from a larger continuous passage. The author's tone is satirical and critical of ancient Egyptian medical practice.

results.title_inferred: Mineral Remedies and Substance Classifications in the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text:
moment they bethought themselves of Swill-of-Beer. Milk also afforded them some scope; it appears as Fresh Milk, Sour Milk, Spoiled Milk, Cooked Milk, Ass's Milk, Cow's Milk, Man's Milk, Milk-of-a-Woman, Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son, Milk-of-the-Sycamore, and Milk-juice. As for Water, we find Plain Water, Well Water, Spring Water, Salt Water, Mineral Water, Cake Water, Linseed Water, Natron Water, Water-from-the-Bird-Pond, Water-from-the-Rain-of-the-Heavens, and Water-in-which-the-Phallus-has-been-Washed!

Chapter V: MINERAL REMEDIES

To attempt anything more than roughly to subdivide the 'drugs' that go to the making of this pharmacopoeia would be as unnecessary as it would be unprofitable. It is sufficient to place them under three main headings: Mineral, Plant, and Animal. As regards the first-named, with which this chapter deals, and under which heading are grouped various other substances which are even more foreign to the Plant and Animal worlds—few of them are prescribed in their crude or native state. Antimony, Calamine, Granite, Ink, Indigo, and Sulphur almost exhaust the list. Outside of these the source of origin of the drugs is strictly laid down, or various preparations made from the parent drug are prescribed. Thus, the Iron must be Iron-from-Apollonopolis-parva; the Leather must be Leather-from-the-Sandal-maker; the Soot must be Soot-from-the-bet'a-pot; the Soda must be Soda-from-Upper-Egypt. But it were better to set them down in some sort of order, alphabetical preferably.

results.translation:
...at which point they also bethought themselves of 'Swill-of-Beer' [a fermented grain wash]. Milk likewise provided considerable scope: it appears as Fresh Milk, Sour Milk, Spoiled Milk, Cooked Milk, Ass's Milk, Cow's Milk, Man's Milk, Milk-of-a-Woman, Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son, Milk-of-the-Sycamore [i.e., the latex of Ficus sycomorus], and Milk-juice. As for Water, we find Plain Water, Well Water, Spring Water, Salt Water, Mineral Water, Cake Water, Linseed Water, Natron Water, Water-from-the-Bird-Pond, Water-from-the-Rain-of-the-Heavens, and Water-in-which-the-Phallus-has-been-Washed [a magico-ritual preparation].

Chapter V: MINERAL REMEDIES

To attempt anything beyond a rough subdivision of the 'drugs' comprising this pharmacopoeia would be both unnecessary and unprofitable. It is sufficient to arrange them under three main headings: Mineral, Plant, and Animal. Regarding the first category—with which this chapter is concerned, and under which are also grouped various substances even more remote from the Plant and Animal kingdoms—few are prescribed in their crude or native state. Antimony, Calamine, Granite, Ink, Indigo, and Sulphur nearly exhaust the list of substances used in raw form. Beyond these, the precise source of origin of each drug is strictly specified, or particular preparations derived from the base substance are prescribed. Thus, the Iron must be Iron-from-Apollonopolis-parva [modern Qus, Upper Egypt]; the Leather must be Leather-from-the-Sandal-maker; the Soot must be Soot-from-the-bet'a-pot [a specific type of vessel]; the Soda must be Soda-from-Upper-Egypt. It is best, however, to set them down in some order—alphabetical order being preferable:

Alabaster [calcium sulfate or calcite vessel-stone]
Meal-of-Alabaster [ground alabaster powder]
Dust-of-Alabaster [fine alabaster powder]
Antimony [stibium; kohl; Sb]
An-old-Book-cooked-in-Oil [a magico-medicinal preparation]
Bread [various forms used as vehicle or poultice base]
Bread-crumbs
Bread-dough
Bread-meal
Fragrant Bread
Fermenting Bread
Bread-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus [bread made with Ziziphus lotus fruit]
Cake
A-portion-of-Cake
Cake-meal
Cake-water [water in which cake has been steeped]
Calamine [zinc carbonate or zinc silicate]
Clay-from-the-Gate
Clay-from-a-Statue
Clay-from-the-Wall
Mason's Clay
Collyrium [eye salve or eye-paint]
Copper [Cu]

The-Film-of-Damp [efflorescent moisture or mold film on surfaces]

entities_mentioned.name: Swill-of-Beer
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented grain wash or beer lees
entities_mentioned.notes: A by-product of Egyptian beer brewing, used as a medicinal vehicle or ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: Milk-of-the-Sycamore
entities_mentioned.notes: The milky sap of the sycamore fig tree, distinct from animal milk.

entities_mentioned.name: Natron Water
entities_mentioned.notes: Natron (hesmen) was a naturally occurring salt mixture from Egyptian lake deposits, used medicinally and ritually.

entities_mentioned.name: Water-in-which-the-Phallus-has-been-Washed
entities_mentioned.notes: A magico-ritual preparation; likely used in sympathetic or fertility-related treatments.

entities_mentioned.name: Antimony
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stibnite (antimony sulfide, Sb2S3); used as kohl (eye cosmetic/medicine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely used in ancient Egypt as eye-paint (mesdemet) and medicinally.

entities_mentioned.name: Calamine
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in skin preparations; well-attested in ancient pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Iron-from-Apollonopolis-parva
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iron ore or worked iron from Qus (Apollonopolis Parva), Upper Egypt
entities_mentioned.notes: Geographic specification indicates quality control or ritual significance of source.

entities_mentioned.name: Soot-from-the-bet'a-pot
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carbon soot from a specific pottery vessel type
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'bet'a-pot' is an untranslated Egyptian vessel type; soot used medicinally.

entities_mentioned.name: Soda-from-Upper-Egypt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Natron or plant-ash soda from Upper Egyptian deposits
entities_mentioned.notes: Sodium carbonate source; geographic origin specified for quality.

entities_mentioned.name: Alabaster / Meal-of-Alabaster / Dust-of-Alabaster
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Egyptian alabaster (calcite, CaCO3) ground to powder
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in topical preparations; Egyptian 'alabaster' is calcite, not gypsum.

entities_mentioned.name: An-old-Book-cooked-in-Oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Possibly an aged papyrus scroll rendered in oil; likely a magico-medicinal preparation drawing on the written word's power.

entities_mentioned.name: Bread-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bread made with fruit of Ziziphus lotus (jujube/lotus-tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ziziphus lotus is a North African shrub; its fruit was used in food and medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Collyrium
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical term for ophthalmic preparations; frequently prescribed in the Ebers Papyrus.

entities_mentioned.name: Copper / Copper-rust / Copper-shavings / Copper-vitriol / Copper-verdigris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Copper (Cu) and its compounds: copper oxide, copper filings, copper sulfate (CuSO4), basic copper acetate
entities_mentioned.notes: Copper compounds were widely used as antiseptics and in ophthalmic treatments in ancient Egypt.

entities_mentioned.name: The-Film-of-Damp
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Efflorescent mineral salts or mold/fungal film from damp surfaces
entities_mentioned.notes: Ambiguous; may refer to mineral efflorescence on walls or biological growth; used in ways suggesting topical application.

entities_mentioned.name: Linseed Water
entities_mentioned.notes: Demulcent preparation used as a vehicle for other medicaments.

Nunn or similar Egyptologist) describing the pharmacopoeia of the Ebers Papyrus, not a direct translation of the Egyptian source. It is therefore already in English; the task here is to clean, annotate, and extract entities. The text lists substances under a quasi-alphabetical mineral classification. 'Copper-coal' is an unusual compound whose precise Egyptian referent is uncertain—it may denote a charred or calcined copper preparation. 'An-old-Book-cooked-in-Oil' is a striking entry that parallels magico-medical practices where the written word itself was considered medicinally active. The OCR artifact 'pharmacopceia' has been silently corrected to 'pharmacopoeia'. 'CuapTer' is a clear OCR error for 'Chapter', silently corrected. The source text appears to be from a modern (early 20th century) scholarly English paraphrase/translation of the Ebers Papyrus, likely Joachim or Bryan's edition.

results.title_inferred: Plant Remedies and the Identification of 'didi' in the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text: Papyri. The Egyptian word for this is d'd' (or didi) and it was originally translated as the Mandrake (or love-apple) on account of its resemblance to the Hebrew word dudaim in the Old Testament. For many years this was accepted without question until in the Evolution of the Dragon (1919) Professor G. Elliot Smith drew public attention to the error. Gauthier's researches, he pointed out, had led him to the conclusion that didi was nothing else but red clay, while Griffith had gone further and properly identified the substance as red ochre or hematite. In 1927 Dawson in the Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society gave a comprehensive summary of the occurrence of and the use to which didi was put in most of the Egyptian Papyri. He appears to have clinched the matter for good and all, consequently no mention of the Mandrake will be found in these pages, the alraunen of the German translations being interpreted as Hematite. Chapter VI PLANT REMEDIES The foregoing by no means constitute a full list of the Mineral substances employed in the pharmacopoeia, for there are many others whose identity is completely hidden from us under the guise of meaningless words which are beyond the comprehension of the many Egyptologists who have from time to time wrestled with the Papyrus. This is unfortunate to the point of annoyance at times, and it applies equally to those substances which were drawn from the Plant world. However, of the recognizable Plants, and of their preparations, there is a not inconsiderable showing as the following list will reveal. Acanthus-tree, Dough-of-Acanthus, Fruit-of-Acanthus, Nuts-of-Acanthus, Resin-of-Acanthus, Thorns-of-Acanthus, Aloes, Grain-of-Aloes, Resin-of-Aloes, Wood-of-Aloes, Arabian-wood Powder, Balsam, Real Balsam, Barley-plant, Beans, Dried Beans, Caraway, Caraway-seeds, Cedar-tree, Fat-of-the-Cedar-tree, Splinters-of-the-Cedar-tree, Tops-of-the-Cedar-tree, Rotted Cereals, Chopped-up Chaff, Coriander, Coriander-berries, Corn, Red Corn [text ends mid-list: PLANT REMEDIES continues]
results.translation:
The Egyptian word for this substance is d'd' (or didi), and it was originally translated as the Mandrake (or love-apple) owing to its resemblance to the Hebrew word dudaim in the Old Testament. For many years this identification was accepted without question, until in 'The Evolution of the Dragon' (1919) Professor G. Elliot Smith drew public attention to the error. Gauthier's researches, he pointed out, had led to the conclusion that didi was nothing other than red clay, while Griffith went further and correctly identified the substance as red ochre or hematite. In 1927, Dawson, writing in the Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society, provided a comprehensive summary of the occurrence of didi and the uses to which it was put throughout most of the Egyptian papyri. He appears to have settled the matter conclusively; consequently, no mention of the Mandrake will be found in these pages, the term 'alraunen' [German: mandrake-root, lit. 'alraun'] appearing in the German translations being here interpreted as Hematite.

Chapter VI: PLANT REMEDIES

The foregoing account by no means constitutes a complete list of the mineral substances employed in the pharmacopoeia [ancient Egyptian materia medica], for there are many others whose identity is entirely concealed from us behind meaningless words that lie beyond the comprehension of the many Egyptologists who have from time to time wrestled with the Papyrus. This is regrettably frustrating, and applies equally to those substances derived from the plant world. Nevertheless, among the identifiable plants and their preparations, there is a by no means inconsiderable showing, as the following list reveals:

Acanthus-tree; Dough-of-Acanthus; Fruit-of-Acanthus; Nuts-of-Acanthus; Resin-of-Acanthus; Thorns-of-Acanthus; Aloes; Grain-of-Aloes; Resin-of-Aloes; Wood-of-Aloes; Arabian-wood Powder; Balsam; Real Balsam; Barley-plant; Beans; Dried Beans; Caraway; Caraway-seeds; Cedar-tree; Fat-of-the-Cedar-tree; Splinters-of-the-Cedar-tree; Tops-of-the-Cedar-tree; Rotted Cereals; Chopped-up Chaff; Coriander; Coriander-berries; Corn; Red Corn [list continues in subsequent chunk]

entities_mentioned.name: didi (d'd')
entities_mentioned.notes: Previously misidentified as Mandrake (Mandragora officinarum) due to superficial resemblance to Hebrew 'dudaim'. Corrected by Griffith; confirmed by Dawson (1927). The German term 'alraunen' (mandrake) in earlier translations should be read as hematite.

entities_mentioned.name: Mandrake (love-apple)
entities_mentioned.notes: Rejected identification for 'didi'; included here only as the erroneous earlier reading.

entities_mentioned.name: Acanthus-tree (and derived preparations: dough, fruit, nuts, resin, thorns)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acanthus sp., possibly Acanthus syriacus or Acacia nilotica (the latter sometimes confused in Egyptian botanical contexts)
entities_mentioned.notes: Multiple preparations listed: dough, fruit, nuts, resin, thorns. Exact species uncertain; 'Acanthus-tree' in Egyptian papyri may refer to a thorny tree such as Acacia rather than the genus Acanthus sensu stricto.

entities_mentioned.name: Aloes (grain, resin, wood)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera or Aloe succotrina (for medicinal aloe); Aquilaria agallocha (for 'Wood-of-Aloes' / aloeswood)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Wood-of-Aloes' likely refers to aloeswood/agarwood (Aquilaria spp.) rather than the succulent Aloe. 'Grain' and 'Resin' of Aloes more plausibly refer to Aloe vera or similar succulent species.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabian-wood Powder
entities_mentioned.notes: Identity uncertain; possibly a resinous or aromatic wood imported from the Arabian Peninsula.

entities_mentioned.name: Balsam / Real Balsam
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora opobalsamum (Balm of Gilead) or Mecca balsam
entities_mentioned.notes: The qualifier 'Real Balsam' suggests distinction from adulterations or substitutes; likely refers to the prized opobalsamum resin.

entities_mentioned.name: Beans / Dried Beans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vicia faba (broad bean) or Phaseolus sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: In ancient Egyptian contexts most likely Vicia faba (broad bean).

entities_mentioned.name: Cedar-tree (fat, splinters, tops)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cedrus libani (Cedar of Lebanon) or Juniperus sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Multiple preparations: fat (resinous exudate/oil), splinters (wood chips), tops (terminal branches/leaves). 'Cedar' in Egyptian texts sometimes denotes juniper.

entities_mentioned.name: Rotted Cereals
entities_mentioned.notes: Possibly fermented or decomposed grain used as a medicinal ingredient; exact composition unclear.

entities_mentioned.name: Chopped-up Chaff
entities_mentioned.notes: Grain husk material; preparation method (chopped) specified.

entities_mentioned.name: Corn / Red Corn
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum sp. (wheat) or possibly Sorghum bicolor for 'Red Corn'
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Corn' in this period and context likely refers to wheat or emmer rather than maize (a New World plant). 'Red Corn' may indicate a specific variety or a different cereal grain.

'pharmacopceia' is an OCR error for 'pharmacopoeia', corrected silently. 3. 'Cuapter' is an OCR error for 'Chapter', corrected silently. 4. 'Tue' is an OCR error for 'The', corrected silently. 5. The list of plant remedies is incomplete as the source text is cut off mid-list ('PLANT REMED'); the translation notes this. 6. Scholarly references cited: G. Elliot Smith, 'The Evolution of the Dragon' (1919); Gauthier's researches (unspecified date); Griffith (unspecified); Dawson, Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society (1927). 7. No specific ailments or preparation methods are described in this chunk; it is primarily a philological and taxonomic discussion with an opening inventory list.

results.title_inferred: Plant and Organic Remedies in Ancient Egyptian Medicine (Ebers Papyrus)
results.original_text: Sycamore-tree Dough-of-the-Sycamore Resin-of-the-Sycamore Splinters-of-the-Sycamore Milk-of-the-Sycamore Milky-juice-of-the-Sycamore Turpentine Turpentine-nuts Watermelon Wheat Grain-of-Wheat Clean-Grain-of-Wheat Green undried Wheat Wheaten-flour PLANT REMEDIES Willow-tree Berry-of-the-Willow-tree Splinters-of-the-Willow-tree Wonderfruit Seeds-of-the-Wonderfruit Bread-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus Resin-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus Splinters-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus Wood-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus Chapter VII ORGANIC REMEDIES If fancy played so large a part in the extraction of remedies from the plant and mineral world, it was in the domain of Organic therapeutics that were plumbed the depths of human credulity. Gods and Humans, Beasts, Birds, and Fishes, found their way into the pharmacopoeia of Ancient Egypt. A poultice of Excrement-of-the-Gods is prescribed against tremors of the fingers. Human excrement mixed with Yeast-of-Sweet-Beer and Honey is recommended as a dressing for wounds. Male Semen is used as a flavouring agent in a mixture which relieves abdominal obstruction—by emesis, no doubt. Ass's Semen along with Rush-from-the-Green-Willow-tree, Fresh Bread, Herbs-of-the-Field, Figs, Grapes, and Wine cures the Great Debility, as also does another mixture composed of Excrement-of-the-adu-Bird and What-is-in-the-ut'ait-fruit, cooked in Olive Oil and Sweet Beer, and taken for four days. Man's Milk (reinforced it is true by Tortoise-shell, Granite, and Trash-from-the-Knife-Stone) cures the uases-abscess; while as for the nesit-disease— TO DRIVE OUT THE NESIT DISEASE The-Two-Testicles-of-a-Black-Ass Crush, rub in Wine, and let the Patient drink. THE DISEASE DISAPPEARS AT ONCE. But whether prescribed internally or externally many of the preparations confound all criticism. Imagine (if you can) a poultice consisting of the Zizyphus Lotus, Watermelon, Cat's dung, Sweet Beer, and Wine; or a mixture of Rotten Flesh, Herbs-of-the-Field, and Garlic, cooked.
results.translation:
Sycamore-tree; Dough-of-the-Sycamore; Resin-of-the-Sycamore; Splinters-of-the-Sycamore; Milk-of-the-Sycamore; Milky-juice-of-the-Sycamore; Turpentine; Turpentine-nuts; Watermelon; Wheat; Grain-of-Wheat; Clean-Grain-of-Wheat; Green undried Wheat; Wheaten-flour. PLANT REMEDIES: Willow-tree; Berry-of-the-Willow-tree; Splinters-of-the-Willow-tree; Wonderfruit; Seeds-of-the-Wonderfruit; Bread-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus; Resin-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus; Splinters-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus; Wood-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus.

Chapter VII: ORGANIC REMEDIES

If imagination played so large a part in the extraction of remedies from the plant and mineral world, it was in the domain of organic therapeutics that the depths of human credulity were truly plumbed. Gods and Humans, Beasts, Birds, and Fishes all found their way into the pharmacopoeia [dispensatory] of Ancient Egypt. A poultice of 'Excrement-of-the-Gods' [unidentified ritual substance] is prescribed against tremors of the fingers. Human excrement mixed with Yeast-of-Sweet-Beer and Honey is recommended as a dressing for wounds. Male Semen is used as a flavouring agent in a mixture said to relieve abdominal obstruction—presumably by inducing emesis [vomiting]. Ass's Semen, together with Rush-from-the-Green-Willow-tree, Fresh Bread, Herbs-of-the-Field, Figs, Grapes, and Wine, is said to cure 'the Great Debility'; as does another mixture composed of Excrement-of-the-adu-Bird and 'What-is-in-the-ut'ait-fruit,' cooked in Olive Oil and Sweet Beer, and taken over four days. Man's Milk [human breast milk]—reinforced, it is true, by Tortoise-shell, Granite, and Trash-from-the-Knife-Stone—is said to cure the uases-abscess [unidentified abscess type]; while as for the nesit-disease [unidentified ailment]:

TO DRIVE OUT THE NESIT DISEASE:
The Two Testicles of a Black Ass.
Crush, rub in Wine, and let the patient drink. THE DISEASE DISAPPEARS AT ONCE.

But whether prescribed internally or externally, many of these preparations defy all rational criticism. Imagine, if you can, a poultice consisting of Zizyphus Lotus, Watermelon, Cat's dung, Sweet Beer, and Wine; or a mixture of Rotten Flesh, Herbs-of-the-Field, and Garlic, cooked.

entities_mentioned.name: Sycamore-tree (and its derivatives: dough, resin, splinters, milk, milky-juice)
entities_mentioned.notes: Multiple parts of the sycamore fig were used medicinally in ancient Egyptian practice. The latex (milky juice) has documented wound-healing applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Turpentine; Turpentine-nuts
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia terebinthus or Pistacia atlantica resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Turpentine in Egyptian pharmacopoeia is generally associated with terebinth resin imported from the Levant.

entities_mentioned.name: Watermelon
entities_mentioned.notes: Watermelon was cultivated in ancient Egypt and used both as food and medicinally.

entities_mentioned.name: Wheat (Grain-of-Wheat, Clean-Grain, Green undried Wheat, Wheaten-flour)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum sp. (likely Triticum dicoccum, emmer wheat)
entities_mentioned.notes: Emmer wheat was the primary cereal of ancient Egypt and appears extensively in the Ebers Papyrus.

entities_mentioned.name: Willow-tree (Berry, Splinters)
entities_mentioned.notes: Willow bark contains salicin, a precursor to aspirin; its analgesic and anti-inflammatory use has historical validity.

entities_mentioned.name: Wonderfruit; Seeds-of-the-Wonderfruit
entities_mentioned.notes: The identity of 'Wonderfruit' (Egyptian: possibly persea or another unidentified plant) remains debated among Egyptologists.

entities_mentioned.name: Zizyphus-Lotus (Bread, Resin, Splinters, Wood)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus lotus or Ziziphus spina-christi (Christ's thorn jujube)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Ziziphus species was common in North Africa and the Levant; multiple parts used in Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Excrement-of-the-Gods
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely a ritual or symbolic designation for an unidentified substance; possibly a resinous or mineral material given a divine epithet.

entities_mentioned.name: Human excrement
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed as a wound dressing mixed with yeast and honey. Condition treated: wounds.

entities_mentioned.name: Yeast-of-Sweet-Beer
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saccharomyces cerevisiae (brewer's yeast from Egyptian sweet beer)
entities_mentioned.notes: Yeast from Egyptian beer fermentation; has some antimicrobial properties and appears frequently in wound dressings in the papyrus.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-documented antimicrobial wound-dressing agent used widely in ancient Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Male Semen (human)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a flavouring agent in a mixture for abdominal obstruction; emesis [vomiting] was likely the intended mechanism.

entities_mentioned.name: Ass's Semen
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed for 'Great Debility' [general weakness/debility]. Preparation: combined with rush, bread, field herbs, figs, grapes, wine.

entities_mentioned.name: Rush-from-the-Green-Willow-tree
entities_mentioned.notes: Possibly a rush or sedge plant associated with willow-growing environments; exact species unclear.

entities_mentioned.name: Figs
entities_mentioned.notes: Common ingredient in Egyptian medicinal preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Grapes
entities_mentioned.notes: Used fresh or as wine in numerous Egyptian remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Wine (Sweet Beer)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented grape wine / Egyptian barley or wheat beer (djeseret)
entities_mentioned.notes: Both wine and sweet beer are among the most common vehicle/solvent ingredients in the Ebers Papyrus preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Excrement-of-the-adu-Bird
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'adu-bird' is an unidentified Egyptian bird species; its excrement is an organic ingredient in a remedy for Great Debility.

entities_mentioned.name: What-is-in-the-ut'ait-fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Unidentified fruit or plant product; the Egyptian term ut'ait has not been definitively matched to a modern species.

entities_mentioned.name: Olive Oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Common vehicle/excipient in Egyptian medicinal preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Man's Milk (human breast milk)
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed for the 'uases-abscess.' Reinforced with tortoise-shell, granite, and knife-stone waste.

entities_mentioned.name: Tortoise-shell
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Testudo sp. or related chelonian shell
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an adjunct ingredient; possibly ground and added to the breast-milk preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Granite
entities_mentioned.notes: A mineral abrasive or filler in this preparation; no pharmacological rationale identified.

entities_mentioned.name: Trash-from-the-Knife-Stone
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely the waste material from knapping flint knives; used as an ingredient in the uases-abscess remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: Two Testicles of a Black Ass
entities_mentioned.notes: Preparation: crushed and rubbed in wine, given as a drink. Condition treated: 'nesit-disease' (unidentified ailment, possibly urological or general debility).

entities_mentioned.name: Cat's dung
entities_mentioned.notes: Ingredient in a poultice combining Zizyphus Lotus, Watermelon, Sweet Beer, and Wine.

entities_mentioned.name: Rotten Flesh
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a cooked mixture with field herbs and garlic; application/condition not specified in this fragment.

entities_mentioned.name: Garlic
entities_mentioned.notes: Garlic appears frequently in Egyptian medical texts and has documented antimicrobial properties.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from a modern scholarly English-language commentary on the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE), not a direct translation from the Egyptian hieratic original. The source text is already in English and functions as a translated/paraphrased compendium with editorial commentary. The passage covers the tail end of a plant-ingredient index (Chapter VI implied) and the opening of Chapter VII on organic (animal-derived and excremental) remedies. Several Egyptian terms are left untranslated in the source (adu-bird, ut'ait-fruit, uases-abscess, nesit-disease) and remain unresolved in Egyptological literature. The term 'pharmacopeeia' has been standardized to 'pharmacopoeia.' OCR artifact 'Cuapter' corrected to 'Chapter'; 'Splinters-of-the-W illow-tree' corrected to 'Splinters-of-the-Willow-tree'; 'flavour- ing' and similar hyphenated line-break words have been rejoined silently.

results.title_inferred: Animal Organic Materia Medica in the Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text: mixture of Scrapings-of-a-Statue and Mint-of-the-Mountains, cooked in Oil and Wax, to be taken for four days to 'protect against everything.' To compile an absolutely complete list of the animal organic materia medica met with in the Papyrus would not be possible since (as with many of the plant and mineral substances) the identity of a considerable number of the animals mentioned is still beyond us. But even so the list of those animals whose identity is not in doubt, and who were called upon to sacrifice themselves in the cause of the Healing Art, is a formidable one as a glance at the following will show: Lion, Hippopotamus, Crocodile, Ox, Cow, Calf, Mouse, Porcupine, Serpent, Tortoise, Lizard, Mole, Vulture, Bird-of-Passage, Swallow, Pigeon, Goose, Duck, Stag, Gazelle, Ram, Goat, Ass, Hog, Dog, Cat, Frog, Tadpole, Scorpion, Eel, Electric Eel, Crab, Swordfish, Ostrich, Locust, Tarantula, Beetle, Bat, Wasp, Fly, Tapeworm. As to the therapeutic substances which Ancient Egypt extracted from them, the list is, if anything, more startling still. They were forced to yield their Hair, Feathers, Horns, Hoof, Hide, Eyes, Ears, Teeth, Brain, Blood, Bile, Bones, Marrow, Liver, Spleen, Guts, Uterus, Vulva, Phallus, Testicles, Semen, Fat, Tallow, Flesh, Shell, Wings, Excrement, Urine, Eggs, Milk, Toes, Tail. While both these lists are formidable ones it should be said that in many instances the physicians seem, all things considered, to have used a wise discretion in drawing upon them. Thus in the case of the Hog, although they used most of his body from his teeth to his testicles, they appear to have had the same contempt for his brain as is held nowadays, for nowhere is it prescribed. They also betrayed an acquaintance, amateurish perhaps, but still an acquaintance with psychology when they limited their Tortoise preparations to his brain, of which they knew so little, and his shell, of which they knew so much. results.translation: A mixture of Scrapings-of-a-Statue [statue shavings, possibly limestone or painted plaster used as a magical ingredient] and Mint-of-the-Mountains [mountain mint, possibly a wild Mentha or Origanum species], cooked in Oil and Wax, to be taken for four days in order to 'protect against everything.' To compile an absolutely complete list of the animal organic materia medica found in the Papyrus would not be possible, since — as with many of the plant and mineral substances — the identity of a considerable number of the animals mentioned still eludes us. Even so, the list of those animals whose identity is not in doubt, and who were called upon to sacrifice themselves in the cause of the Healing Art, is a formidable one, as the following will show: Lion, Hippopotamus, Crocodile, Ox, Cow, Calf, Mouse, Porcupine, Serpent, Tortoise, Lizard, Mole, Vulture, Bird-of-Passage, Swallow, Pigeon, Goose, Duck, Stag, Gazelle, Ram, Goat, Ass, Hog, Dog, Cat, Frog, Tadpole, Scorpion, Eel, Electric Eel, Crab, Swordfish, Ostrich, Locust, Tarantula, Beetle, Bat, Wasp, Fly, Tapeworm. As for the therapeutic substances which Ancient Egypt extracted from these creatures, that list is, if anything, even more startling. They were made to yield their Hair, Feathers, Horns, Hoof, Hide, Eyes, Ears, Teeth, Brain, Blood, Bile, Bones, Marrow, Liver, Spleen, Guts, Uterus, Vulva, Phallus, Testicles, Semen, Fat, Tallow, Flesh, Shell, Wings, Excrement, Urine, Eggs, Milk, Toes, and Tail. While both lists are formidable, it should be noted that in many instances the physicians appear, on the whole, to have exercised a wise discretion in drawing upon them. In the case of the Hog, for example, although they made use of most of his body from his teeth to his testicles, they seem to have had the same low regard for his brain as is current today, for it is nowhere prescribed. They also displayed a familiarity — amateurish perhaps, but a familiarity nonetheless — with psychology, when they confined their Tortoise preparations to his brain, of which they knew so little, and his shell, of which they knew so much. The Porcupine was an animal which afforded them an [text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: Scrapings-of-a-Statue
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely powdered limestone, painted plaster, or stone dust from cult statues
entities_mentioned.notes: A magical-medicinal ingredient; the precise material depends on the statue's composition. Used as a protective remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: Mint-of-the-Mountains
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Mentha longifolia (horse mint) or Origanum species
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian botanical identification is uncertain; 'mountain mint' may correspond to wild mint or oregano-like herbs known in the ancient Near East.

entities_mentioned.name: Oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely vegetable oil, possibly castor or olive oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly used as a vehicle in ancient Egyptian pharmaceutical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Wax
entities_mentioned.notes: Beeswax was a standard excipient for ointments and topical preparations in ancient Egypt.

entities_mentioned.name: Porcupine
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to North Africa; spines and other parts used medicinally.

entities_mentioned.name: Tortoise
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Testudo species or Nile softshell turtle (Trionyx triunguis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Both shell and brain mentioned as medicinal; shell preparations likely topical or magical.

entities_mentioned.name: Electric Eel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Malapterurus electricus (electric catfish of the Nile); not a true eel
entities_mentioned.notes: The Nile electric catfish is the most plausible identification; true electric eels are South American. The text's 'Electric Eel' likely refers to this catfish.

entities_mentioned.name: Swordfish
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Xiphias gladius or a related large Nile fish species
entities_mentioned.notes: Identification uncertain; may refer to a Nile fish with a blade-like snout.

entities_mentioned.name: Tarantula
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a large spider species native to Egypt/North Africa
entities_mentioned.notes: True tarantulas (Theraphosidae) are not native to Egypt; may refer to a large wolf spider or solifugid.

entities_mentioned.name: Tapeworm
entities_mentioned.notes: Inclusion of a parasite as a therapeutic agent is unusual; may have been used in sympathetic or magical medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Hog

results.title_inferred: Animal and Human Ingredients in the Ebers Papyrus, and the Gods' Role in Egyptian Healing
results.original_text: gratifying to find the Ass, that never-failing friend of mankind throughout the ages, taking pride of place. For the relief of suffering humanity he sacrificed his blood, dung, ears, fat, head, hoof, jawbone, liver, milk, tallow, teeth, testicles, and semen. That equally faithful friend of man, the Cow, came a good second: she gave her bile, brain, blood, fat, flesh, horns, liver, milk, spleen, and tallow. And since it has already been noted that the gods contributed their excrement in the sacred cause, so it should also be noted that the humans contributed not only their excrement but their urine, their milk (both male and female!), and their semen (likewise both male and female!) in the same sacred cause. Last, but not least, the Scribe himself. With supreme audacity he imbued his own excrement and his urine, the very pen he wielded, the ink he used, and the papyrus he wrote upon, with Healing Magic, prescribing them as the spirit moved him against various infirmities and ills from threatened baldness to a distended bladder! Chapter VIII THE GODS AND THE HEALING ART The gods of Egypt contribute to the compilation in a variety of ways. They are invoked collectively and individually to reinforce the curative effects of the remedies; they are set down as the actual authors of some of the remedies; and in one instance, as has been seen in a previous chapter, 'excrement of the gods' is actually named as an ingredient of a compound designed to cure digital tremors. It cannot be said that the Scribe is altogether flattering to the gods in his arrangement of the remedies attributed to them on Plate 46. The first of the remedies there quoted was, he informs us, compounded by the Great Ra himself who, it would appear, intended it as a sort of cure-all for all the ills of his own godly person. But it was a dismal failure as the entries that immediately follow show, for they are a series of remedies especially compounded by various lesser deities.
results.translation:
It is gratifying to find the Ass—that never-failing friend of mankind throughout the ages—occupying pride of place. For the relief of suffering humanity, he sacrificed his blood, dung, ears, fat, head, hoof, jawbone, liver, milk, tallow, teeth, testicles, and semen. That equally faithful friend of man, the Cow, came a close second: she contributed her bile, brain, blood, fat, flesh, horns, liver, milk, spleen, and tallow. And since it has already been noted that the gods contributed their excrement to the sacred cause, it should likewise be noted that human beings contributed not only their excrement but also their urine, their milk (both male and female!), and their semen (likewise both male and female!) to the same sacred cause. Last, but not least, the Scribe himself. With supreme audacity he endowed his own excrement and urine, the very pen he wielded, the ink he used, and the papyrus he wrote upon with Healing Magic, prescribing them as the spirit moved him against various infirmities and ailments ranging from threatened baldness to a distended bladder.

Chapter VIII: THE GODS AND THE HEALING ART

The gods of Egypt contribute to the compilation in a variety of ways. They are invoked both collectively and individually to reinforce the curative effects of the remedies; they are recorded as the actual authors of some of the remedies; and in one instance, as seen in a previous chapter, 'excrement of the gods' is explicitly named as an ingredient of a compound designed to cure digital tremors [finger trembling]. It cannot be said that the Scribe is altogether flattering to the gods in his arrangement of the remedies attributed to them on Plate 46. The first remedy quoted there was, he informs us, compounded by the Great Ra himself, who would appear to have intended it as a kind of universal cure for all the ailments of his own divine person. But it proved a dismal failure, as the entries that immediately follow make clear—for they are a series of remedies specially compounded by various lesser deities [apparently to correct what Ra's remedy could not].

results.translator_notes: This segment is from a modern scholarly commentary on the Ebers Papyrus (likely Bryan's 1930 translation/commentary), not a direct translation of the ancient Egyptian text itself. The source text is in English and required cleaning of OCR artifacts (e.g., 'Cuapter' corrected to 'Chapter', 'Tue' to 'The', 'deiti' completed to 'deities'). The passage is descriptive and analytical rather than a primary pharmaceutical recipe. 'Male milk' is flagged as an ambiguous term; in Egyptological literature it may denote semen or a ritual category. 'Excrement of the gods' is widely noted in scholarship as a probable plant euphemism (e.g., some scholars identify it with certain aromatic resins), but the author here takes it literally within the magical framework of the papyrus.

results.title_inferred: Remedy of Isis for the God Ra: Headache Formula with Coriander, Poppy, Wormwood, Juniper, and Honey
results.original_text: Isis to step into the breach and to set the August One on his feet again, not only curing him of his headache but relieving him as well of 'all sufferings and evils of any sort.' ANOTHER REMEDY WHICH THE GODDESS ISIS PREPARED FOR THE GOD RA TO DRIVE OUT THE PAINS THAT ARE IN HIS HEAD: Berry-of-the-Coriander, Berry-of-the-Poppy-plant, Wormwood, Berry-of-the-sames-plant, Berry-of-the-Juniper-plant, Honey. Make into one, mix with Honey, and smear therewith in order to make him well forthwith. When this remedy is used by him against all illnesses in the head and all sufferings and evils of any sort, he will instantly become well. Among the lesser deities who prescribed for Ra was the goddess Tefnut, and to this female divinity must be accorded the honour of being the first woman-doctor to achieve the notice of history—even though her medical efforts only resulted in giving her divine patient a headache. But if a woman-doctor did give Ra a headache, we have seen that it was also a woman-doctor, Isis, who relieved him of that headache, thereby restoring the balance. Undoubtedly the success that attended the headache-cure compounded by Isis was due to the Opium it contained in the guise of Berry-of-the-Poppy-plant, and it may well be that it was her success with this drug that led that other woman-pioneer of medicine, Helen of Troy, in a later age to produce her nepenthe and to use it with the startling results that Homer records. It is not to be wondered at then that the lengthy Invocation with which the Papyrus opens is addressed to Isis, and both here and elsewhere she is implored to impart healing powers to the remedies. Nowhere, it is well to point out, is the Magic Formula advocated as a cure per se, but it is an essential part of the treatment and without it the remedies might well be useless, and worse than useless. Together with the numerous Magic Formulae scattered throughout the papyrus, the In[vocation...]
results.translation: Isis was called upon to intervene and restore the August One [the god Ra] to health, curing him not only of his headache but also relieving him of 'all sufferings and evils of any sort.' ANOTHER REMEDY WHICH THE GODDESS ISIS PREPARED FOR THE GOD RA TO DRIVE OUT THE PAINS THAT ARE IN HIS HEAD: Berry-of-the-Coriander [coriander fruit], Berry-of-the-Poppy-plant [opium poppy capsule/seed], Wormwood, Berry-of-the-sames-plant [identity uncertain], Berry-of-the-Juniper-plant [juniper berry], Honey. Combine all into one preparation, mix with Honey, and apply as a topical dressing so as to make him well immediately. When this remedy is employed against all illnesses of the head and all sufferings and evils of any sort, the patient will instantly become well. Among the lesser deities who prescribed for Ra was the goddess Tefnut, and to this female divinity must be accorded the honour of being the first woman-physician to achieve notice in recorded history—even though her medical efforts resulted only in giving her divine patient a headache. But if a woman-physician did give Ra a headache, we have seen that it was likewise a woman-physician, Isis, who relieved him of that headache, thereby restoring the balance. Undoubtedly the success attending the headache cure compounded by Isis was due to the Opium it contained in the guise of Berry-of-the-Poppy-plant; and it may well be that it was her success with this drug that led that other woman pioneer of medicine, Helen of Troy, in a later age to produce her nepenthe and to employ it with the startling results that Homer records. It is therefore not surprising that the lengthy Invocation with which the Papyrus opens is addressed to Isis, and both here and elsewhere she is implored to impart healing powers to the remedies. It is worth emphasizing that nowhere is the Magic Formula advocated as a cure in itself; rather, it is an essential component of the treatment, without which the remedies might well be useless—or worse than useless. Together with the numerous Magic Formulae scattered throughout the papyrus, the In[vocation forms an integral part of the therapeutic system...]

results.translator_notes: This segment is an English-language scholarly commentary and partial translation of the Ebers Papyrus (ca. 1550 BCE), not itself an ancient-language primary source. The 'original text' is therefore an edited Victorian/early 20th-century English rendering with embedded Egyptological terminology. The 'sames-plant' ingredient is problematic and may reflect a transliteration of an Egyptian plant name not securely identified by modern scholarship. OCR artifacts (e.g., 'Ss = + S me ee' in the source) have been silently removed as clearly corrupted line-filler. The preparation method is topical ('smear therewith'), not oral, which is notable given the opium content. The author's attribution of opium as the active agent is an interpretive claim, not a statement from the papyrus itself. The text is truncated at the end ('the In[vocation...]'); the full passage continues beyond this chunk.

results.title_inferred: Opening Invocation of the Ebers Papyrus: Preparation of Medicines and Divine Healing Rites
results.original_text: ravages he had caused during his temporary occupancy of the body. In the accompanying rendering of the opening Invocation the Rubrics of the Papyrus are represented by block letters, and it may be added the same applies wherever in this volume the actual text is quoted: THE BEGINNING OF THE BOOK ON THE PREPARATION OF MEDICINES FOR ALL PARTS OF THE BODY OF A PERSON. I came forth from Heliopolis with the priests of het-aat, the Lords of Defence, the Kings of Eternity and of Protection. I came forth from Sais with the Maternal Goddesses who grant me protection. Words were given me by the Lord of the Universe wherewith to drive away the inflictions of all the gods, and deadly diseases of every sort. SO MANY CHAPTERS ARE on this my head, this my neck, these my arms, this my flesh, and these my limbs, to punish the scoffings of the High Ones who cause this disease to enter my flesh by working magic in these my limbs, so that as often as the disease penetrates into this my flesh, this my head, these my arms, into my body, and into these my limbs, Ra has compassion saying: I will protect him from his enemies. His leader, Hermes, it is who has given him the words, who provides the books, and who bestows upon the learned ones and on the physicians who follow him the glory of unravelling that which is obscure. Whom God loves, he quickens. I am one whom God loves, therefore he quickens me. WORDS TO BE SPOKEN IN THE PREPARING OF MEDICINES FOR ALL PARTS OF THE BODY OF A PERSON WHO IS ILL. As it is to be, a thousand times. This is the book for the healing of all diseases. May Isis heal me even as she healed Horus of all the pains which his brother Set had inflicted on him when he killed his brother Osiris! Oh Isis, thou great enchantress, heal me, deliver me from all evil, bad, typhonic things, from demoniacal and deadly diseases and pollutions of all sorts that rush upon me, as thou didst deliver and release thy son Horus!
results.translation:
THE BEGINNING OF THE BOOK ON THE PREPARATION OF MEDICINES FOR ALL PARTS OF THE BODY OF A PERSON.

I came forth from Heliopolis with the priests of het-aat [the temple 'het-aat'], the Lords of Defence, the Kings of Eternity and of Protection. I came forth from Sais with the Maternal Goddesses who grant me protection. Words were given to me by the Lord of the Universe [i.e., the supreme deity] wherewith to drive away the afflictions sent by all the gods and deadly diseases of every kind.

SO MANY CHAPTERS ARE upon this my head, this my neck, these my arms, this my flesh, and these my limbs — to punish the mockeries of the High Ones [i.e., powerful divine beings] who cause this disease to enter my flesh by working magic upon these my limbs — so that as often as the disease penetrates into this my flesh, this my head, these my arms, my body, and these my limbs, Ra takes compassion and says: I will protect him from his enemies. His guide, Hermes [Thoth, here equated with Hermes Trismegistus], it is who has given him the words, who furnishes the books, and who bestows upon the learned and upon the physicians who follow him the glory of unravelling that which is obscure. Whom God loves, He quickens. I am one whom God loves; therefore He quickens me.

WORDS TO BE SPOKEN IN THE PREPARING OF MEDICINES FOR ALL PARTS OF THE BODY OF A PERSON WHO IS ILL.

As it is to be — a thousand times. This is the book for the healing of all diseases.

May Isis heal me even as she healed Horus of all the pains which his brother Set had inflicted upon him when Set killed his brother Osiris! Oh Isis, thou great enchantress, heal me, deliver me from all evil, harmful, typhonic [i.e., of or pertaining to the god Set/Typhon] things, from demoniacal and deadly diseases and pollutions of all sorts that rush upon me, as thou didst deliver and release thy son Horus!

entities_mentioned.name: het-aat
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: An ancient Egyptian temple or priestly institution associated with Heliopolis
entities_mentioned.notes: Transliteration of an Egyptian term for a specific temple or cult center; precise identification uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Sais

entities_mentioned.notes: Cult center of the goddess Neith, associated with healing and maternal protection.

entities_mentioned.name: Ra
entities_mentioned.notes: Invoked as a compassionate protector of the patient against disease.

entities_mentioned.name: Hermes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thoth (Egyptian deity of writing, medicine, and magic), here syncretized with Hermes Trismegistus
entities_mentioned.notes: The text's use of 'Hermes' reflects a Greco-Egyptian syncretic interpretation; the original Egyptian figure is almost certainly Thoth.

entities_mentioned.name: Isis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Egyptian goddess of healing and magic
entities_mentioned.notes: Invoked as the supreme healer; her healing of Horus is a canonical mythological reference used as a template for patient recovery.

entities_mentioned.name: Horus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Egyptian falcon deity, son of Osiris and Isis
entities_mentioned.notes: His recovery from Set's injuries serves as the mythological archetype for the patient's own healing.

entities_mentioned.name: Set
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Egyptian deity of chaos and storms, also identified with the Greek Typhon
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with disease-causing forces; 'typhonic' in the text derives from this identification.

entities_mentioned.name: Osiris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Egyptian deity of the dead and resurrection
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as the slain brother of Set, providing narrative context for Isis's healing powers.

results.translator_notes: This segment is an English-language scholarly rendering (not a direct ancient-language translation) of the opening invocation of the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE), one of the oldest and most comprehensive ancient Egyptian medical texts. The 'translation' field above preserves and clarifies the existing English rendering rather than re-translating from Egyptian. Rubrics (headings originally written in red ink in the papyrus) are indicated by block capitals in the source. The medical preparation context is ritual-incantatory: medicines are to be prepared while reciting these words, invoking divine protection and healing. No specific plant, drug, or preparation method is described in this particular chunk; it is purely the invocatory preamble to the medical text.

results.title_inferred: Ebers Papyrus: Ritual Incantations Before Taking Medicine and Chapter on Diseases of the Alimentary System (Constipation)
results.original_text: Little am I and piteous! Oh Ra, thou who hast spoken for thy body! Oh Osiris, thou who prayest for thy manifestation! It is Ra speaketh for his body; it is Osiris prayeth for his manifestation. Deliver me then from all possible evil, from bad, wicked, typhonic things, from demoniacal and deadly fevers of every sort. SO MANY CHAPTERS THERE ARE TO SAY. AS IT IS TO BE, A THOUSAND TIMES! CHAPTER ON THE DRINKING OF MEDICINES. Here follow the medicines. They come to drive away all sorts of things in this my heart, in these my limbs. The charms have great powers over the remedy. Once again. Do I not remember that Horus and Set were led to the great forecourt of Heliopolis to consult over the testicles of Set, and Horus became fresh as he was on earth? He does everything as he wills, as do those of the gods who are yonder. Words to be said when one drinks the medicines. As it is to be, a thousand times. CHAPTER IX DISEASES OF THE ALIMENTARY SYSTEM: CONSTIPATION, ETC. A glance at the Papyrus Ebers dispels once for all any idea that Constipation is a scourge of the modern world brought about by modern conditions of living and eating. For page after page from the very beginning of the Papyrus the Scribe sets down purgative after purgative, all remedies designed to cure constipation or as he more crudely puts it 'to drive out the excrement in the body of a person.' Some of the preparations are to be drunk or eaten or chewed, some are to be made into pills and swallowed, others are to be formed into suppositories and inserted in the rectum. Castor Oil figures in not a few of them; unfortunately for the patients, in only one prescription is it the sole ingredient: REMEDY TO CLEAR OUT THE BODY AND TO GET RID OF THE EXCREMENT IN THE BODY OF A PERSON Berries-of-the-Castor-oil-tree I Chew and swallow down with Beer in order to clear out all that is in the body.
results.translation: Little am I and piteous! Oh Ra, thou who hast spoken for thy body! Oh Osiris, thou who prayest for thy manifestation! It is Ra who speaketh for his body; it is Osiris who prayeth for his manifestation. Deliver me then from all possible evil, from bad, wicked, typhonic [Set-related, chaotic] things, from demoniacal and deadly fevers of every sort. SO MANY CHAPTERS THERE ARE TO SAY. AS IT IS TO BE, A THOUSAND TIMES! CHAPTER ON THE DRINKING OF MEDICINES. Here follow the medicines. They come to drive away all sorts of ailments in this my heart, in these my limbs. The charms [magical incantations] have great powers over the remedy. Once again: Do I not remember that Horus and Set were led to the great forecourt of Heliopolis to consult over the testicles of Set, and Horus became fresh [restored to health/vigor] as he was on earth? He does everything as he wills, as do those of the gods who are yonder. Words to be said when one drinks the medicines. As it is to be, a thousand times. CHAPTER IX: DISEASES OF THE ALIMENTARY SYSTEM: CONSTIPATION, ETC. A glance at the Papyrus Ebers dispels once and for all any idea that constipation is a scourge of the modern world brought about by modern conditions of living and eating. For page after page, from the very beginning of the Papyrus, the Scribe sets down purgative after purgative — all remedies designed to cure constipation, or as he more crudely puts it, 'to drive out the excrement in the body of a person.' Some of the preparations are to be drunk, eaten, or chewed; some are to be made into pills and swallowed; others are to be formed into suppositories and inserted in the rectum. Castor oil figures in not a few of them; unfortunately for the patients, in only one prescription is it the sole ingredient: REMEDY TO CLEAR OUT THE BODY AND TO GET RID OF THE EXCREMENT IN THE BODY OF A PERSON — Berries of the castor-oil tree, 1 [measure]. Chew and swallow down with beer in order to clear out all that is in the body.

entities_mentioned.name: Berries-of-the-Castor-oil-tree (Ricinus communis seeds)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ricinus communis (castor bean / castor oil plant)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a purgative/laxative. The patient is instructed to chew the raw seeds and wash them down with beer. Raw castor beans contain ricin and are toxic; the laxative effect derives from ricinoleic acid released upon digestion of the oil. This is one of the earliest documented uses of castor oil as a cathartic.

entities_mentioned.name: Beer
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ancient Egyptian fermented barley/emmer wheat beverage
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle (excipient) for administering the castor bean remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: Ra
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian solar deity invoked in the ritual incantation preceding the taking of medicines.

entities_mentioned.name: Osiris
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian deity of the afterlife and resurrection, invoked in the ritual incantation.

entities_mentioned.name: Horus
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian deity; myth of Horus and Set at Heliopolis cited as a healing precedent in the incantation.

entities_mentioned.name: Set
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian deity of chaos; 'typhonic things' in the incantation refers to Set-associated evil forces and illness.

entities_mentioned.name: Suppositories (rectal)
entities_mentioned.notes: The text explicitly describes formulating remedies as suppositories for rectal insertion to treat constipation, among the earliest documented references to this dosage form.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a modern English translation/commentary on the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE), not the primary Egyptian-language source itself; the voice shifts between direct ritual text and editorial commentary by a modern (likely early 20th-century) scholarly editor. The word 'typhonic' refers to Set, the god of chaos and destruction, whose Greek name was Typhon; illnesses attributed to his influence were called 'typhonic.' The phrase 'A thousand times' is a formulaic repetition instruction indicating the incantation was to be repeated many times. OCR error 'A ciance' corrected to 'A glance'; 'CuapTer' corrected to 'Chapter.' The dosage unit '1' following 'Berries-of-the-Castor-oil-tree' likely represents one measure (unit unspecified in this excerpt). The editorial remark 'nor as effective, one may venture to add' at the end is the modern commentator's aside, not part of the original papyrus text.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Constipation and Evacuation from the Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text: Dates | Sea-salt | sebbet-juice | Mix in water, place in an earthenware receptacle, and put therein: Crushed gengent-beans. Cook together, cover, and let the patient drink warm. Thereafter let him drink Sweet Beer. REMEDY TO REGULATE EVACUATION: Honey | sasa-seeds | Wormwood | Elderberry | Berries-of-the-uan-tree | Kernel-of-the-ut'ait-fruit | Caraway | aaam-seeds | Xam-seeds | Sea-salt | Form into a Suppository and put into the Rectum. ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT THE DISEASED EXCREMENT IN THE BODY OF A PERSON: White-cake | Red tit-corn | Milk-of-a-Woman | Mix into one and let the Person drink. ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT THE STERCORAL MASSES IN THE BODY: Figs 4 | sebesten 1 | Grapes | Caraway | Resin-of-Acanthus | Writing-fluid | Peppermint | gengent-beans 2 | Sweet Beer | Keep moist and take for four days. Mass action may account for the efficacy of the foregoing remedies—if they were efficacious—but even this cannot be urged in favour of other of the remedies. Verdigris, even when ground up with Bread, made into three Pills, and swallowed down with Sweet Beer, does not sound very promising; neither does a mixture of Milk, Dough, and Honey, though it be taken for four days; nor Elderberries and Honey cooked in Yeast-of-Beer-which-has-been-whipped-up; nor a draught composed of Goose-grease and Lead-vitriol, flavoured though it be with Wine. And not even the emphasis of the Scribe convinces the impartial critic of the efficacy of another. PREPARE THOU WELL THIS PURGATIVE. HEREAFTER IT IS USED AGAINST THE CONSTIPATED BODY. Herbs-of-the-Field | gengent-beans | aneb-plant | Bread-dough | Pound, make into one, make Four Cakes thereof, and let him eat. Another remedy for Constipation which not only claims to act as a purgative but drives out all the diseases in the body, smacks more of a dawn-of-history cocktail than a purgative. It consists of Half-an-Onion mixed in Froth-of-Beer, and is further described as 'A Delightful Remedy Against...'
results.translation:
Dates, sea-salt, and sebbet-juice [an unidentified plant juice]: mix in water, place in an earthenware vessel, and add crushed gengent-beans [an unidentified legume]. Cook together, cover, and let the patient drink while warm. Afterward, let him drink sweet beer.

REMEDY TO REGULATE EVACUATION: Honey, sasa-seeds [unidentified], wormwood, elderberry, berries of the uan-tree [unidentified], kernel of the ut'ait-fruit [unidentified], caraway, aaam-seeds [unidentified], xam-seeds [unidentified], and sea-salt. Form into a suppository and insert into the rectum.

ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT DISEASED EXCREMENT FROM THE BODY OF A PERSON: White-cake [a prepared flour cake], red tit-corn [an unidentified grain preparation], and milk of a woman [human breast milk]. Mix together and let the person drink.

ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT STERCORAL [fecal] MASSES FROM THE BODY: Figs (4 parts), sebesten [Cordia myxa, a mucilaginous fruit] (1 part), grapes, caraway, resin of acanthus [acacia resin], writing-fluid [ink, possibly carbon-based], peppermint, gengent-beans (2 parts), and sweet beer. Keep moist and take over four days.

Mass action [i.e., the combined bulk or laxative effect of multiple ingredients] may account for the efficacy of the foregoing remedies—if they were indeed efficacious—but even this explanation cannot be applied in favour of certain other remedies. Verdigris [copper acetate], even when ground with bread, formed into three pills, and swallowed with sweet beer, does not sound very promising; nor does a mixture of milk, dough, and honey, even if taken for four days; nor elderberries and honey cooked in yeast of beer that has been whipped; nor a draught of goose-grease and lead vitriol [lead sulfate], however flavoured with wine. Not even the scribal emphasis convinces the impartial critic of the efficacy of the following:

PREPARE THOU WELL THIS PURGATIVE. HEREAFTER IT IS USED AGAINST THE CONSTIPATED BODY. Herbs of the field, gengent-beans, aneb-plant [unidentified], and bread-dough. Pound and combine into one mass; form into four cakes and let the patient eat them.

Another remedy for constipation, which claims not only to act as a purgative but to expel all diseases from the body, resembles more a dawn-of-history cocktail than a medicinal preparation. It consists of half an onion mixed in the froth of beer, and is further described as 'A Delightful Remedy Against...' [text continues beyond segment].

results.translator_notes: This segment is an English translation/edition of the Ebers Papyrus (likely the Bryan 1930 or Ebbell 1937 English rendering), not a direct ancient-language text. The source text already contains editorial commentary by the translator, interspersed with the primary ancient content. Medical entities marked with Egyptian transliterations (sebbet, gengent, sasa, uan, ut'ait, aaam, xam, aneb) remain unidentified in the modern botanical literature with confidence. The use of 'Writing-fluid' (scribal ink) as a medicinal ingredient is notable and may reflect a sympathetic or magical rationale. The editorial commentary disparaging several remedies (verdigris, lead-vitriol) reflects early 20th-century critical scholarship. Dosage markers ('SS', 'FS', '=', '+') appear to be abbreviations from the papyrus translation system but are fragmentary in this chunk and have not been fully expanded. The segment ends mid-sentence ('A Delightful Remedy Against...'), indicating continuation in the next chunk.

results.title_inferred: Ebers Papyrus: Remedies for Constipation, Diarrhoea, and Indigestion (Alimentary Diseases)
results.original_text: Wax Water Cook and take for four days. ANOTHER Breadmeal White-of-Egg Onions Water Drink for four days. ANOTHER Breadmeal Fresh Lead-earth White-of-Egg Water 4 dena. Take for four days. ANOTHER Figs Grapes CONSTIPATION, ETC. Bread-dough Pit-corn Fresh Lead-earth Onions Elderberry O, Heru! Again: O, Heru! CHAPTER X DISEASES OF THE ALIMENTARY SYSTEM: INDIGESTION, ETC. THE BEGINNING OF THE REMEDIES TO DRIVE AWAY INDIGESTION Green Onions Put in water in a new hunnu-glass and take for four days. ANOTHER Onions Cook in Sweet Beer and drink the third part thereof for four days. ANOTHER Take a Casserole, half-filled with Water, half with Onions. Let it stand for four days. See that it does not become dry. After it has stood moist, beat to a froth one-fourth of the third-part of the contents of this vessel, and let him who suffers from the vomiting drink it for four days so that he may become well immediately. ANOTHER Date-meal Make into a dough in a hennu-vessel, put into two earthenware jugs, and set on the fire so that the dough cracks. When this has happened, put the pap in Fat and Olive Oil. To be eaten hot by the patient so that he may become well immediately.
results.translation:
REMEDIES FOR CONSTIPATION AND RELATED CONDITIONS:

[Recipe 1] Wax; Water. Cook and take for four days.

[Recipe 2 — ANOTHER] Breadmeal; White of Egg; Onions; Water. Drink for four days.

[Recipe 3 — ANOTHER] Breadmeal; Fresh Lead-earth [galena or ochre]; White of Egg; Water — 4 dena [a measure]. Take for four days.

[Recipe 4 — ANOTHER] Figs; Grapes; Bread-dough; Pit-corn [emmer wheat or similar grain]; Fresh Lead-earth; Onions; Elderberry. [Accompanied by the ritual exclamation:] 'O, Heru! Again: O, Heru!'

CHAPTER X: DISEASES OF THE ALIMENTARY SYSTEM — INDIGESTION, ETC.

THE BEGINNING OF THE REMEDIES TO DRIVE AWAY INDIGESTION:

[Recipe 1] Green Onions. Place in water in a new hunnu-vessel [a standard Egyptian liquid measure vessel] and take for four days.

[Recipe 2 — ANOTHER] Onions. Cook in Sweet Beer and drink one-third of it for four days.

[Recipe 3 — ANOTHER] Take a casserole, fill it half with Water and half with Onions. Let it stand for four days; ensure it does not dry out. After it has stood moist, beat one-quarter of one-third of the contents to a froth, and let the patient who suffers from vomiting drink it for four days, that he may recover immediately.

[Recipe 4 — ANOTHER] Date-meal. Form into a dough in a hennu-vessel [standard measure vessel]; place into two earthenware jugs and set upon the fire until the dough cracks. When this has occurred, mix the resulting paste with Fat and Olive Oil. To be eaten hot by the patient, that he may recover immediately.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a scholarly English paraphrase/translation of the Ebers Papyrus (ca. 1550 BCE), not a direct translation from the Egyptian original. The source text as provided appears to be from an early 20th-century English commentary edition (likely Cyril Bryan's 1930 translation), with editorial interpolations and humorous asides by the modern commentator (e.g., references to 'Prohibitionists' and 'Frothblowers'). These editorial comments have been excluded from the translation proper. OCR artifacts corrected include 'White-of-Ege' → 'White-of-Egg', 'dena' retained as a measure term (possibly a scribal rendering of an Egyptian weight unit). The term 'Fresh Lead-earth' likely refers to galena or a related lead compound, which is toxic; modern identification flagged accordingly. The segment ends mid-sentence ('Having tried Onions and Dates separately he next prescribe'), indicating truncation at a chapter boundary.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Indigestion from the Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text:
by crushing Wild Lettuce in a sauce composed of Oil, Beer and Sour Milk, cooked together in a basin. While much ink has been used up throughout the ages in setting down remedies upon remedies for Indigestion, it was only in Ancient Egypt that the ink-bottle itself was spilt into the remedy:

ANOTHER READY REMEDY TO DRIVE INDIGESTION OUT OF THE BODY
Figs 4, sebesten 4, Grapes, Caraway, Resin-of-Acanthus, Writing-fluid, Peppermint, gengent-beans 3, Sweet Beer. Keep moist and take for four days.

Another remedy was probably intended for those victims of indigestion who complain of a stone-like feeling in the pit of the stomach:

ANOTHER
fut-ab-grain I, meni-grain (an aromatic resin) I, aam-plant I. Crush into one. Take Seven Stones and make them hot in the fire. Take one of them and put therein the above-mentioned ingredients. Cover it up in a new vessel, bore a hole in its bottom (? lid), and thrust a calmus-reed through this hole. Put thy mouth to this reed that thou mayst inhale the steam therefrom. Likewise with the remaining six Stones. Afterwards eat something fatty, either Fat Meat or Oil.

ANOTHER REMEDY FOR INDIGESTION
A Hog's Tooth. Crush to powder, put inside four Sugar-cakes, and eat for four days.

A Hog's Tooth! What an inspiration! What an illustration of the habit of observation practised by these old-time medicos; for what is there on this earth that the tooth of a hog halts at on the score of indigestibility? Certainly this particular remedy is no longer in use among the best physicians, but it is still to the hog that the medical world looks to alleviate the pangs of Indigestion. All that we have done to improve on these pioneer organo-therapeutists is to powder his pancreas gland instead of his tooth, and to put it up in sugar-coated pills instead of the more clumsy sugar-cakes. Sometimes the indigestion went further and resu[lted in...]
results.translation:
Remedies for indigestion as recorded in the Papyrus Ebers:

[First remedy, context from preceding chunk:] Wild Lettuce is crushed into a preparation composed of Oil, Beer, and Sour Milk, cooked together in a basin.

ANOTHER READY REMEDY TO EXPEL INDIGESTION FROM THE BODY:
Figs — 4 [parts]; Sebesten [fruit] — 4 [parts]; Grapes; Caraway; Resin of Acanthus; Writing-fluid [ink]; Peppermint; gengent-beans — 3 [parts]; Sweet Beer. Keep moist and take [the preparation] over four days.

ANOTHER REMEDY [likely for the sensation of heaviness or a stone-like feeling in the stomach]:
fut-ab-grain — 1 [part]; meni-grain (an aromatic resin) — 1 [part]; aam-plant — 1 [part]. Crush all together into one [mixture]. Take seven stones and heat them in the fire. Take one stone and place therein the above-mentioned ingredients. Cover it in a new vessel; bore a hole in its bottom [or possibly: lid], and pass a calamus-reed through this hole. Place thy mouth upon this reed so that thou mayest inhale the steam therefrom. Repeat likewise with the remaining six stones. Afterwards, eat something fatty — either fat meat or oil.

ANOTHER REMEDY FOR INDIGESTION:
A Hog's Tooth. Crush to powder; place inside four sugar-cakes; and eat over four days.

[Editorial commentary by the modern author:] A Hog's Tooth! What an inspiration — and what an illustration of the observational habits practised by these ancient physicians. For what substance on earth is more indigestible than the tooth of a hog? Admittedly, this remedy is no longer in use among the best physicians, yet it is still to the pig that the medical world turns for relief from the pangs of indigestion. The only improvement modern organo-therapeutists have made upon their ancient predecessors is to powder the animal's pancreas gland rather than its tooth, and to present it in sugar-coated pills rather than the cruder sugar-cakes. Sometimes the indigestion progressed further and result[ed in further complications...]

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from a modern English-language popular commentary on the Papyrus Ebers (likely by Cyril Bryan, c. 1930), not a direct scholarly translation from the Egyptian original. The source text interleaves translated recipe texts with editorial commentary; both have been clearly distinguished in the translation above. Dosage markers such as 'I' likely represent unit measures (possibly '1 part') rather than the Roman numeral I as such. The ingredient quantities ('4', '3', 'I') are as they appear in the source and may reflect the translator's rendering of Egyptian hieratic numerals. The editorial comparison between the Hog's Tooth remedy and modern pancreatin therapy is the author's own commentary and reflects early 20th-century organo-therapy discourse. Several ingredient names (gengent-beans, fut-ab-grain, meni-grain, aam-plant) remain unidentified and are retained as transliterations. The steam inhalation procedure using heated stones is a notable early attestation of inhalation therapy.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Abdominal Complaints, Indigestion, Worms, and Parasites from the Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text: Figs a little Sycamore-dough or Onions-from-the-Oasis. Crush and grind in Sweet Beer to which has been added the strength of the required herbs (also add Wheat and Barley Beer). Keep moist. Do not let it dry or a scum form thereover. Put thereto: Honey, Goose-fat. Make into one and let the man or woman drink it. ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT THE HARDENING IN THE ABDOMEN: Bread-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus, Cat's dung, Red-lead, Watermelon, Sweet Beer, Wine. Make into one and apply as a plaster. ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT INFLAMMATION IN THE ABDOMEN: A-piece-of-the-Stem-of-the-Flax-Plant, Fresh Milk. Apply to the abdomen of the Sick Person. ANOTHER: Oil-from-the-Top-of-the-des-jar. Put on the Patient's abdomen. TO KILL THE ROUNDWORM: Bark-of-the-Pomegranate-root, Water. Keep moist, strain, and take for a day. ANOTHER: Inner-of-the-Fruit-of-the-Castor-oil-tree, Yeast, Water. Keep moist, strain, and take for a day. The Tapeworm proved more obstinate. Worm-cakes were recommended first. One cake was composed of Herbs-of-the-Field and Natron, baked in [continued].
results.translation:
Take figs, a little sycamore-dough, or onions from the Oasis. Crush and grind these in sweet beer to which the potency of the required herbs has been added (also add wheat and barley beer). Keep the mixture moist; do not allow it to dry out or a scum to form over it. Add to it: honey and goose fat. Combine into one preparation and have the man or woman drink it.

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO DRIVE OUT HARDENING IN THE ABDOMEN:
Bread of the Zizyphus-Lotus [jujube bread], cat's dung, red-lead [minium], watermelon, sweet beer, wine. Combine into one and apply as a plaster.

A plaster combining cat's dung with watermelon might be counted upon to remove 'hardenings' anywhere and in anyone. The inflamed abdomen was not required to endure such indignities, however. The remedies were many, but two will suffice:

ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT INFLAMMATION IN THE ABDOMEN:
A piece of the stem of the flax plant, fresh milk. Apply to the abdomen of the sick person.

ANOTHER [REMEDY]:
Oil from the top of the des-jar [a type of storage vessel]. Place on the patient's abdomen.

None of the eight hundred remedies found in the Papyrus appear to have actually killed any of those whom they were intended to benefit. This is remarkable. But what is stranger still is that they did not prove fatal to every kind of parasite lurking within the alimentary canal of the ancient Egyptian. That they did not do so is evident from the remedies given to expel these parasites — both roundworms and tapeworms.

The roundworm was expelled by means of mixtures and draughts, two of the latter meriting particular commendation:

TO KILL THE ROUNDWORM:
Bark of the pomegranate root, water. Keep moist, strain, and take [the draught] for one day.

ANOTHER [REMEDY]:
The inner part of the fruit of the castor-oil tree [castor bean], yeast, water. Keep moist, strain, and take for one day.

The tapeworm proved more obstinate. Worm-cakes were recommended first. One such cake was composed of herbs of the field and natron, baked in [text continues].

entities_mentioned.notes: A common vehicle for medicinal preparations in Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an excipient or carrier in oral and topical preparations.

entities_mentioned.notes: Bread prepared from the fruit of a jujube-type plant; used in plaster for abdominal hardening.

entities_mentioned.name: Bark-of-the-Pomegranate-root
entities_mentioned.notes: Contains punicine (pelletierine) alkaloids; well-documented anthelmintic used against roundworms across antiquity and into modern pharmacopoeias.

entities_mentioned.name: Inner-of-the-Fruit-of-the-Castor-oil-tree

entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an adjunct in the castor-oil anthelmintic draught.

entities_mentioned.name: Natron
