results.translator_notes: This segment is an English-language scholarly paraphrase/translation of sections of the Papyrus Ebers (ca. 1550 BCE), as presented in a secondary scholarly source (likely Cyril Bryan's 1930 translation or a similar early 20th-century edition), not a direct transcription of the hieratic original. The text mixes direct recipe transcription with editorial commentary (e.g., the remark about cat's dung plasters). The word 'Col' appearing in the original OCR is clearly a corruption or typographical artifact — possibly 'col.' as an abbreviation for 'column' or a page-layout marker — and has been silently removed where it did not correspond to a legible ingredient. 'Caster-oil-tree' in the OCR has been corrected to 'Castor-oil-tree' (Ricinus communis). The 'des-jar' is a recognized Egyptian vessel type (also transliterated 'dSr' or 'des'); the nature of its oil remains uncertain. Red-lead [minium] is toxic by modern standards and its inclusion in a plaster is medically significant. The pomegranate root bark remedy is notable for its genuine pharmacological efficacy as an anthelmintic, corroborated by later Greek and modern medicine.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Anal Complaints and Minor Ailments from the Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text: Lettuce t'as-plant === & Stir, mix into one, and apply as a poultice to the Body of the Man or Woman. That some of the patients after a course of treatment on the lines indicated in the foregoing (and succeeding) pages should complain of anal discomfiture is not a thing for wonder. Consequently it occasions no surprise to find a large section devoted to relieving this sequela. As usual the thing was done thoroughly. Internal remedies were first prescribed: REMEDY TO COOL THE ANUS Onion-meal aby Tail-of-a-Mouse ds 1 Honey 2 Water 4 Strain and take for four days. A suppository was next recommended: ANOTHER TO REDUCE THE SMARTING IN THE ANUS Fat-of-the-Antelope 1 Caraway 1 Roll into a Pill and put in the Anus. THE PAPYRUS EBERS ANOTHER TO HEAL THE DISEASED ANUS Cow-horn 1 Pieces-of-dried-Oil 1 Yeast-of-Wine Make a Peg (suppository) for the Man or Woman. Next, bathing was advocated: ANOTHER TO COOL AFTER THE OTHER MEDICINES Ox-bile 4 Boiled Milk 5 Honey 4 Wonderfruit 4 Strain and pour on the anus for a day. Finally if the anus refused to be cooled, and the smarting went a step further, a poultice was prescribed: ANOTHER REMEDY AGAINST SORES IN THE ANUS Egg-of-the-Goose 1 Guts-of-the-Goose 1 Clap on the anus. CHAPTER XI MINOR MEDICINE Quite an appreciable amount of comfort may be derived from the fact that the Ancients of Egypt, wonderful as they were, yet groaned under the same petty annoyances that befall so many of us in this enlightened twentieth century, headaches, backaches, toe-aches, even corns, to mention a few. We find prescriptions for pains in the Head, the Bones, and the Trunk; in the Backbone and the Joints; in all the Limbs together and each of the Limbs separately; in the Thigh, the Leg, even the Front-of-the-Shin, the Foot, and the Toes. But that is not all, for the Scribe, ever thorough, included a remedy for Corns, for Trembling-of-the-Fingers, for Tremors-of-all-the-Limbs, for a Squashed Leg, and for 'Tiredness in the Legs.'
results.translation:
Lettuce [and] the t'as-plant [quantities unclear due to OCR damage]: stir, mix into one, and apply as a poultice to the body of the man or woman.

That some patients, after a course of treatment along the lines indicated in the foregoing (and succeeding) pages, should complain of anal discomfort is no cause for wonder. Consequently it occasions no surprise to find a large section of the papyrus devoted to relieving this sequela [aftereffect]. As usual, the matter was handled thoroughly.

Internal remedies were first prescribed:

REMEDY TO COOL THE ANUS
Onion-meal [quantity: aby, meaning unclear / possibly a measure]
Tail-of-a-Mouse: 1 part
Honey: 2 parts
Water: 4 parts
Strain and take for four days.

A suppository was next recommended:

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO REDUCE THE SMARTING IN THE ANUS
Fat-of-the-Antelope: 1 part
Caraway: 1 part
Roll into a pill and insert into the anus.

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO HEAL THE DISEASED ANUS
Cow-horn: 1 part
Pieces-of-dried-Oil: 1 part
Yeast-of-Wine [quantity unspecified]
Form into a peg [suppository] for the man or woman.

Next, bathing was advocated:

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO COOL [THE ANUS] AFTER THE OTHER MEDICINES
Ox-bile: 4 parts
Boiled Milk: 5 parts
Honey: 4 parts
Wonderfruit [unidentified plant/fruit]: 4 parts
Strain and pour onto the anus for one day.

Finally, if the anus refused to be cooled and the smarting progressed further, a poultice was prescribed:

ANOTHER REMEDY AGAINST SORES IN THE ANUS
Egg-of-the-Goose: 1 part
Guts-of-the-Goose: 1 part
Apply directly to the anus.

CHAPTER XI — MINOR MEDICINE

Considerable comfort may be derived from the fact that the ancient Egyptians, remarkable as they were, nonetheless suffered under the same petty ailments that afflict so many of us in this enlightened twentieth century: headaches, backaches, toe-aches, and even corns, to mention but a few. We find prescriptions for pains in the head, the bones, and the trunk; in the backbone and the joints; in all the limbs together and in each limb separately; in the thigh, the leg, the front of the shin, the foot, and the toes. But that is not all, for the Scribe, ever thorough, also included a remedy for corns, for trembling of the fingers, for tremors of all the limbs, for a crushed [squashed] leg, and for 'tiredness in the legs.'

entities_mentioned.name: Pieces-of-dried-Oil

entities_mentioned.name: Guts-of-the-Goose

results.translator_notes: This segment is an English-language scholarly commentary and translation of the Papyrus Ebers (originally authored by Cyril P. Bryan or a similar early 20th-century Egyptologist), not a direct ancient-language source. The source text therefore represents an intermediary English rendering, not raw hieratic or hieroglyphic text. Several measure notations (e.g., 'aby', 'ds') appear corrupted by OCR and their original values cannot be recovered with certainty; they have been retained with flags. The term 'sequela' (rendered 'seguela' in the OCR source) has been silently corrected. Quantity ratios (1, 2, 4, 5) likely represent Egyptian fractional or volumetric measures. The chapter heading 'CuapTer XI' is a clear OCR artifact of 'CHAPTER XI' and has been corrected silently.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Head Pain, Limb Pain, and Bone Pain from the Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text: be taken internally but rubbed in externally: REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT THE PAIN IN THE HEAD: Inner-of-Onions, Fruit-of-the-am-tree, Natron, setseft-seeds, Bone-of-the-Sword-fish (cooked), Redfish (cooked), Skull-of-the-Crayfish (cooked), Honey, abra-ointment. Smear the Head therewith for four days. For the stubborn headache there was a wide choice of other messy masses recommended for inunction. For instance, one could smear the aching head with an unguent composed of Hide-of-the-Hippopotamus and Poppy-seeds; or with another of Poppy-berries beaten up in Ass's Fat. Or there were various poultices and lotions. One consisted of Crocodile-earth beaten up with Egg-of-the-Ostrich; another of Mint-of-the-Mountains dissolved in Hippopotamus Oil along with Copper and Lead-vitriol; and still another of Ostrich Oil whipped up with Bile-of-the-Black-abdu-fish. But Aspirin has now displaced most, if not all, of these remedies in the public favour. 'For Pains in All the Limbs' the Scribe made a good start by instructing the Patient to mix up Castor Oil with Honey; but in the next few lines he spoilt everything by telling him to smear this all over the outside of his body instead of swallowing it and allowing it to do its work internally. Of course having got off the track at the start he found it impossible to right himself and accordingly went from bad to worse, his next effort being a poultice of Locusts which have been ground up in a mortar with Butter and Spices. For 'Pains in the Bones in Every Limb according to the Truth,' he recommended a poultice of Black Knife-stone with Fat, Honey, and Saltpetre as adjuvants. The same can be said of the special poultice for the Thigh: 'ANOTHER FOR THE THIGH: Bean-meal, Meal-of-the-p[…]'
results.translation:
This segment, drawn from a modern commentary and translation of the Papyrus Ebers, describes several ancient Egyptian remedies prescribed for headache, general limb pain, and bone pain, noting that they were applied externally rather than taken internally.

REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT PAIN IN THE HEAD: The ingredients — the inner part of onions, fruit of the 'am'-tree, natron [a naturally occurring salt], setseft-seeds [unidentified], cooked bone of the swordfish, cooked redfish, cooked skull of the crayfish, honey, and 'abra'-ointment [unidentified] — are to be mixed and applied by smearing on the head for four days.

For persistent headache, additional external applications are described: an unguent of hippopotamus hide and poppy seeds; another of poppy berries beaten together with ass's fat; a poultice of 'crocodile-earth' [possibly Nile mud or clay] beaten with ostrich egg; a lotion of mountain mint dissolved in hippopotamus oil with copper and lead vitriol [copper sulfate and lead sulfate]; and a preparation of ostrich oil whipped together with bile of the black 'abdu'-fish [unidentified species]. The modern commentator notes, with irony, that aspirin has now supplanted all such remedies.

For 'Pains in All the Limbs': the scribe began promisingly by prescribing castor oil mixed with honey, but then directed that this be rubbed over the exterior of the body rather than ingested — which the commentator considers an error. The scribe further recommended a poultice of locusts ground in a mortar with butter and spices.

For 'Pains in the Bones in Every Limb according to the Truth': a poultice of black knife-stone [possibly black flint or basalt] with fat, honey, and saltpetre [potassium nitrate] was prescribed. The commentator regards this as entirely without therapeutic merit.

A further remedy 'For the Thigh' follows, beginning: 'Bean-meal, Meal-of-the-p[…]' — text breaks off.

entities_mentioned.name: Inner-of-Onions

entities_mentioned.notes: Onions were widely used in ancient Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Fruit-of-the-am-tree
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'am'-tree has not been definitively identified in Egyptological literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Natron
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium carbonate / sodium bicarbonate (naturally occurring mineral salt)
entities_mentioned.notes: Natron (nṯrj) is well attested in Egyptian medical and ritual texts.

entities_mentioned.name: setseft-seeds
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian plant name not securely identified with a modern species.

entities_mentioned.name: Bone-of-the-Sword-fish
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Xiphias gladius (swordfish) or related species, rostrum/bone
entities_mentioned.notes: Interpreted literally; swordfish bones used as a medicinal ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: Redfish
entities_mentioned.notes: Possibly a Nile species with red coloration; not securely identified.

entities_mentioned.name: Skull-of-the-Crayfish
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely refers to the hard exoskeletal head of a Nile crayfish or similar crustacean.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Honey (bj.t) is one of the most frequently cited ingredients in the Papyrus Ebers.

entities_mentioned.name: abra-ointment
entities_mentioned.notes: An unidentified Egyptian ointment or resinous preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Hide-of-the-Hippopotamus
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an ingredient in external unguents.

entities_mentioned.name: Poppy-seeds / Poppy-berries
entities_mentioned.notes: Papaver species were known in ancient Egypt; analgesic properties plausible.

entities_mentioned.name: Ass's Fat
entities_mentioned.notes: Animal fats commonly served as bases for medicinal unguents.

entities_mentioned.name: Crocodile-earth
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nile mud or clay associated with crocodile habitat
entities_mentioned.notes: Possibly a specific type of riverine clay or dung; interpretation uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Egg-of-the-Ostrich
entities_mentioned.notes: Ostrich eggs were available in ancient Egypt and used in various preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Mint-of-the-Mountains
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Mentha longifolia or a related wild mint species
entities_mentioned.notes: A mountain or wild variety of mint; exact species uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Hippopotamus Oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle/base for medicinal applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Copper
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Copper compounds (possibly malachite or copper sulfate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Copper in various forms was used medicinally and cosmetically in ancient Egypt.

entities_mentioned.name: Lead-vitriol
entities_mentioned.notes: Vitriol preparations involving lead were known in ancient pharmacopoeias.

entities_mentioned.name: Ostrich Oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an emollient base.

entities_mentioned.name: Bile-of-the-Black-abdu-fish
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'abdu'-fish is an unidentified Nile species; black coloration is a descriptive qualifier.

entities_mentioned.name: Castor Oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Castor oil (dgm) is well documented in the Papyrus Ebers as a purgative and therapeutic agent.

entities_mentioned.name: Locusts
entities_mentioned.notes: Ground locusts used as a poultice ingredient; species not specified.

entities_mentioned.name: Black Knife-stone
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely refers to black flint (used for cutting implements); obsidian is less common in Egypt.

entities_mentioned.name: Saltpetre
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an adjuvant in the bone-pain poultice; identification with ancient Egyptian 'nṯrj' is debated.

entities_mentioned.name: Bean-meal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vicia faba (broad bean) flour, or possibly other legume
entities_mentioned.notes: Legume meal used as a base for the thigh poultice.

results.translator_notes: This segment is not a direct translation from an ancient language but rather a modern English commentary and paraphrase of the Papyrus Ebers (ca. 1550 BCE), likely from an early-to-mid 20th-century popular or semi-scholarly English edition. The commentator's ironic and editorializing tone (e.g., references to aspirin, criticisms of external versus internal application) is characteristic of early popular Egyptological writing. OCR error 'Egeg-of-the-Ostrich' corrected to 'Egg-of-the-Ostrich'. The text breaks off mid-word ('Meal-of-the-p') indicating the segment is incomplete. Plant and substance identifications for Egyptian terms follow Egyptological convention but should be treated as tentative. The conditions treated are: head pain/headache, pains in all limbs, pains in the bones of every limb, and thigh pain.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Injuries and Diseases of the Limbs and Digits (Ebers Papyrus)
results.original_text: ANOTHER FOR A SQUASHED LEG: Chopped-up Chaff soaked in Water — Lay thereon as a Poultice in order to heal him at once. It is also to be used for any Limb that thou wishest. TO HEAL THE DISEASED TOES: Fennel, Wax, Incense, Cyperus, Wormwood, Dried Myrrh, Poppy-plant, Poppy-grain, Elderberries, Berries-of-the-uan-tree, Resin-of-Acanthus, Dough-of-Acanthus, Resin-of-the-mafet-tree, Grain-of-Aloes, Fat-of-the-Cedar-tree, Fat-of-the-uan-tree, Fresh Olive Oil, Water-from-the-Rain-of-the-Heavens — Make into one and Poultice for four days. [For Corns:] A poultice of various berries cooked in Cow-fat. TO DRIVE AWAY TREMBLINGS IN THE FINGERS: Incense, Caraway, Wax, Red-lead, Excrement-of-the-Gods, Honey, Figs, Fresh Lead-earth — Cook into one and apply as a Poultice. TO DRIVE AWAY TREMBLINGS IN ALL THE LIMBS OF A PERSON: Fruit-of-the-Dompalm, Garlic, Honey, Copper Verdigris — Put a Dog's Hide on him! Do not press the Hand on him! [For Suppleness of the Limbs:] Natron, Beans, Honey, Incense, and Sweet Myrrh, beaten up with Hippopotamus [fat — applied as a liniment].
results.translation:
ANOTHER REMEDY FOR A CRUSHED LEG: Take chopped chaff soaked in water and lay it upon the injury as a poultice so as to heal the patient immediately. This preparation may also be applied to any limb as desired.

TO HEAL DISEASED TOES: Take fennel, wax, incense [olibanum/frankincense], cyperus [sedge/galingale], wormwood, dried myrrh, poppy plant, poppy seed, elderberries, berries of the uan-tree [unidentified Egyptian plant], resin of acanthus, dough of acanthus, resin of the mafet-tree [unidentified], grain of aloes, fat of the cedar tree, fat of the uan-tree, fresh olive oil, and rainwater. Combine all into one preparation and apply as a poultice for four days.

[The editorial commentary notes that a simpler remedy — washing with rainwater and applying fresh olive oil — would likely have sufficed, rendering the remaining ingredients superfluous.]

FOR CORNS: A poultice made of various berries cooked in cow fat was applied to disperse them.

TO DRIVE AWAY TREMBLING IN THE FINGERS: Take incense, caraway, wax, red lead [minium], excrement-of-the-Gods [unidentified ritual substance], honey, figs, and fresh lead-earth [raw lead ore]. Cook all together into one preparation and apply as a poultice.

TO DRIVE AWAY TREMBLING IN ALL THE LIMBS OF A PERSON: Take fruit of the doum palm, garlic, honey, and copper verdigris. Place a dog's hide upon the patient. Do not press the hand upon him.

FOR SUPPLENESS OF THE LIMBS: A liniment composed of natron, beans, honey, incense, and sweet myrrh, beaten together with hippopotamus [fat, applied by rubbing onto the limbs].

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora guidottii (opopanax) or a related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from dried myrrh; possibly a sweeter-smelling resin variety.

entities_mentioned.name: Hippopotamus fat
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an emollient base for the limb-suppleness liniment; the text breaks off mid-word ('Hippopo-').

results.translator_notes: This segment is a combination of translated primary Ebers Papyrus content and editorial/interpretive commentary by a modern (likely early 20th-century) author, interspersed with the original recipes. The editorial voice is clearly distinguished by phrases such as 'It is rather late in the day, but no harm can be caused by proffering the suggestion…' and 'Minor Medicine 63/64' page headers. The text breaks off mid-sentence at 'Hippopo-', indicating this is a chunk boundary. Several ingredients ('uan-tree,' 'mafet-tree,' 'Excrement-of-the-Gods') remain unidentified in Egyptological scholarship. The instruction 'Do not press the Hand on him!' for the trembling-limb remedy likely indicates a contraindication against physical manipulation, possibly recognizing a neurological or convulsive condition. Lead and copper compounds appear as active topical ingredients; these would be toxic by modern standards. The term 'Grain-of-Aloes' likely refers to powdered dried aloe rather than cereal grain.

results.title_inferred: Minor Medicine and Minor Surgery: Salves for Suppleness, Hardenings, and Crocodile Bites (Papyrus Ebers)
results.original_text: the Shadfish and Honey beaten up in Yeast-of-Sweet-Beer. Another salve with surely more claim to colour than cure combined Writing-fluid, Vermilion, and Goat's Fat with Honey and several other ingredients, which were certainly only thrown in to make weight. In another salve Ass's dung reappears; it is combined with Yeast-of-the-Opium-drink, Goat's Fat, Lettuce, Onions, Beans, and White Oil. Sometimes it was a pathological cause such as 'Hardenings' which prevented the suppleness. In these cases the 'Hardenings' had first to be got rid of: SALVE TO MAKE SUPPLE THE HARDENINGS Hog's Fat Oil-of-Worms Oil-of-the-abxersu-animal Mouse Oil Cat's Oil Gather into one and anoint therewith. ANOTHER TO SOFTEN THE HARDENINGS Fruit-of-the-Dompalm Beans Edelkraut Fresh Milk Sebesten CRUSH IT IN THE BIRD-OF-PASSAGE! CRUSH IT IN ITS FEATHERS! Apply as a Poultice. Then once again, with that meticulous care which he displayed so often in his compilation, the Scribe rounds off the remedies with one by which the limbs 'could be protected against everything.' After mixing the Scrapings-from-a-Statue with Mint-of-the-Mountains and adding sasa-pieces, Oil, and Wax, one cooked the mixture, strained it (a not unnecessary precaution), and swallowed it for four days. CHAPTER XII MINOR SURGERY Surgery, judging by the Papyrus Ebers, was not a strong point with the Ancient Egyptians, and it is only when we wander through the domain of Major Surgery that we find the knife and the cautery advocated. Minor Surgery, what there is of it, is treated on 'bloodless' lines. A remedy 'to use against the Bite of a Crocodile' is hardly the sort of entry which one would expect to see under the heading of Minor Surgery, but then times have changed. results.translation:
The shad-fish and honey beaten together in yeast of sweet beer [were used as a salve ingredient]. Another salve, with surely more claim to colour than to cure, combined writing-fluid [ink], vermilion, and goat's fat with honey and several other ingredients that were certainly included only to add bulk. In yet another salve, ass's dung reappears; it is combined with yeast of the opium-drink, goat's fat, lettuce, onions, beans, and white oil. Sometimes it was a pathological cause such as 'Hardenings' [indurations or contractures] that prevented suppleness of the limbs. In such cases the 'Hardenings' had first to be removed:

SALVE TO MAKE SUPPLE THE HARDENINGS
Hog's fat
Oil of worms
Oil of the abxersu-animal [unidentified animal]
Mouse oil
Cat's oil
Gather all into one and anoint therewith.

ANOTHER [SALVE] TO SOFTEN THE HARDENINGS
Fruit of the doum-palm
Beans
Edelkraut [noble herb; identity uncertain]
Fresh milk
Sebesten [sebesten plum / Cordia myxa]
CRUSH IT IN THE BIRD-OF-PASSAGE [i.e., using a migratory bird as mortar vessel or ritualistic instrument]! CRUSH IT IN ITS FEATHERS! Apply as a poultice.

Then once again, with that meticulous care which the Scribe displayed so often in his compilation, he rounds off the remedies with one by which the limbs 'could be protected against everything.' After mixing the scrapings from a statue [magical/ritual ingredient] with mint-of-the-mountains and adding sasa-pieces [unidentified plant or material], oil, and wax, one cooked the mixture, strained it—a not unnecessary precaution—and swallowed it over four days.

CHAPTER XII: MINOR SURGERY

Surgery, judging by the Papyrus Ebers, was not a strong point with the ancient Egyptians, and it is only when we enter the domain of Major Surgery that we find the knife and the cautery [hot-iron burning instrument] advocated. Minor Surgery, such as it is, is treated along 'bloodless' lines. A remedy 'to use against the bite of a crocodile' is hardly the sort of entry one would expect to see under the heading of Minor Surgery, but times have changed. Three thousand years ago, we are compelled to assume, bites from crocodiles were about as common as black eyes in our own day; and to carry the analogy further, both were treated in the same manner: WHAT TO DO AGAINST THE [BITE OF A CROCODILE—text continues in following segment].

entities_mentioned.name: Shadfish
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alosa species (shad) or Nile shad-type fish
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as ingredient in a topical salve, beaten with honey and yeast of sweet beer.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Common Egyptian pharmaceutical vehicle and wound-healing agent; appears in multiple salves.

entities_mentioned.name: Yeast-of-Sweet-Beer
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saccharomyces cerevisiae fermentation residue from sweet beer
entities_mentioned.notes: Fermentation product used as a base or active ingredient in salves.

entities_mentioned.name: Writing-fluid (ink)

entities_mentioned.name: Ass's dung
entities_mentioned.notes: Recurring ingredient in Egyptian salves; used here combined with yeast-of-the-opium-drink, goat's fat, lettuce, onions, beans, and white oil.

entities_mentioned.name: Lettuce

entities_mentioned.name: Beans

entities_mentioned.name: Hardenings
entities_mentioned.notes: Pathological term used to describe hard tissue masses that impair joint or limb suppleness.

entities_mentioned.name: Hog's Fat
entities_mentioned.notes: Emollient base in salve for softening hardenings.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown animal; possibly a rodent or reptile
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian term 'abxersu' (transliteration varies) has not been definitively identified with a modern species.

entities_mentioned.notes: Zoological ingredient in anti-hardening salve.

entities_mentioned.name: Cat's Oil

entities_mentioned.notes: German word ('noble herb') used in this English translation as a placeholder; the original Egyptian plant is unidentified.

entities_mentioned.name: Fresh Milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Common pharmaceutical vehicle in Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Sebesten
entities_mentioned.notes: Mucilaginous fruit used medicinally; known from Egyptian and later Islamic medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Scrapings-from-a-Statue
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stone or metal dust from a cult statue; magical ingredient
entities_mentioned.notes: Ritual/magical ingredient believed to transfer protective power of the deity represented by the statue.

entities_mentioned.name: Mint-of-the-Mountains

entities_mentioned.notes: Wild mountain mint used in a protective/tonic preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: sasa-pieces
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown plant or material; Egyptian term unresolved
entities_mentioned.notes: Unidentified ingredient cooked with oil and wax into a swallowed preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Wax
entities_mentioned.notes: Common pharmaceutical excipient in Egyptian medicine; used as a binding or coating agent.

entities_mentioned.name: Cautery
entities_mentioned.notes: Surgical instrument mentioned in the context of major surgery; not used in the minor surgery section.

OCR errors corrected include: 'Brrp-or-Passace' → 'BIRD-OF-PASSAGE', '1TS' → 'ITS', 'oné' → 'one', 'Cuapter' → 'CHAPTER', 'SurcERY' → 'Surgery'. 2. results.title_inferred: Remedies for Human Bites, Burns, and Related Wound Treatments from the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text: the hands, or rather the teeth, of a lady or gentleman friend required more intensive treatment than that demanded by the bite of a crocodile: REMEDY AGAINST THE BITE OR STING OF PEOPLE Make thou for him a poultice of Raw Flesh on the first day. Afterwards treat him with Oil and Honey in order to do him good. Then put thou Oil in Wax in order to make him completely well at once. Next, Burns. Burns are troublesome lesions at the best of times and much needless suffering is saved if one can have at hand a quick and easy remedy to apply. The Ancients recognized this, although we might not be able to see any particular merit in the preparations they employed: TO PREVENT BURN WOUNDS A Frog Warm in Oil and rub therewith. ANOTHER The Head-of-the-Electric-Eel Warm in Oil and apply to the part of the body. If one had more time there were other remedies that could be employed; for example, Elderberries and Papyrus-plant mixed in Gum-water and applied thereto. Or, if one preferred a dry remedy: ANOTHER A-portion-of-Cake Hair-of-a-Cat Crush into one and apply thereto. A more elaborate remedy, or series of remedies, is set forth for the use of those who wish to leave nothing to chance: AGAINST BURNS: TO USE ON THE FIRST DAY Black amat-juice Put thereto. ANOTHER ON THE FIRST DAY Elderberries uah-corn Cat's dung Mix into one in Cake-water and apply thereto. TO USE ON THE SECOND DAY Goat's dung Burn, crush, rub in Yeast-that-is-fermenting, and put thereto. TO USE ON THE THIRD DAY Thorns-of-Acanthus, dried Crush them in cooked durra-corn and Onions, put in Oil, and apply as a plaster. TO USE ON THE FOURTH DAY Wax Roasted Cow's Fat Palm-fibres Make into one in uah-corn, and apply as a plaster. TO USE ON THE FIFTH DAY Onions Red-lead Fruit-of-the-am-tree Crush, rub in Copper-splinters, make into one and apply as a plaster. results.translation:
The bite of a human — whether from the hands or, more precisely, the teeth of a lady or gentleman acquaintance — was considered to require more intensive treatment than even the bite of a crocodile.

REMEDY AGAINST THE BITE OR STING OF PEOPLE
Prepare for the patient a poultice of Raw Flesh on the first day. Afterwards, treat him with Oil and Honey to promote healing. Then apply Oil in Wax in order to effect a complete cure at once.

On Burns:
Burns are troublesome wounds under the best of circumstances, and much unnecessary suffering can be avoided if a quick and easy remedy is ready to hand. The ancients recognized this, though we may not readily appreciate the virtue of the preparations they employed:

TO PREVENT BURN WOUNDS
A Frog — warm in Oil and rub [the affected area] therewith.

ANOTHER [REMEDY]
The Head of the Electric Eel — warm in Oil and apply to the affected part of the body.

If more time was available, other remedies might also be employed; for example, Elderberries and Papyrus-plant mixed in Gum-water and applied to the wound. Or, if a dry remedy was preferred:

ANOTHER [REMEDY]
A portion of Cake [and] Hair of a Cat — crush together into one [preparation] and apply thereto.

A more elaborate remedy, or series of remedies, is prescribed for those who wish to leave nothing to chance:

AGAINST BURNS — TO BE USED ON THE FIRST DAY
Black amat-juice — apply thereto.

ANOTHER [REMEDY] FOR THE FIRST DAY
Elderberries, uah-corn [a variety of grain, identity uncertain], Cat's dung — mix together in Cake-water and apply thereto.

TO BE USED ON THE SECOND DAY
Goat's dung — burn [it], crush, rub in Yeast-that-is-fermenting, and apply thereto.

TO BE USED ON THE THIRD DAY
Thorns of Acanthus, dried — crush them together with cooked durra-corn [sorghum] and Onions; combine with Oil and apply as a plaster.

TO BE USED ON THE FOURTH DAY
Wax, Roasted Cow's Fat, Palm-fibres — combine in uah-corn and apply as a plaster.

TO BE USED ON THE FIFTH DAY
Onions, Red-lead [minium], Fruit of the am-tree — crush together, rub in Copper-splinters, combine into one preparation and apply as a plaster.

It is to the credit of these ancient practitioners that they provided for emergencies, and in the unlikely event of [text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: Raw Flesh
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a poultice for human bites on the first day of treatment; a common ancient Egyptian wound dressing.

entities_mentioned.name: Oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vegetable oil, possibly castor or sesame oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a base for numerous topical remedies throughout the Ebers Papyrus.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-attested antimicrobial agent in ancient Egyptian medicine; used here for wound healing after human bites.

entities_mentioned.name: Wax
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle for Oil in wound treatment, and as a base in the fourth-day burn plaster.

entities_mentioned.name: Frog
entities_mentioned.notes: Warmed in oil and rubbed on burns; the precise species and rationale are unclear.

entities_mentioned.name: Head-of-the-Electric-Eel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Malapterurus electricus (African electric catfish) or a similar Nile species
entities_mentioned.notes: Warmed in oil and applied to burns; the Egyptian original likely refers to a specific Nile fish known for its electrical properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Elderberries
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sambucus nigra or a related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in multiple burn remedies; identification as elderberry is the translator's inference and may reflect the Egyptian original's plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Papyrus-plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Mixed with elderberries in gum-water and applied to burns.

entities_mentioned.name: Gum-water
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aqueous solution of plant gum, possibly acacia gum
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a solvent/vehicle for topical applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Hair-of-a-Cat
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a dry remedy for burns, crushed with a portion of cake.

entities_mentioned.name: Black amat-juice
entities_mentioned.notes: The plant 'amat' has not been conclusively identified; the black juice may be a resinous or berry-derived extract.

entities_mentioned.name: uah-corn
entities_mentioned.notes: An unidentified Egyptian grain or cereal product used as a vehicle in multiple remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Cat's dung
entities_mentioned.notes: Mixed with elderberries and uah-corn in cake-water for first-day burn treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: Goat's dung
entities_mentioned.notes: Burned, crushed, and combined with fermenting yeast for second-day burn treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: Yeast-that-is-fermenting
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Active yeast, likely from beer fermentation
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle in the second-day burn remedy; may have had mild antiseptic properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Thorns-of-Acanthus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acanthus species, possibly Acanthus mollis or a local thorny shrub
entities_mentioned.notes: Dried and crushed with durra-corn and onions in oil for third-day burn plaster.

entities_mentioned.name: Durra-corn
entities_mentioned.notes: Cooked and used as a base in the third-day burn plaster.

entities_mentioned.name: Onions
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in both the third-day and fifth-day burn plasters; known for mild antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Roasted Cow's Fat
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an emollient base in the fourth-day burn plaster.

entities_mentioned.name: Palm-fibres
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a structural component in the fourth-day burn plaster.

entities_mentioned.name: Red-lead
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in the fifth-day burn plaster; highly toxic by modern standards, but used in ancient medicine for its drying and astringent properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Fruit-of-the-am-tree
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'am-tree' has not been conclusively identified; possibly a local Egyptian fruit-bearing tree.

entities_mentioned.name: Copper-splinters
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an astringent/antiseptic in the fifth-day burn plaster; copper compounds have known antimicrobial properties.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from a modern English paraphrase and commentary on the Ebers Papyrus (ca. 1550 BCE) rather than a direct translation from the Egyptian original. The source text is likely from Cyril P. Bryan's 1930 translation with editorial commentary. Several ingredient names (amat, uah-corn, am-tree) are transliterations or approximations of Egyptian terms that remain unidentified in the botanical or pharmacological literature. The multi-day treatment regimen for burns is a notable feature, suggesting structured wound management protocols. The use of Red-lead (minium) and Copper-splinters, while medically rationale-adjacent in antiquity, would be considered hazardous by modern standards. The passage ends mid-sentence, indicating continuation in the next chunk.

results.title_inferred: Minor Surgery: Treatments for Burns and Open Wounds from the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text: from-the-Shore, Crocodile-incense, Fat-of-the-Egyptian-Goat, Onions, and Wax could be used. Or, again, Stone-from-the-Parting-of-the-Waters could be used instead of Stone-from-the-Shore in the last-mentioned prescription. If the burn still persisted in its downward course and began to 'turn white,' one is advised to use a remedy that, applied earlier in the day, one cannot help remarking, would have saved a deal of trouble; namely, a Linen-shirt steeped in Oil. Another simple remedy, although not so simple nor so effective as the foregoing must have proved, is, however, backed up by an unsolicited testimonial that is quite convincing in its brevity: ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE AWAY THE TURNING-WHITE OF A BURN Durra-Bread-in-Oil-and-Salt Mix into one and apply as a plaster in order that he may become well at once. IT IS QUITE TRUE. I HAVE SEEN IT. IT HAS OFTEN HAPPENED TO ME. But, as every medical man knows, Burns can be stubborn affairs. Hence it is not surprising that in spite of the above unsolicited testimonial the Ancients found some burns beyond the ingenuity of mortal man, and were forced to invoke the aid of the gods to reinforce the therapeutic effect of the remedies: INCANTATION FOR A BURN 'O son, Horus! There is Fire in the Land! Water is not there and thou art not there! Bring Water over the River-bank to quench the Fire.' To be spoken over Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-Borne-a-Son. ANOTHER INCANTATION FOR A BURN 'O thou son of god, Horus! There is Fire in the Land! Though there be water there or not: now, the Water is in thy Mouth, the Nile is at thy Feet when thou comest to quench the Fire.' To be spoken over Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-Borne-a-Son. Cake Ram's Hair Apply to the Burn. The victim of a Burn had little to thank the Healing Profession for, it will be admitted; but the person unfortunate enough to contract an open wound had still less. One remedy directs that Cow's Fat or Cow's Flesh be placed on the wound in order [to heal it].
results.translation: From the shore [mineral/stone ingredient], Crocodile-incense, Fat of the Egyptian Goat, Onions, and Wax could be used [in treatment of burns]. Alternatively, Stone-from-the-Parting-of-the-Waters could be substituted for Stone-from-the-Shore in the aforementioned prescription. If the burn continued to progress downward and began to 'turn white' [i.e., show signs of necrosis or eschar formation], the recommended remedy was a linen shirt steeped in oil — a treatment which, the author observes, would have saved considerable trouble had it been applied earlier. Another simple remedy — though not as simple nor as effective as the foregoing — is nonetheless supported by an unsolicited testimonial remarkable for its brevity: ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE AWAY THE WHITENING OF A BURN: Durra [sorghum] bread in oil and salt. Mix together and apply as a plaster, so that he may recover at once. IT IS QUITE TRUE. I HAVE SEEN IT. IT HAS OFTEN HAPPENED TO ME. But, as every medical man knows, burns can be stubborn. It is therefore unsurprising that, despite the above testimonial, the Ancients sometimes found burns beyond mortal remedy and were compelled to invoke divine assistance to reinforce the therapeutic effect: INCANTATION FOR A BURN: 'O son, Horus! There is Fire in the Land! Water is not there and thou art not there! Bring Water over the River-bank to quench the Fire.' To be spoken over the milk of a woman who has borne a son. ANOTHER INCANTATION FOR A BURN: 'O thou son of god, Horus! There is Fire in the Land! Though there be water there or not: now, the Water is in thy Mouth, the Nile is at thy Feet when thou comest to quench the Fire.' To be spoken over the milk of a woman who has borne a son. [Also:] Ram's hair, formed into a cake, to be applied to the burn. The victim of a burn had little cause to thank the healing profession, it must be admitted; but the person unfortunate enough to suffer an open wound fared even worse. One remedy directs that cow's fat or cow's flesh be placed upon the wound [to promote healing].

entities_mentioned.name: Stone-from-the-Shore
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian mineral ingredient of uncertain botanical or mineralogical identity; possibly natron or a riverine stone.

entities_mentioned.name: Crocodile-incense
entities_mentioned.notes: May refer to a resinous substance associated with crocodile habitat or crocodile-derived material; identity uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Fat-of-the-Egyptian-Goat
entities_mentioned.notes: Animal fat used as an emollient base in topical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Onions
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a topical ingredient in burn treatment; known antimicrobial properties are consistent with traditional use.

entities_mentioned.name: Wax
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely beeswax used as a binding or occlusive agent in topical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Stone-from-the-Parting-of-the-Waters
entities_mentioned.notes: An Egyptian mineral ingredient; possibly a specific type of riverine or lacustrine stone; identity unresolved.

entities_mentioned.name: Linen-shirt steeped in Oil
entities_mentioned.notes: A simple occlusive wound dressing; functionally analogous to modern oil-impregnated gauze for burns.

entities_mentioned.name: Durra-Bread-in-Oil-and-Salt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sorghum bicolor bread with oil and salt
entities_mentioned.notes: Durra is a variety of sorghum or millet; the preparation is a poultice/plaster for burn whitening (eschar).

entities_mentioned.name: Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-Borne-a-Son
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the vehicle over which a ritual incantation to Horus is spoken; applied to burns. Human breast milk has known anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Ram's Hair (caked)
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied directly to burns; may have served as a mechanical dressing or escharotic agent.

entities_mentioned.name: Cow's Fat
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a topical treatment placed on open wounds.

entities_mentioned.name: Cow's Flesh
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied directly to open wounds; possibly intended as a biological dressing or to supply moisture and nutrients.

results.translator_notes: This segment is taken from a modern English paraphrase/translation of the Ebers Papyrus (likely the Cyril Bryan or similar early 20th-century edition) rather than from the original hieratic Egyptian. The source text is thus already one remove from the primary source. The phrase 'turning white' of a burn likely refers to the appearance of necrotic or blanched tissue (eschar). The first-person testimonial ('IT IS QUITE TRUE. I HAVE SEEN IT. IT HAS OFTEN HAPPENED TO ME.') is a rare example of a personal attestation embedded within ancient Egyptian medical texts, and is preserved verbatim as it appears in the papyrus. The incantations to Horus reflect the integration of magico-religious practice with empirical therapeutics in ancient Egyptian medicine. The segment ends mid-sentence ('placed on the wound in o—'), indicating truncation at the chunk boundary.

results.title_inferred: Minor Surgery: Remedies for Splinters, Abscesses, and Ulcers from the Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text: complain of the slowness of medical progress, let him be thankful that in the extraction of splinters, to mention another department, — MINOR SURGERY we have advanced since the days of Tut-Ankh-Amen. It does not seem much to boast of, and yet.... WHAT TO DO TO DRAW OUT SPLINTERS IN THE FLESH The per-baibait-bird-with-Honey Apply thereto. ANOTHER Worms' blood, cook and crush in Oil; Mole, kill, cook, and drain in Oil; Ass's dung, mix in Fresh Milk Apply to the opening. ANOTHER Male-and-Female-Semen Apply thereto. ANOTHER Skull-of-the-Shadfish, cooked in Oil Apply to the point of the splinter. Thereby it comes out. ANOTHER Incense Dough Sea-salt Wasp's dung Fat Red-lead Wax Apply thereto. It draws the matter thereout. It is unfortunate that the Scribe did not think it worth while to describe the 'uases' abscess so that we might make an effort to identify it. That it was a stubborn eruption, the remedies show. One that cures the abscess by 'making the blood fall' is composed of Man's Milk, Granite, Tortoise-shell, and Trash-from-the-Knifestone. Just as picturesque, and assuredly as useless, is the second remedy: ANOTHER REMEDY AGAINST THE UASES ABSCESS Blood-of-a-Dove Blood-of-a-Goose Blood-of-a-Swallow Blood-of-a-Vulture Anoint therewith. 'Stinking ulcers'—their number must have been legion—were dealt with at length, but why the tag of advice was attached only to the following remedy is somewhat puzzling. It could with advantage have been appended to many others in the Papyrus: ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT A STINKING ULCER IN THE BODY OF A MAN OR WOMAN Egg-of-an-Ostrich Tortoise-shell Thorns-of-the-am-tree Warm and anoint therewith. But DON'T GET TIRED DOING IT! To dry a wound it was plastered with Incense, Onions, and Cow Fat; or if the only animal available was a goat, then some Goat's Fat was beaten up with Onions and Wax and used as a poultice. For those who had any objection to Cows or Goats there remained the H[orse/other animal — ILLEGIBLE: text truncated]
results.translation:
Those who complain of the slowness of medical progress should be thankful that in the extraction of splinters — to mention one department of minor surgery — we have advanced since the days of Tutankhamun. It does not seem much to boast of, and yet…

WHAT TO DO TO DRAW OUT SPLINTERS FROM THE FLESH
[Remedy 1:] The per-baibait bird [an unidentified Egyptian bird] with Honey. Apply thereto.

[Remedy 2:] Worms' blood, cooked and crushed in Oil; Mole, killed, cooked, and drained in Oil; Ass's dung, mixed in Fresh Milk. Apply to the opening.

[Remedy 3:] Male-and-Female-Semen [i.e., combined human semen]. Apply thereto.

[Remedy 4:] Skull of the Shadfish [a species of fish], cooked in Oil. Apply to the point of the splinter. Thereby it comes out.

[Remedy 5:] Incense, Dough, Sea-salt, Wasp's dung, Fat, Red-lead, Wax. Apply thereto. It draws the matter out.

It is unfortunate that the Scribe did not think it worthwhile to describe the 'uases' [Egyptian term of uncertain meaning] abscess, so that we might make an effort to identify it. That it was a stubborn eruption the remedies make clear. One remedy that cures the abscess by 'making the blood fall' is composed of Man's Milk, Granite, Tortoise-shell, and Trash-from-the-Knifestone [grinding residue from a whetstone]. Equally picturesque, and assuredly as useless, is the second remedy:

ANOTHER REMEDY AGAINST THE UASES ABSCESS
Blood of a Dove, Blood of a Goose, Blood of a Swallow, Blood of a Vulture. Anoint therewith.

'Stinking ulcers' — their number must have been legion — were dealt with at length, but why the following note of practical advice was attached only to the remedy below is somewhat puzzling. It could with advantage have been appended to many others in the Papyrus:

ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT A STINKING ULCER IN THE BODY OF A MAN OR WOMAN
Egg of an Ostrich, Tortoise-shell, Thorns of the am-tree [unidentified Egyptian tree species]. Warm and anoint therewith. But DO NOT GROW WEARY DOING IT!

To dry a wound it was plastered with Incense, Onions, and Cow Fat; or, if the only animal available was a goat, then Goat's Fat was beaten up with Onions and Wax and used as a poultice. For those who had any objection to Cows or Goats, there remained the H[— ILLEGIBLE: text truncated]

entities_mentioned.name: per-baibait bird
entities_mentioned.notes: An unidentified Egyptian bird name used as a materia medica ingredient. Possibly a wading or water bird; no secure modern equivalent.

entities_mentioned.name: Worms' blood
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Earthworm hemolymph or crushed worm fluid
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely refers to fluid extracted from earthworms or similar annelids. Used cooked and crushed in oil.

entities_mentioned.name: Mole
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Talpa europaea or related mole species
entities_mentioned.notes: Killed, cooked, and drained in oil; applied as a drawing agent for splinters.

entities_mentioned.name: Ass's dung
entities_mentioned.notes: Mixed in fresh milk and applied to wound opening. A common dung-based remedy in ancient Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Skull of the Shadfish
entities_mentioned.notes: Cooked in oil and applied to a splinter; intended as a drawing agent. The exact species is uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Red-lead
entities_mentioned.notes: A mineral pigment with known toxicity; used here in a topical drawing plaster.

entities_mentioned.name: Wasp's dung
entities_mentioned.notes: Possibly the residue or frass found in wasp nests; used topically.

entities_mentioned.name: Uases abscess
entities_mentioned.notes: An Egyptian medical term for a type of stubborn skin eruption or abscess; precise clinical identity unknown.

entities_mentioned.name: Man's Milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in remedy for 'uases' abscess to 'make the blood fall'; human breast milk was a common ingredient in ancient Egyptian pharmacy.

entities_mentioned.name: Trash-from-the-Knifestone
entities_mentioned.notes: The fine powder or debris produced when sharpening a knife on a whetstone.

entities_mentioned.name: Egg of an Ostrich
entities_mentioned.notes: Used warmed and applied as an ointment base for stinking ulcers.

entities_mentioned.name: Tortoise-shell
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Testudo spp. or related tortoise carapace
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in multiple remedies including for the uases abscess and for stinking ulcers; likely powdered or burnt.

entities_mentioned.name: Thorns of the am-tree
entities_mentioned.notes: An unidentified Egyptian tree referred to as 'am'; thorns used in a topical application for stinking ulcers.

entities_mentioned.name: Incense
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly frankincense (Boswellia sacra) or a resin blend
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both in the splinter-drawing plaster and in the wound-drying preparation with onions and cow fat.

entities_mentioned.name: Onions
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in wound-drying preparations, both with cow fat and goat fat and wax.

entities_mentioned.name: Cow Fat
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a topical vehicle for wound-drying plaster with incense and onions.

entities_mentioned.name: Goat's Fat
entities_mentioned.notes: Alternative to cow fat; beaten with onions and wax to form a poultice.

results.translator_notes: This segment is an English-language secondary source (a modern scholarly commentary and translation of the Papyrus Ebers), not a direct translation of the ancient Egyptian. The text presented here is already in English and appears to derive from Cyril P. Bryan's 1930 translation/commentary 'The Papyrus Ebers.' The segment ends abruptly mid-sentence ('there remained the H'), indicating truncation at chunk boundary. Page numbers 73–75 from the original publication are present as inline artifacts and have been removed from the cleaned text. The note 'BUT DON'T GET TIRED DOING IT!' is a direct rendering of an actual ancient admonition preserved in the papyrus and is not editorial humor, though the surrounding commentary is the translator's own voice. OCR error 'Ege-of-an-Ostrich' silently corrected to 'Egg-of-an-Ostrich.' The term 'uases' is retained as a transliteration of an Egyptian medical term without a confident modern clinical equivalent.

results.title_inferred: Treatments for Wheals, Wound Healing, and the Urinary System (Ebers Papyrus)
results.original_text: DRIVE AWAY THE WHEALS FROM BLOWS Honey Cow's Brain Mason's Clay Linseed-water Date-juice Cook, and apply as a plaster. ANOTHER Dust-of-Alabaster Dust-of-a-Statue Granite Fresh Milk Anoint therewith. ANOTHER A portion-of-hemit, dried Crush, powder, and put in a Sugar-cake. One part thereof soaked in Honey to be eaten by the person. Skin-grafting, it may be shortly said, formed no part of the accomplishments of the medical practitioner in Ancient Egypt. Nevertheless he did his best to stimulate granulation: TO MAKE THE FLESH GROW Collyrium Cow-fat Chips-of-Verdigris Honey Crush, and apply as poultice. Curiously enough this remedy appeared to have succeeded at times; in fact to have over-succeeded on occasions, for it is followed by another remedy to check excessive granulation. Since this, however, was composed of an unguent concocted of Ostrich-egg ground down with Tortoise-shell and Thorns-of-the-am-tree, its efficacy may be seriously doubted. CHAPTER XIII THE URINARY SYSTEM The Urinary System received nothing like the detailed attention paid to the Alimentary System. But it made up for that in variety. Urinary troubles in the adult were corrected in three ways: a rectal injection of Olive Oil, Honey, Sweet Beer, Sea-salt, and Seeds-of-the-Wonderfruit was one way. Anointing the Phallus was another: TO REGULATE THE URINE Xet-plant Mix in Fresh Milk and apply to the Phallus. ANOTHER Crocus-from-the-South, Beans roasted. Put in Oil and anoint the Phallus therewith. ANOTHER Wood-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus Mix in Yeast-of-the-mesta-drink, and anoint the Phallus therewith. A third method was by means of mixtures: REMEDY TO REGULATE THE URINE A measuring-glass filled with Water-from-the-Bird-pond, Elderberry, Fibres-of-the-asit-plant, Fresh Milk, Beer-Swill, Flowers-of-the-Cucumber, Green Dates. Make into one, strain, and take for four days. The less said about the remedies for the treatment of Polyuria and frequency...
results.translation: DRIVE AWAY THE WHEALS FROM BLOWS: [Ingredients:] Honey; Cow's Brain; Mason's Clay [mortar clay]; Linseed-water; Date-juice. Cook together and apply as a plaster. ANOTHER [remedy]: Dust of Alabaster; Dust scraped from a Statue; Granite [powder]; Fresh Milk. Anoint the affected area therewith. ANOTHER [remedy]: A portion of hemit [unidentified plant or substance], dried. Crush, reduce to powder, and incorporate into a sugar-cake [confection]. One part thereof, soaked in Honey, to be eaten by the patient. Skin-grafting, it may be briefly stated, formed no part of the accomplishments of the medical practitioner in Ancient Egypt. Nevertheless he did his best to stimulate granulation tissue: TO MAKE THE FLESH GROW: [Ingredients:] Collyrium [eye-paint, i.e., kohl]; Cow-fat; Chips of Verdigris [copper acetate]; Honey. Crush together and apply as a poultice. Curiously enough, this remedy appears to have succeeded at times — indeed, to have over-succeeded on occasions — for it is followed by another remedy intended to check excessive granulation. Since this latter remedy, however, was composed of an unguent concocted of Ostrich-egg ground down with Tortoise-shell and Thorns-of-the-am-tree [unidentified], its efficacy may be seriously doubted. CHAPTER XIII: THE URINARY SYSTEM. The Urinary System received nothing like the detailed attention paid to the Alimentary System, but it made up for that in variety. Urinary troubles in the adult were corrected in three ways: a rectal injection of Olive Oil, Honey, Sweet Beer, Sea-salt, and Seeds-of-the-Wonderfruit [probably Ricinus communis, castor plant] was one way. Anointing the Phallus was another: TO REGULATE THE URINE: Xet-plant [unidentified]. Mix in Fresh Milk and apply to the Phallus. ANOTHER [remedy]: Crocus-from-the-South [possibly saffron or a southern variety of crocus]; Beans, roasted. Put in Oil and anoint the Phallus therewith. ANOTHER [remedy]: Wood of the Zizyphus-Lotus [Ziziphus lotus, jujube]. Mix in the yeast of the mesta-drink [an Egyptian fermented beverage], and anoint the Phallus therewith. A third method was by means of internal mixtures: REMEDY TO REGULATE THE URINE: A measuring-glass filled with Water-from-the-Bird-pond [pond water, possibly a specific sacred or medicinal source]; Elderberry; Fibres of the asit-plant [unidentified]; Fresh Milk; Beer-Swill [dregs of beer]; Flowers of the Cucumber; Green Dates. Combine into one preparation, strain, and take [orally] for four days. The less said about the remedies for the treatment of Polyuria [excessive urination] and urinary frequency [the text breaks off here]...

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from a modern scholarly edition/translation of the Ebers Papyrus (an ancient Egyptian medical papyrus, c. 1550 BCE), not from the original hieratic text itself. The editorial commentary (on skin-grafting, excessive granulation, and the urinary system) is part of the translator/editor's framing prose and is not a literal translation of the papyrus. The term 'hemit' is left untranslated as its Egyptian referent is unresolved. 'Collyrium' in this context refers to kohl (eye-paint), not an ophthalmic preparation, and its repurposing in a wound poultice is notable. The verdigris remedy's apparent efficacy is pharmacologically plausible due to the antimicrobial properties of copper compounds. The chapter on the urinary system is noted as incomplete — the source text breaks off mid-sentence discussing polyuria and urinary frequency. OCR artifacts corrected include 'CuaptTer' → 'Chapter', 'Tue' → 'The', and 'One-_' → 'One'. 'Mason's Clay' likely refers to the clay used in mudbrick or plastering, not a specialized mineral; modern identification as a generic clay mineral (kaolinite/illite) is reasonable.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Children's Urinary Complaints and Diseases of Women (Ebers Papyrus)
results.original_text: drink the mixture out of a cool flask. If a boy, he had to drink it out of a hennu-vessel. A quaint remedy relieved retention in a child: REMEDY FOR A CHILD FOR CLEARING OUT THE ACCUMULATION OF URINE IN HIS BODY An-old-Book-cooked-in-Oil Smear on his Body. An old Book means, of course, a discarded papyrus and in this case the virtue of the papyrus would be enhanced by the writing-fluid, both of which 'drugs' —paper and ink—are as much a part of the pharmacopoea of the day as Red-lead or Honey, or a host of others, and are frequently prescribed for many ailments. Another remedy proposed for a child who is having trouble with his urine must be noticed here. Xent-grains are made into a pill, which is given to the child in his food if he is old enough to manage it. But, if it is a suckling that is to be treated, the wet-nurse is directed to warm it in her mouth first, and then to spit it into the mouth of the babe for four days. This interpretation of the passage, however, is open to question and is opposed by several writers who have quoted the prescription in their works. Their contention is that the Scribe intended the mother to swallow the Pill, the child receiving the benefit of it in the maternal milk. CHAPTER XIV DISEASES OF WOMEN The women's section covers some four or five pages of the Papyrus and includes remedies for errors of menstruation and lactation, diseases of the breasts and genitalia, aids to abortion and parturition, and various valuable hints to nursing mothers. Two of these hints enable them to distinguish the quality of their milk: thus, if the milk be good, it smells like the pollen of uah-grain; if, on the other hand, the milk be bad, it smells like the entrails of the mehit-fish. Other hints enable them to prognosticate the fate of the new-born child: PROGNOSIS FOR A CHILD ON THE DAY IT IS BORN If it cries ni, it will live. If it cries ba, it will die. ANOTHER If it lets a loud lamentation be heard, it will die.
results.translation: The mixture was to be drunk from a cool flask. If the patient was a boy, he had to drink it from a hennu-vessel [a specific Egyptian liquid measure or vessel]. A curious remedy was prescribed for urinary retention in a child: REMEDY FOR A CHILD TO CLEAR THE ACCUMULATION OF URINE IN HIS BODY — An Old Book [discarded papyrus] cooked in Oil; smear on his Body. Here, 'An Old Book' refers to a discarded papyrus scroll, the medicinal virtue of which was considered to be enhanced by the writing fluid absorbed into it. Both 'drugs'—the paper and the ink—were as standard a part of the pharmacopoeia of the time as red lead or honey or numerous other substances, and were frequently prescribed for a variety of ailments. Another remedy for a child experiencing difficulty urinating deserves mention here. Xent-grains [unidentified grain or seed] were formed into a pill and given to the child in his food, if he was old enough to take it in that manner. However, if the patient was still a nursing infant, the wet-nurse was instructed to warm the pill in her own mouth first, and then to spit it into the mouth of the infant for four days. This interpretation of the passage is, however, disputed and has been challenged by several scholars who have cited the prescription in their works. Their argument is that the Scribe intended for the mother herself to swallow the pill, with the child receiving its benefit through the mother's milk. CHAPTER XIV — DISEASES OF WOMEN. The section devoted to women's complaints spans some four or five pages of the Papyrus and includes remedies for disorders of menstruation and lactation, diseases of the breasts and genitalia, aids to abortion and to childbirth [parturition], and various practical instructions for nursing mothers. Two of these instructions allow nursing mothers to assess the quality of their milk: if the milk is good, it smells like the pollen of uah-grain [unidentified grain]; if the milk is bad, it smells like the entrails of the mehit-fish [unidentified fish species]. Other instructions allow them to foretell the fate of a newborn child: PROGNOSIS FOR A CHILD ON THE DAY OF ITS BIRTH — If it cries 'ni,' it will live. If it cries 'ba,' it will die. ANOTHER [PROGNOSIS] — If a loud lamentation is heard from it, it will die.

entities_mentioned.name: hennu-vessel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: an ancient Egyptian liquid measure or drinking vessel
entities_mentioned.notes: The hennu was an Egyptian unit of liquid measurement; here used as a specific vessel for administering medicine to a male child.

entities_mentioned.name: An Old Book (discarded papyrus) cooked in Oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: burnt or macerated papyrus scroll with ink residue, infused in oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Used topically as a smear for urinary retention in children. The medicinal value was attributed to both the papyrus material and the carbon-based writing ink. Condition treated: urinary retention in children. Preparation: old papyrus cooked in oil, applied externally to the body.

entities_mentioned.name: Xent-grains
entities_mentioned.notes: An unidentified grain or seed formed into a pill for administration to children with urinary difficulty. Given in food for older children; pre-warmed in the mouth of the wet-nurse and administered orally for nursing infants, or alternatively swallowed by the mother to pass benefit through breast milk. Condition treated: urinary difficulty in infants and children. Preparation: compressed into pill form.

entities_mentioned.name: Red-lead
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a standard pharmacopoeal ingredient in the Ebers Papyrus; toxic by modern standards.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Ubiquitous ingredient in ancient Egyptian medicine; used as a vehicle and for its preservative and healing properties.

entities_mentioned.name: uah-grain
entities_mentioned.notes: An Egyptian grain whose pollen served as the olfactory standard for good-quality breast milk. Botanical identity unresolved.

entities_mentioned.name: mehit-fish
entities_mentioned.notes: An Egyptian fish species whose entrails served as the olfactory standard for spoiled or poor-quality breast milk. Species identity unresolved.

results.translator_notes: This segment is drawn from a modern English-language scholarly commentary on the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE) rather than a direct translation from the original Egyptian hieratic script; the source text is itself an interpretive paraphrase and analysis. The 'Xent-grains' pill passage reflects a genuinely disputed reading in Egyptological scholarship regarding whether the remedy was administered directly to the infant or mediated through the nursing mother's milk — this ambiguity is preserved in the translation. The neonatal prognostic cries 'ni' and 'ba' are phonetic transcriptions from the Egyptian and may carry symbolic resonance (e.g., 'ba' as the Egyptian soul concept), though the text does not elaborate on this. OCR errors were minimal and corrected silently (e.g., 'CuHapTer' → 'CHAPTER').

results.title_inferred: Diseases of Women: Gynecological Remedies, Obstetric Treatments, and Breast Ailments from the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text: failed, a douche composed of Fennel, Wonderfruit, Honey and Sweet Beer was substituted and persisted in for four days. To protect a virgin from Leucorrhoea it was necessary to anoint her breast, body, and all the limbs with a lotion prepared by crushing the dried liver of a Swallow in Sour Milk. To produce Milk in the breast of a woman about to suckle a child, Bones-of-the-Swordfish were warmed in Oil and her backbone smeared therewith. The same result could be brought about by mixing Fragrant Bread (which had been made from Soured Durra) with the Poppy-plant, and making her eat it while she sat cross-legged. To bring about abortion, only one remedy is given: Dates, Onions, and the Fruit-of-the-Acanthus, were crushed in a vessel with Honey, sprinkled on a cloth, and applied to the Vulva. It ensured abortion either in the first, second or third period. To hurry on labour, however, there was no lack of remedies: REMEDY TO CAUSE A WOMAN TO BE DELIVERED Peppermint — Let the woman apply it to her bare posterior. ANOTHER TO CAUSE ALL AND EVERYTHING THAT IS IN THE BODY OF A WOMAN TO FALL Pot-of-a-new-hennu-vessel — Crush in Warm Oil and pour on her genitals. ANOTHER TO LOOSEN A CHILD IN THE BODY OF A WOMAN Sea-salt, Clean Grain-of-Wheat, Female Reed — Plaster the abdomen therewith. ANOTHER Fresh Salt, Honey — Strain and take for one day. ANOTHER Fennel, Incense, Garlic, sert-juice, Fresh Salt, Wasp's dung — Make into a ball and put in the Vagina. ANOTHER Tail-of-a-Tortoise, Shell-of-a-Beetle, sefet-oil, sert-juice, Oil — Crush into one and poultice therewith. To remedy a diseased Breast one could plaster it for four days with a compound of Calamine, Cow's Brain, and Wasp's dung; or one could invoke the goddess Isis by means of a magic formula: MAGIC FORMULA FOR THE BREAST — The Breast is the same diseased Breast of Isis who in the city of Xebt bore the gods Su and Tefnut! She has prepared for it this Magic Formula to be said over [text continues].
results.translation: Where prior treatment had failed, a douche [vaginal irrigation] composed of Fennel, Wonderfruit [unidentified Egyptian plant ingredient], Honey, and Sweet Beer was substituted and continued for four days. To protect a virgin from Leucorrhoea [vaginal discharge], it was necessary to anoint her breast, body, and all the limbs with a lotion prepared by crushing the dried liver of a Swallow in Sour Milk. To produce Milk in the breast of a woman about to nurse a child, Bones-of-the-Swordfish were warmed in Oil and her backbone smeared therewith. The same result could also be brought about by mixing Fragrant Bread (made from Soured Durra [sorghum]) with the Poppy-plant, and having her eat it while she sat cross-legged. To bring about abortion, only one remedy is recorded: Dates, Onions, and the Fruit-of-the-Acanthus were crushed together in a vessel with Honey, sprinkled on a cloth, and applied to the Vulva. This was said to ensure abortion in the first, second, or third period [trimester or stage of pregnancy]. To hasten labour, however, there was no shortage of remedies: REMEDY TO CAUSE A WOMAN TO BE DELIVERED — Peppermint: Let the woman apply it to her bare posterior. ANOTHER, TO CAUSE ALL AND EVERYTHING THAT IS IN THE BODY OF A WOMAN TO FALL — [Substance from] a Pot-of-a-new-hennu-vessel: Crush in Warm Oil and pour upon her genitals. ANOTHER, TO LOOSEN A CHILD IN THE BODY OF A WOMAN — Sea-salt, Clean Grain-of-Wheat, Female Reed: Plaster the abdomen therewith. ANOTHER — Fresh Salt, Honey: Strain and take internally for one day. ANOTHER — Fennel, Incense, Garlic, sert-juice [unidentified plant juice], Fresh Salt, Wasp's dung: Form into a suppository ball and insert into the Vagina. ANOTHER — Tail-of-a-Tortoise, Shell-of-a-Beetle, sefet-oil [unidentified oil], sert-juice, Oil: Crush all together and apply as a poultice. To treat a diseased Breast, one could plaster it for four days with a compound of Calamine [zinc carbonate mineral], Cow's Brain, and Wasp's dung; or alternatively one could invoke the goddess Isis by means of a magic formula: MAGIC FORMULA FOR THE BREAST — The Breast [in question] is the same diseased Breast of Isis who, in the city of Xebt [Coptos/Qift], bore the gods Shu and Tefnut! She has prepared for it this Magic Formula, to be spoken over [the afflicted breast — text continues].

results.translator_notes: This segment is a translation/paraphrase (by Bryan, Ebbell, or similar early 20th-century Egyptologist) of portions of the Ebers Papyrus (ca. 1550 BCE) dealing with women's diseases. Several substance names — 'sert-juice,' 'sefet-oil,' 'Wonderfruit,' and 'hennu-vessel contents' — are transliterations or approximations of Egyptian terms whose botanical or chemical identity remains uncertain. 'Leucorrhoea' is a Victorian medical spelling (modern: leucorrhea). The 'Fragrant Bread' is a translator's interpretive gloss. The mythological identifications of Shu and Tefnut as children of Isis 'in Xebt' may reflect a local variant of the Heliopolitan cosmogony. The segment text appears to originate from an early 20th-century English paraphrase of the papyrus rather than a direct hieratic-to-English translation, hence some period medical vocabulary is retained in the translation.

results.title_inferred: Gynecological Remedies: Vaginal Douches, Uterine Displacement, and Menstrual Disorders (Ebers Papyrus)
results.original_text:
dryness of the Eyes which ensues. SPEAK OVER AAT-PLANT, OVER HEALTH-GRAINS, OVER THE-FRUITFUL-PART-OF-REEDS, OVER HAIR-OF-THE-HEAD-OF-THE-ABT-PLANT. LET IT POUR OUT ON THE LEFT SIDE. MAKE SEVEN PORTIONS AND GIVE THEM. The Vulva could be protected against the entry of disease by injecting a douche in which Garlic and Horn-of-a-Cow figure as ingredients. Should this prophylactic fail and inflammation ensue, the douche was changed to one of Bile-of-the-Cow, Cassia, and Oil. If the disease progressed and corrosion occurred, the Cow was discarded for the Hog, and a douche of Fresh Dates and Hog's Bile administered. If, in spite of these vigorous measures, pustules appear in the vagina, recourse is now had, as so often before, to the Ass, and the douche is composed of Fresh Dates and Ass's Milk.

REMEDY TO ALLOW THE WOMB OF A WOMAN TO SLIP INTO ITS PLACE
The-Film-of-Dampness-which-is-found-on-the-Wood-of-Ships (mould): Rub in Yeast-of-Fermented-Beer and let her drink it.

ANOTHER: Put an Ibis-of-Wax on the coals and allow the fumes thereof to penetrate into her sex-organs.

ANOTHER: Dried Human excrement — Put in Incense; the woman bends herself over the same and lets the fumes thereof penetrate into her sex-organs.

ANOTHER: Oil-of-the-Earth (? Petroleum) with peddu (manure) — Keep in Honey and rub the body of a woman therewith.

ANOTHER: Dried Excrement, Froth-of-Beer — Rub the finger of the woman therewith and put it to all her limbs against her sufferings. (Joachim suggests that she was to smear the prolapsed uterus.)

WHEN THOU EXAMINEST A WOMAN WHO HAS LIVED many years without her Menstruation having appeared; she vomits something like foam and her body is as though a fire were under it, but she recovers after the vomiting; then say thou to her: This is a rising of Blood to
results.translation:
...dryness of the eyes which follows. [Ritual incantation:] SPEAK OVER THE AAT-PLANT, OVER HEALTH-GRAINS [kyllestis-grain], OVER THE FRUITFUL PART OF REEDS, OVER THE HAIR OF THE HEAD OF THE ABT-PLANT. LET IT POUR OUT ON THE LEFT SIDE. MAKE SEVEN PORTIONS AND GIVE THEM.

The vulva could be protected against the entry of disease by injecting a douche [vaginal irrigation] in which garlic and horn-of-a-cow figured as ingredients. Should this prophylactic fail and inflammation ensue, the douche was changed to one of bile-of-the-cow, cassia, and oil. If the disease progressed and corrosion [ulceration] occurred, the cow was discarded in favour of the hog, and a douche of fresh dates and hog's bile was administered. If, in spite of these vigorous measures, pustules appeared in the vagina, recourse was had, as so often elsewhere, to the ass, and the douche was composed of fresh dates and ass's milk.

REMEDY TO CAUSE THE WOMB OF A WOMAN TO RETURN TO ITS PROPER PLACE:
The film of moisture found on the timber of ships [ship's mould / fungal growth]: rub together with yeast of fermented beer and let her drink it.

ANOTHER: Place a wax figure of an ibis upon the coals and allow the fumes thereof to penetrate into her genital organs.

ANOTHER: Dried human excrement — place in incense [upon burning coals]; the woman bends over it and allows the fumes thereof to penetrate into her genital organs.

ANOTHER: Oil-of-the-earth [? petroleum / bitumen] with peddu [manure] — keep in honey and rub the body of the woman therewith.

ANOTHER: Dried excrement, froth of beer — rub onto the finger of the woman and apply it to all her limbs against her suffering. (Joachim interprets this as instruction to smear the prolapsed uterus directly.)

WHEN THOU EXAMINEST A WOMAN WHO HAS LIVED MANY YEARS WITHOUT HER MENSTRUATION HAVING APPEARED; she vomits something resembling foam and her body is as though a fire burned beneath it, but she recovers after the vomiting; then say thou to her: This is a rising of blood to [the head — text continues].

results.translator_notes: This chunk is a translated/paraphrased English rendering of the Ebers Papyrus gynecological section, not a raw hieratic or hieroglyphic transcription; the source appears to be a published English edition (likely Cyril Bryan's 1930 translation). The ritual incantation at the opening (AAT-plant, ABT-plant, etc.) is fragmentary and the segment is mid-passage. The medical practices described include: (1) vaginal douching for prophylaxis and progressive vaginal disease using animal-derived ingredients escalating from cow to hog to ass; (2) multiple remedies for uterine prolapse employing fumigation, oral ingestion, and topical application; (3) a clinical observation on amenorrhea with symptoms suggestive of possible menopause or another systemic condition, which the text begins to diagnose as 'a rising of blood' — the passage cuts off at this point. Joachim's interpretive note on the final excrement-and-froth remedy is preserved from the source text. 'Peddu' is an untranslated Egyptian term retained in the original English rendering. The identification of 'Oil-of-the-Earth' as petroleum is the translator's own uncertain gloss, plausible given known ancient Near Eastern use of bituminous substances.

results.title_inferred: Ancient Egyptian Skin Remedies: Itch, Scurf, and Poultices from the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text: A mixture composed of Onion, crushed in Honey and taken in Beer, was prescribed. If the Itch was confined to the Neck, a Chopped-up Bat applied to it as a poultice healed it 'at once.' For the Thigh: REMEDY FOR THE THIGH — Onions, Dried Beans, Red Natron, Sea-salt, Sour Milk — Make into a poultice. IT HEALS AT ONCE. If the itching spread to all the limbs and became generalized, various remedies were available. Among them a Poultice of Corn, Natron, Clay-from-the-Gate, Onions, Incense, and Refuse-of-Dates. This was calculated to soothe it, but there was also a certain sure remedy for it: ANOTHER TO ALLAY ITCHING — Cyperus-from-the-Meadow, Onion-meal, Incense, Wild Date-juice — Make into one and apply to the scurvy place. LOOK TO IT BECAUSE THIS IS THE TRUE REMEDY. IT WAS FOUND AMONG THE PROVEN REMEDIES IN THE TEMPLE OF THE GOD, OSIRIS. IT IS A REMEDY WHICH DRIVES AWAY THE SCURF IN EVERY LIMB OF A PERSON. YES, IT HEALS AT ONCE. YOU SEE. If the itching were accompanied by 'blood corrosion' the trouble became involved, and mild or simple remedies were out of the question. A concoction of Elderberries, Linseed, Wormwood, Natron, and various native plants, rubbed in Semen-of-a-Man, Yeast-of-Wine, and Juice-of-the-Wild-Date, was warmed and applied as a poultice. In the event of this and similar remedies not bringing relief, rectal injections were employed. One such enema consisted of Fresh Milk and Sea-salt; another of Fresh Milk, Sea-salt, and Incense; another of Urine, Onions, and Oil; and still another of Honey, Fresh Milk, Olive Oil, Copper-rust, and Collyrium. This line of treatment is that known as counter-irritation and persists to this day, though not in this form, nor with these (nor similar) remedies, nor indeed for this particular disease. For common scurf the ketket-plant and the Berry-of-the-uan-tree were ground into one with the Milk-of-a-Woman, strained, and drunk for four days. A simpler but hardly less pleasant [text breaks off].
results.translation: A mixture composed of onion crushed in honey and taken in beer was prescribed [for itch]. If the itch was confined to the neck, a chopped-up bat applied as a poultice was said to heal it 'at once.' For the thigh, the prescribed remedy consisted of onions, dried beans, red natron [sodium carbonate/bicarbonate], sea-salt, and sour milk, made into a poultice — declared to heal at once. If the itching spread to all the limbs and became generalized, several remedies were available, among them a poultice of corn, natron, clay-from-the-gate [a locally sourced clay], onions, incense, and refuse-of-dates [date-palm waste]. This was calculated to soothe the condition. There was also a certain and sure remedy: another preparation to allay itching, composed of cyperus-from-the-meadow [a sedge, possibly Cyperus esculentus or related species], onion-meal, incense, and wild date-juice, to be combined and applied to the affected [scurfy] place. The text declares emphatically: 'Look to it, because this is the true remedy. It was found among the proven remedies in the Temple of the God Osiris. It is a remedy which drives away the scurf in every limb of a person. Yes, it heals at once.' If the itching were accompanied by 'blood corrosion' [a term likely indicating an inflammatory or ulcerative condition], the case became more complex and simple remedies were insufficient. A concoction of elderberries, linseed, wormwood, natron, and various native plants, mixed with human semen, yeast-of-wine, and juice-of-the-wild-date, was warmed and applied as a poultice. Should this and similar remedies fail to bring relief, rectal injections [enemas] were employed. One such enema consisted of fresh milk and sea-salt; another of fresh milk, sea-salt, and incense; another of urine, onions, and oil; yet another of honey, fresh milk, olive oil, copper-rust [verdigris], and collyrium [an eye/topical preparation, here used internally]. This therapeutic approach corresponds to what is known as counter-irritation and continues in principle to the present day, though not in this form, nor with these or similar remedies, nor for this particular disease. For common scurf, the ketket-plant and the berry-of-the-uan-tree were ground together with the milk of a woman, strained, and drunk for four days. A simpler but hardly less [pleasant — text breaks off].
