results.translator_notes: This segment is an English-language secondary source (a modern scholarly translation/commentary of the Ebers Papyrus, likely by Cyril P. Bryan or a similar early 20th-century Egyptologist) rather than a direct primary-language source. The text has been lightly cleaned of OCR artifacts (e.g., 'OsrRIs' corrected to 'Osiris', 'Gop' to 'God', 'Ir' to 'It', 'sore-' joined words resolved). The word 'corn' almost certainly refers to a grain such as emmer wheat or barley, not Zea mays, which was unknown in ancient Egypt. The identification of 'elderberries' is the translator's inference and should be treated with caution. 'Blood corrosion' is a literal rendering of an Egyptian pathological term whose exact clinical meaning is uncertain — possibly referring to an erythematous, hemorrhagic, or ulcerative skin condition. The rhetorical inscription attributing the remedy to the Temple of Osiris reflects a common authenticating formula in Egyptian medical papyri. The segment ends mid-sentence, indicating the chunk cuts off before the complete passage.

results.title_inferred: Treatment of Scalp Conditions and Diseases of the Eyes (Ebers Papyrus)
results.original_text: On the second day the head was anointed with Fish-oil. On the third day with Hippopotamus Oil. On the fourth day with abra-oil. After this course of intensive treatment the offending head, if the Eczema still persisted, was smeared daily with Bread-meal and dressed with 'Rotted Cereals.' CHAPTER XVI DISEASES OF THE EYES Nine pages of the original papyrus are devoted to eye-conditions, a fact which need occasion no surprise to anyone acquainted with that country even after the thousands of years that separate us from that far-distant period. In a separate volume published in 1889 Ebers gave a detailed translation of this interesting chapter, differentiating the following conditions: Blepharitis, Blindness, Carcinoma, Cataract, Chalazion, Chemosis, Ectropion, Entropion, Granulation, Hemorrhage, Hydrophthalmus, Inflammation, Iritis, Leucoma, Ophthalmoplegia, Pinguecula, Pterygium, Staphyloma, Trichiasis. To improve the sight a 'lotion' prepared by warming Chips-of-a-new-hennu-pot in Fresh Milk was recommended. Another lotion could be prepared by combining Cream with the Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son. Apply to the eyes frequently, is added. A number of salves are also given in each of which Collyrium figures. Thus in one the Collyrium is simply combined with Honey; in another with Honey and Sap-from-Fresh-Onions; in another with the Marrow-of-an-Ox; in another with Goose-grease and Water; in another with Incense and Real Lapis lazuli; in another with Antimony, Copper-vitriol, Writing-fluid, and Onions; and in still another with Writing-fluid, Myrrh, Opal-resin, Arabian-wood-powder, and Saltpetre-from-Upper-Egypt. For Bleary Eyes one beat up Myrrh, Onions, Verdigris, and Cyperus-from-the-North, with Antilope-dung, Clear Oil, and Entrails-of-the-qadit-animal. This could be painted on with a Vulture's feather. Fortunately for Bleary-eyed patients of that day, and for those whose means did not run to Antilopes and Vultures (not to mention qadit animals)...
results.translation:
On the second day the head was anointed with Fish-oil. On the third day with Hippopotamus Oil. On the fourth day with abra-oil [an unidentified oil of Egyptian origin]. After this course of intensive treatment, if the Eczema still persisted, the affected scalp was smeared daily with Bread-meal and dressed with 'Rotted Cereals.'

CHAPTER XVI: DISEASES OF THE EYES

Nine pages of the original papyrus are devoted to eye conditions, a fact which should surprise no one acquainted with that country, even across the thousands of years separating us from that remote period. In a separate volume published in 1889, Ebers provided a detailed translation of this interesting chapter, distinguishing the following conditions: Blepharitis [inflammation of the eyelids], Blindness, Carcinoma, Cataract, Chalazion [eyelid cyst], Chemosis [conjunctival edema], Ectropion [outward-turning eyelid], Entropion [inward-turning eyelid], Granulation [trachoma-type granulation tissue], Hemorrhage, Hydrophthalmus [abnormal enlargement of the eye], Inflammation, Iritis [inflammation of the iris], Leucoma [corneal opacity], Ophthalmoplegia [paralysis of eye muscles], Pinguecula [conjunctival deposit], Pterygium [conjunctival overgrowth], Staphyloma [abnormal protrusion of uvea], and Trichiasis [inward-turning eyelashes].

To improve the sight, a lotion prepared by warming chips of a new hennu-pot [a type of ceramic vessel] in Fresh Milk was recommended. Another lotion could be prepared by combining Cream with the Milk of a Woman who has Borne a Son. The instruction adds: apply to the eyes frequently.

A number of salves are also given, in each of which Collyrium [a medicinal eye-wash or eye-salve] figures as an ingredient. In one preparation, Collyrium is simply combined with Honey; in another, with Honey and Sap from Fresh Onions; in another, with the Marrow of an Ox; in another, with Goose-grease and Water; in another, with Incense and Real Lapis lazuli; in another, with Antimony, Copper-vitriol [copper sulfate], Writing-fluid [ink], and Onions; and in yet another, with Writing-fluid, Myrrh, Opal-resin, Arabian-wood-powder, and Saltpetre from Upper Egypt [natron or a related salt].

For Bleary Eyes, one was to beat together Myrrh, Onions, Verdigris [copper acetate patina], and Cyperus from the North, along with Antelope-dung, Clear Oil, and the Entrails of the qadit-animal [unidentified animal]. This compound could be applied with a Vulture's feather used as a brush. Fortunately for bleary-eyed patients of that era, and for those whose means did not extend to Antelopes and Vultures — not to mention qadit animals...

entities_mentioned.name: Fish-oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a scalp anointing agent for eczema treatment; species of fish unspecified.

entities_mentioned.name: Hippopotamus Oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Topical application to scalp for eczema; use of large-animal fat common in ancient Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: abra-oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Unidentified oil; may be a transliteration of an Egyptian term. Condition treated: scalp eczema.

entities_mentioned.name: Bread-meal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ground cereal/flour (likely emmer wheat, Triticum dicoccum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied as a paste to scalp for persistent eczema.

entities_mentioned.name: Rotted Cereals
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a dressing for scalp eczema; may have had mild antimicrobial properties due to fermentation.

entities_mentioned.name: Collyrium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Medicated eye-wash or eye-salve (kohl-based, likely containing galena/lead sulfide)
entities_mentioned.notes: Base ingredient appearing in multiple ophthalmic preparations. The ancient Egyptian collyrium was frequently galena (lead sulfide), known as kohl.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with Collyrium as an ophthalmic salve; known antibacterial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Sap-from-Fresh-Onions
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in ophthalmic preparations; onion has known antimicrobial compounds.

entities_mentioned.name: Marrow-of-an-Ox
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a lipid vehicle in ophthalmic salve.

entities_mentioned.name: Goose-grease
entities_mentioned.notes: Lipid vehicle for ophthalmic salve; common in ancient Egyptian pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Incense
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely frankincense (Boswellia sacra) or related resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with lapis lazuli in ophthalmic salve.

entities_mentioned.name: Real Lapis lazuli
entities_mentioned.notes: Powdered mineral used in ophthalmic preparation; distinguished from imitation lapis.

entities_mentioned.name: Antimony
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stibnite (antimony sulfide, Sb₂S₃) or related antimony compound
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in eye salve; antimony compounds were common in ancient eye cosmetics and medicines.

entities_mentioned.name: Copper-vitriol
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in ophthalmic preparation; has astringent and antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Writing-fluid
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears in two ophthalmic recipes; likely carbon black (soot) or red ochre suspended in a gum solution.

entities_mentioned.name: Myrrh
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in ophthalmic salves and the bleary-eye compound; well-attested in ancient Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Opal-resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Possibly a translucent plant resin; exact identity unclear from context.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabian-wood-powder
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly powdered acacia wood or another aromatic Arabian timber
entities_mentioned.notes: Unidentified aromatic wood imported from Arabia; used in ophthalmic preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Saltpetre-from-Upper-Egypt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Natron (sodium carbonate/bicarbonate mixture) or potassium nitrate
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian natron is the most likely candidate; used as a preservative, antiseptic, and desiccant.

entities_mentioned.name: Verdigris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Copper acetate or basic copper carbonate
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in bleary-eye compound; has astringent properties. Modern identification: basic copper acetate [Cu(CH₃COO)₂·Cu(OH)₂].

entities_mentioned.name: Cyperus-from-the-North
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cyperus sp. (sedge), possibly Cyperus papyrus or Cyperus rotundus
entities_mentioned.notes: Sedge plant used in bleary-eye compound; geographic qualifier 'from the North' may indicate a specific variety or trade origin.

entities_mentioned.name: Antelope-dung
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fecal matter of an antelope species (likely Gazella sp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Animal dung used in ancient Egyptian medicine; possibly valued for desiccant or ritual properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Entrails-of-the-qadit-animal
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'qadit' animal is unidentified; the specific organ material used is visceral tissue.

entities_mentioned.name: Vulture's feather
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Feather of Gyps fulvus or similar vulture species used as an application brush
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an applicator for the bleary-eye compound, analogous to a modern brush or swab.

entities_mentioned.name: Chips-of-a-new-hennu-pot
entities_mentioned.notes: Ceramic material from a specific type of Egyptian vessel; the therapeutic rationale is unclear — possibly mineral leaching into milk.

entities_mentioned.name: Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son
entities_mentioned.notes: Specifically postpartum human breast milk from a mother of a male child; used as ophthalmic lotion base. The gender-specificity is a ritual/magical qualification common in ancient Egyptian medicine.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a secondary scholarly summary of the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE) rather than a direct translation of the primary hieratic text. The author — likely Georg Ebers or a commentator on his work — is synthesizing and paraphrasing the content. The eye-disease list (Chapter XVI) corresponds to Ebers Papyrus columns 54–63. The term 'Collyrium' (Latin/Greek) is used here as a translation convention for the Egyptian eye-medicine term; in the original papyrus it would be a specific Egyptian preparation, most likely galena (kohl). 'Bleary Eyes' likely corresponds to ancient descriptions of trachoma or chronic conjunctivitis, extremely common in ancient Egypt due to fly exposure and dust. The text is cut off mid-sentence at 'qadit,' indicating this is an incomplete chunk. The identification of 'Writing-fluid' as an ophthalmic ingredient is noteworthy — carbon black (lampblack/soot) had recognized antimicrobial uses. 'Saltpetre-from-Upper-Egypt' is rendered ambiguously; natron (hesmen) is the most historically attested Egyptian mineral salt of this type, though true saltpetre (KNO₃) cannot be ruled out. The 1889 Ebers volume referenced is: Ebers, G. (1889). 'Papyros Ebers: Die Maasse und das Kapitel über die Augenkrankheiten.' Leipzig.

results.title_inferred: Ancient Egyptian Remedies for Eye Diseases: Bloodshot Eyes, Xanthelasma, Film on the Eyes, and Related Conditions (Ebers Papyrus)

results.translation:
In equal parts, [the preceding preparation] often sufficed to clear up bloodshot eyes [conjunctival hyperemia]. If it did not, a more complicated poultice composed of onions, verdigris [copper acetate], collyrium [kohl/antimony-based eye cosmetic], and Arabian-wood powder, crushed to a powder with ink and water, was recommended. There was then another prescription:

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO DRIVE OUT BLOOD IN THE EYES
Two clay vessels: in one put powdered Fruit-of-the-Dompalm [doum palm fruit, Hyphaene thebaica] and Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son. In the other, Cow's Milk. Keep moist. In the morning bathe both eyes with the Fruit-of-the-Dompalm [preparation]. Next wash the eyes with the Cow's Milk four times [daily] for six days.

Xanthelasma seemingly proved a troublesome condition, for after laying down various remedies the scribe thought it advisable to inject some urgency into the treatment:

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO DRIVE OUT FAT-IN-THE-EYES
Red-lead [minium, lead tetroxide], 1 [part]
Goose-grease, 1 [part]
Smear the eyes therewith. SEE TO IT!

Another remedy approximated more closely to the modern treatment of the disease:

ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO DRIVE OUT FAT-IN-THE-EYES
Knife-stone [whetstone or obsidian scraper]
Mix in Fresh Milk and apply thereto very often.

Today it is the knife rather than a knife-stone that we apply to the eye, while the patient is instructed to drink the fresh milk.

'To him whose two eyes suffer from a flow of matter [purulent discharge/conjunctivitis],' a poultice of Clay-from-a-Statue, Leaves-of-the-Castor-oil-tree [Ricinus communis], and Honey is advised.

'To restore the lustre of the eyes,' one could apply a poultice of Onions, Resin, and Verdigris [copper acetate], crushed in Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son.

If a film [opacity] covered the eyes, the remedies were many, but two will suffice:

TO DISPEL THE FILM IN THE EYES ON THE FIRST DAY
Water-from-the-Bird-pond, 1 [part]

Whether this was drunk or merely applied to the eyes does not appear; but fortunately in the next remedy there was no room for doubt:

ANOTHER REMEDY FOR THE FILMING-OVER WHICH RISES IN THE EYE
Dried-excrement-from-the-Body-of-a-Child, 1 [part]
Honey, 1 [part]
Put in Fresh Milk and then apply to the Eyes.

Leucoma [corneal opacity/white scar on the cornea] was a complaint that set these early oculists on their mettle. They tried to coax it away with [the text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: Bloodshot Eyes
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated with equal-part preparations, and a more complex poultice of onions, verdigris, collyrium, and Arabian-wood powder with ink and water.

entities_mentioned.name: Onions
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in multiple eye preparations, including for bloodshot eyes and restoring eye lustre.

entities_mentioned.name: Verdigris
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an ophthalmic antiseptic ingredient; known antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Collyrium
entities_mentioned.notes: A traditional eye cosmetic/medicinal preparation used throughout the ancient Near East and Egypt.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabian-wood powder
entities_mentioned.notes: Identity uncertain; may refer to a resinous or aromatic wood from the Arabian peninsula.

entities_mentioned.name: Fruit-of-the-Dompalm
entities_mentioned.notes: Used powdered in a clay vessel with human breast milk for bathing bloodshot eyes.

entities_mentioned.name: Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son
entities_mentioned.notes: Specifically specified as milk from a mother who has given birth to a son; used as a vehicle in multiple eye preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Cow's Milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to wash the eyes four times daily for six days in the treatment of bloodshot eyes.

entities_mentioned.name: Xanthelasma / Fat-in-the-Eyes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Xanthelasma palpebrarum (fatty deposits around the eyes)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Egyptian term 'Fat-in-the-Eyes' is interpreted by the commentator as xanthelasma; treated with red-lead and goose-grease, or knife-stone in fresh milk.

entities_mentioned.name: Red-lead
entities_mentioned.notes: Used with goose-grease as a topical smear for xanthelasma; toxic by modern standards.

entities_mentioned.name: Goose-grease
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle/emollient with red-lead for eye application.

entities_mentioned.name: Knife-stone
entities_mentioned.notes: Mixed in fresh milk and applied to the eye; commentator notes this anticipates surgical scraping with a knife in modern treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: Clay-from-a-Statue
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a poultice with castor-oil tree leaves and honey for purulent eye discharge; may have symbolic or ritual significance.

entities_mentioned.name: Leaves-of-the-Castor-oil-tree
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a poultice for eyes suffering from a flow of matter (purulent discharge).

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in multiple preparations; known for antimicrobial properties, consistent with ophthalmic use.

entities_mentioned.name: Resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Used with onions and verdigris to restore lustre to the eyes; specific resin not identified.

entities_mentioned.name: Water-from-the-Bird-pond
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed on the first day to dispel a film over the eyes; whether ingested or applied topically is unclear from the text.

entities_mentioned.name: Dried-excrement-from-the-Body-of-a-Child
entities_mentioned.notes: Used with honey in fresh milk as a topical application for corneal opacity/film; a magico-medical ingredient common in ancient Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Film-in-the-Eyes / Filming-over
entities_mentioned.notes: The Egyptian term likely encompasses various forms of corneal clouding or opacity.

entities_mentioned.name: Leucoma
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified by the commentator as leucoma; text is interrupted and remedies continue beyond this segment.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a commentary-translation hybrid: an early 20th-century scholarly editor (likely translating/paraphrasing from the Ebers Papyrus) interweaves Egyptian prescriptions with editorial commentary (e.g., 'To-day it is the Knife instead of a Knife-stone'). The source text itself is thus a secondary translation layer, not the raw Egyptian hieratic. The unit marker 'I' in prescriptions likely denotes equal parts or a single measure (heqat or similar Egyptian unit). 'SEE TO IT!' may translate an Egyptian emphatic or imperative formula. 'Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son' is a ritual specification reflecting Egyptian medical belief in the particular efficacy of milk from a mother of a male child. The identification of 'Fat-in-the-Eyes' with xanthelasma is the commentator's interpretation and should be treated with caution; other lipid deposits or conditions may be intended. Red-lead (minium) is acutely toxic and its ophthalmic use underscores the significant divergence from modern safety standards. Pagination references (pp. 97–98) indicate this is from a printed scholarly edition.

results.title_inferred: Egyptian Eye Disease Treatments from the Papyrus Ebers: Cataract, Pterygium, and Ritual Formulae
results.original_text: EYES There is a Shouting in the Southern Sky in the Darkness. There is an Uproar in the Northern Sky. The Hall of Pillars falls into the Waters. The Ship-folk of the Sun-god beat their oars so that the heads at his side fall into the water. Who leads hither what he finds? DISEASES OF THE EYES I lead forth what I find. I lead forth your heads. I lift up your necks. I fasten what has been cut from you in its place. I lead you forth to drive away the God of Fevers and all possible Deadly Arts. SO MANY THERE ARE. A formula to repeat over the Brain-of-a-Tortoise that is mixed in Honey, and then laid on the Eyes. Cataract was another condition which had the Ancients at their wits' end. Goose-grease and Honey was tried as a poultice; Isinglass and Verdigris; Lapis lazuli, Milk, Incense, and Crocodile-earth. But how intractable this disease must have proved itself is revealed in the call made to the gods to reinforce the remedy: ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT CATARACT IN THE EYES Come, Verdigris! Come, Verdigris! Come, Thou Fresh One! Come, Efflux from the Eye of the god Horus! It comes, That which issues forth from the Eye of Tum! Come, Juice that gushes from Osiris! He comes to him, he drives away from him Water, Matter, Blood, Inflammation of the Eyes, Mattery-discharge, Blindness, Dripping Eyes. This the God of Fever works all Deadly Arts, the uwedu of every kind, and all things evil of these eyes. SO MANY THERE ARE OF THEM. Words to be spoken over Verdigris mixed with Beetle-wax. Add Cyperus thereto and carefully apply to the Eye. Another growth in the eye, Pterygium, was not looked on kindly, judging by the remedies which these people appeared ready to submit to. Certainly the Scribe leads off with an inoffensive poultice of Honeycomb, following it with another of Beetle-wax, but these must have proved useless for he soon passes on to more complicated and stronger remedies. Excrement-of-the-henut-bird, is recommended, mixed up with Sea-salt and [TEXT CONTINUES]
results.translation:
DISEASES OF THE EYES

[Ritual incantation:] There is a Shouting in the Southern Sky in the Darkness. There is an Uproar in the Northern Sky. The Hall of Pillars falls into the Waters. The Ship-folk of the Sun-god beat their oars so that the heads at his side fall into the water. Who leads hither what he finds? I lead forth what I find. I lead forth your heads. I lift up your necks. I fasten what has been cut from you in its place. I lead you forth to drive away the God of Fevers and all possible Deadly Arts. SO MANY THERE ARE.

[Prescription:] A formula [incantation] to be recited over Brain-of-a-Tortoise mixed with Honey, then laid upon the Eyes.

Cataract [opacity of the lens] was another condition which had the Ancients at their wits' end. Goose-grease and Honey were tried as a poultice; Isinglass [fish-derived gelatin] and Verdigris [copper acetate]; Lapis lazuli, Milk, Incense, and Crocodile-earth [dried crocodile dung or mud associated with crocodiles]. How intractable this disease must have proved is revealed in the call made to the gods to reinforce the remedy:

ANOTHER [INCANTATION] TO DRIVE OUT CATARACT IN THE EYES

Come, Verdigris! Come, Verdigris! Come, Thou Fresh One! Come, Efflux from the Eye of the god Horus! It comes—that which issues forth from the Eye of Atum [Tum]! Come, Juice that gushes from Osiris! He comes to him; he drives away from him: Water, Discharge [Matter], Blood, Inflammation of the Eyes, Purulent discharge [Mattery-discharge], Blindness, Weeping Eyes [Dripping Eyes]. These the God of Fever works through all Deadly Arts—the uwedu [a class of evil supernatural agency or disease-demon] of every kind—and all things evil afflicting these eyes. SO MANY THERE ARE OF THEM.

[Prescription:] Words to be spoken over Verdigris mixed with Beetle-wax [beeswax or insect-derived wax]. Add Cyperus [rush or sedge, likely Cyperus rotundus] thereto and carefully apply to the Eye.

Another growth in the eye—Pterygium [a fibrovascular conjunctival overgrowth]—was evidently regarded with great concern, judging by the remedies to which these people were prepared to submit. The Scribe begins with an inoffensive poultice of Honeycomb, followed by another of Beetle-wax, but these must have proved useless, for he soon passes on to more complicated and stronger remedies. Excrement of the henut-bird is recommended, mixed with Sea-salt and [TEXT CONTINUES — segment ends abruptly].

entities_mentioned.name: Brain-of-a-Tortoise mixed in Honey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tortoise brain (Testudo spp. or similar); mixed with honey as an excipient
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a topical ophthalmic preparation applied to the eyes; exact tortoise species unknown. Honey has known antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Goose-grease
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rendered fat from domestic goose (Anser anser domesticus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a poultice base for treating cataract.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as vehicle/excipient in multiple preparations; has established antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Isinglass
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried swim bladder of fish, primarily sturgeon (Acipenser spp.); a form of collagen/gelatin
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a component in ophthalmic poultice for cataract. Translator's English gloss; the original Egyptian term may differ.

entities_mentioned.name: Verdigris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Copper(II) acetate or basic copper carbonate; a copper salt compound
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both in incantation and as a topical eye preparation mixed with beetle-wax and cyperus. Has known antimicrobial (copper-based) activity but is toxic.

entities_mentioned.name: Lapis lazuli
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lapis lazuli (a metamorphic rock rich in lazurite, pyrite, calcite)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ground and used in ophthalmic preparations; possibly for its blue pigment or perceived magical properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Milk
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Animal milk (species unspecified; likely human, cow, or donkey milk in Egyptian medicine)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a component in cataract treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: Incense
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely frankincense (Boswellia sacra) or a resinous aromatic; exact species uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Generic term; specific resin not identified in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: Crocodile-earth
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly dried crocodile dung or nile mud associated with crocodiles (Crocodylus niloticus)
entities_mentioned.notes: The exact substance is uncertain; 'crocodile-earth' may refer to feces, skin secretion, or habitat mud.

entities_mentioned.name: Beetle-wax
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly beeswax (Apis mellifera) or a wax secreted by beetles; some scholars equate this with beeswax
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an excipient in ophthalmic preparations; the Egyptian original may specify a particular insect.

entities_mentioned.name: Cyperus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Cyperus rotundus (nutgrass/purple nutsedge) or related Cyperus species
entities_mentioned.notes: Added to verdigris and beetle-wax preparation for ophthalmic application.

entities_mentioned.name: Honeycomb
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beeswax comb with residual honey (Apis mellifera)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an initial poultice for pterygium treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: Excrement-of-the-henut-bird
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dung of an unidentified Egyptian bird referred to as 'henut'; species unknown
entities_mentioned.notes: The henut-bird is not securely identified in modern ornithology; its dung was mixed with sea-salt for pterygium treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: Sea-salt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium chloride (NaCl) derived from seawater or salt deposits
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a component in the pterygium remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: uwedu
entities_mentioned.notes: An Egyptian term (transliterated as 'uwedu' by the translator) referring to a class of supernatural disease-causing agents or demons; not a plant or drug.

entities_mentioned.name: Pterygium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pterygium oculi — a fibrovascular growth of the conjunctiva onto the cornea
entities_mentioned.notes: The translator/editor identifies this eye condition by its modern clinical name.

entities_mentioned.name: Cataract
entities_mentioned.notes: The translator/editor identifies this eye condition by its modern clinical name.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a translated and editorially annotated excerpt from a published English rendering of the Papyrus Ebers (an ancient Egyptian medical papyrus, c. 1550 BCE), not a direct OCR of the hieratic original. The source text therefore already represents a layer of translation and interpretive commentary. The page numbers (99, 100) and editorial remarks (e.g., 'the Ancients at their wits' end') belong to the modern editor/translator, not the papyrus itself. The term 'uwedu' is a transliteration of an Egyptian concept and has been preserved as such. 'Brain-of-a-Tortoise,' 'Beetle-wax,' 'Crocodile-earth,' and 'henut-bird' are likely English renderings of Egyptian material culture terms that may not map precisely to modern species. The segment ends mid-sentence, indicating a chunk boundary in the source document. The incantation invoking Horus, Atum (Tum), and Osiris reflects the intertwining of magical and empirical medical practice characteristic of ancient Egyptian medicine.

results.title_inferred: Ancient Egyptian Ophthalmic Remedies: Pterygium, Ocular Discharge, Trichiasis, and Ingrowing Eyelashes
results.original_text:
Crushed some Collyrium in the Egg-of-a-Vulture; but what the colour of the following turned out to be only a brave man would say.

DISEASES OF THE EYES

ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT PTERYGIUM
Red-lead
Powdered-wood-from-Arabia
Iron-from-Apollonopolis-parva
Calamine
Egg-of-an-Ostrich
Saltpetre-from-Upper-Egypt
Sulphur
Honey
Make into one and apply to the Eyes.

Eyes which suffered from a 'flow of matter' were dismissed with a mere couple of remedies. Clay-from-a-Statue was ground up in Honey along with some Leaves-from-the-Castor-Oil-tree, and applied to the eyes. The second remedy was not so easily put up. Some Real Collyrium was soaked in Water for four days; then for another four days it was soaked in Goose-grease. It was now washed in the Milk-of-a-Woman-who-had-borne-a-Son, and allowed to dry for nine days. Thereafter it was crushed with some Myrrh, made up into a ball, and rubbed on the eye.

The victims of ingrowing eyelashes must have been a veritable godsend to the rising ophthalmologist in Ancient Egypt. At the same time they must have proved a very severe drain on the live-stock of the country, as may be seen from a perusal of some of the remedies prescribed for that irritating affliction:

TO DRIVE OUT TRICHIASIS
Myrrh 1
Lizard's Blood 1
Bat's Blood 1
Tear out the Hairs and put thereon in order to make him well.

TO PREVENT THE HAIR GROWING INTO THE EYE AFTER IT HAS BEEN PULLED OUT
Incense-ground-in-Lizard's-dung
Cow's Blood
Ass's Blood
Pig's Blood
Dog's Blood
Stag's Blood
Collyrium
Incense
Crush, rub into one in the different kinds of Blood, and place on the part whence the Hair has been pulled out so that it may not grow again.

ANOTHER
Bat's Blood 1
Rim-of-a-new-hennu-vessel 1
Honey 1
Powder and place where the Hair has been pulled out.

ANOTHER
Fat-of-an-Ox 1
Olive Oil 1
Entrails-of-Moles 1
Crush into one, put on the fire, and put in the place of the Hair.

ANOTHER
Brain-of-[text breaks off]
results.translation:
Crushed some collyrium [eye-paint/medicinal eye preparation] in the egg of a vulture; but what colour the resulting mixture turned out to be, only a brave man would venture to say.

DISEASES OF THE EYES

ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT PTERYGIUM [pterygium: abnormal conjunctival growth]
Red-lead
Powdered wood from Arabia
Iron ore from Apollonopolis Parva [a town in Upper Egypt]
Calamine
Egg of an ostrich
Saltpetre from Upper Egypt
Sulphur
Honey
Combine into one preparation and apply to the eyes.

Eyes suffering from a 'flow of matter' [ocular discharge / conjunctival suppuration] were treated with only a couple of remedies. Clay from a statue was ground up in honey together with leaves from the castor-oil tree, and applied to the eyes. The second remedy required more elaborate preparation. Some genuine collyrium was soaked in water for four days; then for a further four days it was soaked in goose-grease. It was then washed in the milk of a woman who had borne a son, and allowed to dry for nine days. It was thereafter crushed with some myrrh, formed into a ball, and rubbed on the eye.

Sufferers from ingrowing eyelashes must have been a veritable godsend to the aspiring ophthalmologist in ancient Egypt, though at the same time they must have placed a very severe drain on the livestock of the country, as may be judged from a perusal of some of the remedies prescribed for that irritating affliction:

TO DRIVE OUT TRICHIASIS [ingrowing eyelashes]
Myrrh — 1 part
Lizard's blood — 1 part
Bat's blood — 1 part
Pull out the hairs and apply this preparation thereto in order to cure him.

TO PREVENT THE HAIR GROWING BACK INTO THE EYE AFTER IT HAS BEEN PULLED OUT
Incense ground in lizard's dung
Cow's blood
Ass's blood
Pig's blood
Dog's blood
Stag's blood
Collyrium
Incense
Crush together, mix thoroughly in the various kinds of blood, and place upon the site from which the hair was pulled out, so that it may not grow again.

ANOTHER REMEDY
Bat's blood — 1 part
Rim of a new hennu-vessel [a ritual/measuring vessel] — 1 part
Honey — 1 part
Grind to a powder and place where the hair was pulled out.

ANOTHER REMEDY
Fat of an ox — 1 part
Olive oil — 1 part
Entrails of moles — 1 part
Crush together, heat over the fire, and apply to the site from which the hair was removed.

ANOTHER REMEDY
Brain of — [text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: Collyrium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eye-paint or medicated eye preparation, often kohl (antimony sulfide or galena)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both cosmetically and therapeutically in ancient Egypt; composition varied.

entities_mentioned.name: Egg-of-a-Vulture
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle/excipient for medicinal preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Pterygium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pterygium oculi — abnormal fibrovascular conjunctival growth encroaching on the cornea
entities_mentioned.notes: Named explicitly; still a recognized ophthalmic condition today.

entities_mentioned.name: Red-lead
entities_mentioned.notes: Toxic heavy metal compound; used in ancient pharmacopoeias as an astringent/antiseptic.

entities_mentioned.name: Powdered-wood-from-Arabia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly sandalwood (Santalum album) or another aromatic Arabian timber; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Identity unclear; may refer to a traded aromatic wood.

entities_mentioned.name: Iron-from-Apollonopolis-parva
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iron ore or iron preparation from Apollonopolis Parva (Qus), Upper Egypt
entities_mentioned.notes: Geographic provenance suggests a specific mineral source; likely iron oxide or ochre.

entities_mentioned.name: Calamine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zinc carbonate or zinc oxide (ZnCO₃ / ZnO)
entities_mentioned.notes: Still used in modern dermatological preparations; mildly astringent.

entities_mentioned.name: Egg-of-an-Ostrich
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an excipient or binder.

entities_mentioned.name: Saltpetre-from-Upper-Egypt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Potassium nitrate (KNO₃) or natron (sodium carbonate/bicarbonate); natron more likely in Egyptian context
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian 'saltpetre' often refers to natron, a naturally occurring salt used in purification and medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Sulphur
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an antiseptic and antiparasitic in ancient medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Common vehicle and preservative in Egyptian medicine; also possesses antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Clay-from-a-Statue
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clay or mud taken from a cult statue; ritual rather than purely pharmacological ingredient
entities_mentioned.notes: The ritual provenance (from a statue) suggests magical/religious significance.

entities_mentioned.name: Leaves-from-the-Castor-Oil-tree
entities_mentioned.notes: Castor plant was widely used in ancient Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Goose-grease
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rendered fat from domestic goose (Anser anser domesticus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a lipid vehicle/excipient in Egyptian pharmaceutics.

entities_mentioned.name: Milk-of-a-Woman-who-had-borne-a-Son
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Human breast milk (specified gender of child has ritual significance)
entities_mentioned.notes: Human milk was a common ingredient in Egyptian eye remedies; the specification of a male child is ritual/magical.

entities_mentioned.name: Myrrh
entities_mentioned.notes: Aromatic resin with antimicrobial properties; used widely in ancient medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Trichiasis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trichiasis — ingrowing eyelashes abrading the cornea
entities_mentioned.notes: Named explicitly; a common ophthalmic complaint still treated today.

entities_mentioned.name: Lizard's Blood
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Blood of an unspecified lizard species
entities_mentioned.notes: Animal blood used in magical-medical contexts; species not specified.

entities_mentioned.name: Bat's Blood
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Blood of an unspecified bat species
entities_mentioned.notes: Appears in multiple trichiasis remedies; possibly depilatory or ritual significance.

entities_mentioned.name: Incense
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely frankincense (Boswellia sacra) or a blend of aromatic resins
entities_mentioned.notes: Generic term; frankincense was most common in Egyptian ritual and medical use.

entities_mentioned.name: Hennu-vessel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A specific type of Egyptian ritual or measuring vessel
entities_mentioned.notes: The rim (scrapings?) of a new hennu-vessel used as an ingredient; possibly ceramic material with ritual significance.

entities_mentioned.name: Entrails-of-Moles
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Internal organs of the mole (possibly Spalax ehrenbergi or Talpa species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a topical preparation; the rationale is obscure.

entities_mentioned.name: Olive Oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Common lipid vehicle in ancient medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Fat-of-an-Ox
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard lipid excipient in Egyptian pharmacopoeia.

results.translator_notes: This segment is an English translation/paraphrase (by George Ebers or a subsequent editor/translator) of portions of the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE), not a direct translation from the hieratic original. The source text is thus already one step removed. OCR artefacts corrected include: 'A pollonopolis-parva' → 'Apollonopolis-parva'; 'U pper-Egypt' → 'Upper-Egypt'; scattered stray characters (eS, S+, =S, etc.) representing alignment/formatting marks in the original tabular recipe layout have been silently removed. The portion ends mid-sentence ('Brain-of-') indicating the chunk boundary falls within a recipe. Dosage notations ('I', '1') in the recipes appear to represent equal parts rather than absolute quantities. The editorial commentary interpolated between recipes (e.g., observations about livestock drain) is the voice of the modern editor/translator, not the papyrus itself.

results.title_inferred: Ebers Papyrus: Eye Remedies and Related Treatments
results.original_text: calamity. The first, a 'lotion' made by crushing Dried Myrrh in Sour Milk, we may be sure accomplished nothing. The second, however, a poultice of Powdered Onions, most assuredly made the blind man see stars. The third remedy spared the patient somewhat, the disease being attacked through that less-sensitive organ, the ear: ANOTHER AGAINST BLINDNESS The Two Eyes of a Pig; remove the water therefrom True Collyrium Red-lead Wild Honey Crush, powder, make into one, and inject into the Ear of the Patient. Thereby he will at once recover. When thou hast seen properly to this mixing, repeat this Magic Formula: I HAVE BROUGHT THIS THING AND PUT IT IN ITS PLACE. THE CROCODILE IS WEAK AND POWERLESS. (Twice). 'For the Drawing-together of the Pupil of the Eye,' whatever that may mean, one bathed the eye frequently with a lotion made by crushing Shavings-from-Ebony-Wood with Soda-from-Upper-Egypt in Water. For a swelling in the Nose caused by inflammation of the tear-sac a salve composed of Collyrium, Dried Myrrh, Arabian-wood powder, and Honey, was rubbed in for four days. 'MARX TUTS WELL (the Scribe adds), FOR IT IS THE RIGHT THING TO DO.' Another eye-salve is prescribed for 'Driving out Tumours in-the-Head,' while the Scribe also finds room to quote two salves for general use: one 'AS PRESCRIBED BY THE PRIESTLY PHARMACIST XUI,' the other 'AS TOLD US BY A JEW FROM BYBLOS.' As well he provides against any obscure ailment of the eye not specifically dealt with by him: REMEDY FOR THE EYE WHEN SOMETHING EVIL HAS HAPPENED TO IT: A Human Brain. Divide it in halves. To one half add Honey and anoint the Eye therewith in the Evening. Dry the other half, crush, powder, and anoint the Eye therewith in the Morning. To correct Squint, Collyrium, Red-lead, and Soda were beaten into a paste and applied to the wandering eye. When the futility of this became apparent, a poultice of Onions, Granite, and Resin, was tried. This in turn failed.
results.translation:
The text describes a series of remedies drawn from the Papyrus Ebers, a collection of ancient Egyptian medical prescriptions, here presented with editorial commentary. The first remedy for blindness — a lotion made by crushing Dried Myrrh in Sour Milk — is judged by the modern commentator to have been entirely ineffective. The second remedy, a poultice of Powdered Onions applied to the eyes, would certainly have caused intense irritation ('made the blind man see stars'). The third remedy takes a different approach, treating the eye disease via the ear, presumably on the theory that this route would be less painful:

ANOTHER REMEDY AGAINST BLINDNESS:
Ingredients: The two eyes of a pig (the fluid/water removed); True Collyrium [antimony or kohl]; Red-lead [lead oxide/minium]; Wild Honey. Method: Crush, powder, combine into one preparation, and inject [instill] into the ear of the patient. He will thereby recover at once. After correctly mixing the preparation, recite the following magical formula: 'I HAVE BROUGHT THIS THING AND PUT IT IN ITS PLACE. THE CROCODILE IS WEAK AND POWERLESS.' (To be repeated twice.)

For a condition described as 'Drawing-together of the Pupil of the Eye' [possibly miosis or pupil contraction of uncertain etiology], the eye was frequently bathed with a lotion made by crushing ebony-wood shavings together with natron [soda/sodium carbonate] from Upper Egypt in water.

For a swelling of the nose caused by inflammation of the tear-sac [dacryocystitis], a salve composed of Collyrium, Dried Myrrh, Arabian-wood powder [possibly a resinous wood from Arabia], and Honey was rubbed in for four consecutive days. The scribe appends the note: 'THIS WORKS WELL, FOR IT IS THE RIGHT THING TO DO.'

A further eye-salve is prescribed for 'Driving out Tumours in the Head.' The scribe also records two salves for general ophthalmic use: one as prescribed by the priestly pharmacist Xui, the other as communicated by a Jew from Byblos [ancient Phoenician city, modern Jubayl, Lebanon].

The scribe also provides a catch-all remedy for unspecified eye ailments:

REMEDY FOR THE EYE WHEN SOMETHING EVIL HAS HAPPENED TO IT:
Ingredients: A human brain. Method: Divide in half. Mix one half with Honey and anoint the eye with this in the evening. Dry the other half, crush to a powder, and anoint the eye with this in the morning.

For the correction of a squint [strabismus], Collyrium, Red-lead, and Soda were beaten into a paste and applied to the affected eye. When the inefficacy of this treatment became evident, a poultice of Onions, Granite [powdered granite or stone], and Resin was attempted. This too ultimately failed.

entities_mentioned.name: Dried Myrrh
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a lotion with sour milk for blindness; also in a salve for dacryocystitis with collyrium, Arabian-wood powder, and honey.

entities_mentioned.name: Sour Milk
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the vehicle for a lotion for blindness.

entities_mentioned.name: Powdered Onions
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied as a poultice for blindness; the commentator notes this would have caused severe irritation. Also used in a later poultice for squint with granite and resin.

entities_mentioned.name: Eyes of a Pig
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'water' (aqueous humor or vitreous humor) was to be removed before use. Combined with collyrium, red-lead, and honey for a remedy injected into the ear for blindness.

entities_mentioned.name: True Collyrium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Antimony sulfide (stibnite) or kohl; possibly galena (lead sulfide)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard ancient Egyptian ophthalmic preparation. Appears in multiple remedies throughout the segment.

entities_mentioned.name: Red-lead
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in the ear-instilled blindness remedy and in the paste for squint. Toxic by modern standards.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild Honey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Honey from wild bees (Apis mellifera or related species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a binding and possibly antimicrobial agent in multiple preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Shavings-from-Ebony-Wood
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diospyros ebenum or related ebony species
entities_mentioned.notes: Crushed with soda in water as a lotion for pupillary constriction ('drawing-together of the pupil').

entities_mentioned.name: Soda-from-Upper-Egypt
entities_mentioned.notes: A naturally occurring mineral salt mined in Egypt, used as a lotion base and in the paste for squint.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabian-wood powder
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown aromatic or resinous wood from Arabia; possibly aloeswood (Aquilaria sp.) or similar
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a salve for dacryocystitis alongside collyrium, myrrh, and honey.

entities_mentioned.name: Human Brain
entities_mentioned.notes: Split in half; one half mixed with honey for evening application to the eye, the other half dried and powdered for morning application. A general-purpose eye remedy for unspecified ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: Granite
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a poultice for strabismus (squint) together with onions and resin. Presumably ground to a fine powder.

entities_mentioned.name: Resin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unspecified plant resin; possibly pine or terebinth resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in poultice for squint alongside onions and granite.

entities_mentioned.name: Priestly Pharmacist Xui
entities_mentioned.notes: Named ancient Egyptian priestly pharmacist cited as the authority for a general-use eye salve.

entities_mentioned.name: A Jew from Byblos
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as the source of a general-use eye salve. Byblos is an ancient Phoenician/Canaanite coastal city (modern Jubayl, Lebanon). Textual attribution suggests foreign medical knowledge was incorporated into Egyptian practice.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text is a modern English secondary commentary on the Papyrus Ebers (an ancient Egyptian medical papyrus, c. 1550 BCE), not a direct translation from the original hieratic script. The segment mixes direct quotation/paraphrase of papyrus content with editorial commentary. 
2. OCR error 'HE CROCODILE IS WEAK' corrected to 'THE CROCODILE IS WEAK' (initial 'T' dropped by OCR). 
3. 'MARX TUTS WELL' is almost certainly an OCR/typographical corruption of a scribal attribution or phrase; it may read 'MARKS IT WELL' or could be a proper name corruption. Retained with a note, as the intended reading is ambiguous. 
4. 'Byszios' (OCR artifact) corrected to 'Byblos,' the well-known ancient Phoenician city, based on context and standard scholarly references to this papyrus passage. 
5. The magical formula invoking the crocodile reflects the integration of ritual magic with medical treatment standard in ancient Egyptian medicine. 
6. The condition 'Drawing-together of the Pupil of the Eye' is not readily identifiable as a specific modern diagnosis; it may refer to miosis, a pterygium, or another anterior segment condition. 
7. The text cuts off mid-sentence ('This in turn fai-') indicating the chunk boundary falls mid-word ('failed').

results.title_inferred: Remedies for Eye, Ear, Nose and Mouth Diseases from the Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text: Several remedies for 'Hotness in the Eyes,' a condition which without doubt must have been visited rather frequently on those driven for various reasons to place themselves under the care and treatment of an Oculist of the Pharaohonic period. As an eye-cooler, 'Baked Ox-liver, placed carefully thereon' does not sound very hopeful, but Soda-from-Upper-Egypt (even when dissolved in Spring-water) sounds positively alarming in spite of the Scribe's special note appended to the prescription: 'It Heats It!' The third remedy, however, shows more promise, and reveals once again a belief on the part of these ancient physicians in the homœopathic doctrine of like-curing-like: ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT HOTNESS IN THE EYES: Tallow-from-the-Jawbone-of-an-Ass. Mix in Cool Water and let the Patient put on his Temples in order that he may be healed forthwith. Chapter XVII: DISEASES OF THE EAR, NOSE AND MOUTH. In contradistinction to eye-troubles, affections of the Ears receive scant treatment. As might be expected, the chapter opens with a remedy for the Ear-that-Hears-Badly, but it is extremely doubtful if the sufferer experienced any relief from having his ears plugged with Red-lead and Resin-from-the-am-tree, even when rubbed up beforehand with Fresh Olive Oil. For an Ear-that-Discharges-foul-smelling-Matter, an application of Incense-in-Goose-Grease ground up with Cream-from-the-Milk-of-a-Cow in various grains is recommended, but whether as poultice or injection is not altogether clear. Not that it mattered much, one is safe in asserting. Failing the above, an injection composed of Ass's Ear, Red-lead, Caraway and Olive Oil, could be squirted into the Ear. But discharging ears were as resistant to treatment in those days as in these, and detailed instructions were sometimes necessary: ANOTHER TO TREAT THE EAR: Treat it with cooling remedies, not warm ones. When the met trembles then make for him [an amulet] out of Greenstone. Crush an [remainder of text cut off].
results.translation: Several remedies are given for 'Hotness in the Eyes' [ocular inflammation], a condition that must have been quite common among those who sought the care of an oculist during the Pharaonic period. As an eye-cooler, 'Baked Ox-liver, placed carefully thereon' does not sound very hopeful; and Soda-from-Upper-Egypt [natron], even when dissolved in spring-water, sounds positively alarming, despite the scribe's special marginal note appended to the prescription reading: 'It Heats It!' The third remedy, however, shows more promise and reveals once again the ancient physicians' belief in the homœopathic doctrine of like curing like: ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO DRIVE OUT HOTNESS IN THE EYES: Tallow from the jawbone of an ass. Mix in cool water and let the patient apply it to his temples so that he may be healed forthwith. Chapter XVII: DISEASES OF THE EAR, NOSE, AND MOUTH. In contrast to eye complaints, affections of the ears receive comparatively little treatment. As might be expected, the chapter opens with a remedy for the Ear-that-Hears-Badly [deafness or impaired hearing], though it is extremely doubtful whether the sufferer gained any relief from having his ears plugged with red lead [minium] and resin from the 'am'-tree, even when first rubbed up with fresh olive oil. For an Ear-that-Discharges-Foul-Smelling-Matter [otorrhea], an application of incense in goose-grease, ground together with cream from cow's milk, combined with various grain ingredients, is recommended—though it is not entirely clear whether this was intended as a poultice or an injection. Failing that remedy, an injection composed of ass's ear [a plant ingredient], red lead, caraway, and olive oil could be instilled into the ear. Discharging ears were, however, as resistant to treatment then as now, and detailed instructions were sometimes necessary: ANOTHER [REMEDY] TO TREAT THE EAR: Treat it with cooling remedies, not warm ones. When the 'met' [possibly a vessel or tendon; Egyptian anatomical term] trembles, then make for him [an amulet or preparation] out of greenstone [malachite or similar green mineral]. Crush an [text incomplete].

entities_mentioned.name: Baked Ox-liver (applied to eyes)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used topically as an eye-cooler for ocular inflammation; liver contains vitamin A, though baking would degrade it.

entities_mentioned.name: Soda-from-Upper-Egypt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Natron (hydrated sodium carbonate/bicarbonate, Na2CO3·10H2O or similar)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dissolved in spring-water and applied to eyes; highly caustic, likely harmful. Scribe's own note warns 'It Heats It!'

entities_mentioned.name: Tallow-from-the-Jawbone-of-an-Ass
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Animal fat rendered from the jaw of a donkey (Equus asinus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mixed with cool water and applied to the temples to treat 'hotness in the eyes'; reflects homœopathic principle of like-curing-like.

entities_mentioned.name: Red-lead
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in two separate ear remedies; highly toxic by modern standards.

entities_mentioned.name: Resin-from-the-am-tree
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; possibly a resin from an unidentified Egyptian tree
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'am'-tree is an unidentified Egyptian plant; its resin was used in combination with red lead and olive oil for impaired hearing.

entities_mentioned.name: Fresh Olive Oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle/carrier for mixing ear remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Incense-in-Goose-Grease
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aromatic resin (possibly frankincense, Boswellia sacra) combined with rendered goose fat
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for otorrhea (foul-smelling ear discharge), ground with cream from cow's milk.

entities_mentioned.name: Cream-from-the-Milk-of-a-Cow
entities_mentioned.notes: Ground together with incense in goose-grease for treatment of discharging ear.

entities_mentioned.name: Ass's Ear
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a plant part named for its shape, or literal donkey ear tissue
entities_mentioned.notes: Ingredient in an ear-injection remedy alongside red lead, caraway, and olive oil.

entities_mentioned.name: Caraway
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carum carvi or possibly Cuminum cyminum (cumin); Egyptian usage sometimes conflates these
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in an injection remedy for discharging ear.

entities_mentioned.name: Greenstone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malachite (copper carbonate hydroxide) or similar green mineral
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a preparation or amulet for ear treatment when the 'met' trembles; context suggests possible crushing and topical application.

entities_mentioned.name: met
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Egyptian anatomical term, variously translated as vessel, tendon, or channel
entities_mentioned.notes: An ancient Egyptian anatomical concept referring to conduits in the body; the exact referent in the ear context is unclear.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a secondary scholarly commentary on the Papyrus Ebers rather than a direct translation of the hieratic source; it paraphrases and discusses the original Egyptian prescriptions. The OCR error 'Iw' has been corrected to 'In' and 'Ir Heats 1T' to 'It Heats It'. The 'K 106' appears to be a page-header artifact and has been omitted from the cleaned text. The text breaks off mid-sentence ('Crush an') indicating an incomplete OCR capture. The 'am-tree' remains unidentified in Egyptological literature. The homœopathic interpretation ('like-curing-like') is the commentary author's gloss, not an Egyptian claim.

results.title_inferred: Treatments for Tumours, Ear Ulcers, and Coryza (Ebers Papyrus)
results.original_text: It clears up through the shooting-forth of the god Su. If it does not fall from him onto the earth, then make for him Slime-from-the-Greatness-of-that-which-presents-itself (i.e. the tumour), and enclose it in the Milky-Juice-of-the-Sycamore-Tree, that it may bind up his blood; neither Oil nor Honey put thereto; DISEASES OF EAR, NOSE, AND MOUTH cut off a half, as one would not wish that his Blood fall away from the other half... (uncertain). 4. WHEN AFTERWARDS THOU RECOGNIZEST THAT IT HAS BOUND ITSELF, SO MAKE THOU FOR HIM: Oil Wax Cook and plaster therewith. Don't use too much. Treat in the same way every swelling which breaks. If it goes off in abundance, then have a Linen band tied around the back of his head. Considered in the light of more recent research it seems a pity for the sake of the patient that the Linen-band was not thought of in the first place and the remainder of the remedy conveniently forgotten. Lastly, in the event of ulceration forming and extending into the Ear: WHAT TO DO TO TREAT ULCERS WHICH EXTEND INTO THE EAR There is a swelling internally. There is matter from the ulcers and from the dirt in his Ears, with fluid like Water-of-the-fermented-meswxa-drink. Go round the Ulcers with the Knife as far as there is Disease therein. Prepare for him Oil and Honey; put inside of it Charpie of Flax-of-a-cloth. Plaster it therewith so that it becomes well. THE PAPYRUS EBERS The nose is dealt with even more summarily than are the Ears, Coryza being the only condition mentioned. It could be treated by pouring Date-juice into the nostrils, or by rubbing the Nose with Peppermint-in-Dates; or much more vigorously, not to mention picturesquely, by a combination of Magic, Medicine and Surgery: TO CHARM AWAY CORYZA Spit it out, thou Slime, Son of Slime: Grasp the bones, touch the skull, smear with tallow, give the patient seven openings in the head, serve the god Ra, thank the god Thoth. Then I brought thy remedy for thee, thy drink for the
results.translation:
It clears up through the shooting-forth [divine expulsion] of the god Su. If it [the tumour or swelling] does not fall from him onto the earth, then prepare for him the Slime-from-the-Greatness-of-that-which-presents-itself (i.e., the tumour), and enclose it in the Milky-Juice-of-the-Sycamore-Tree, so that it may bind up [staunch] his blood; add neither Oil nor Honey to it. [Section heading:] DISEASES OF EAR, NOSE, AND MOUTH. Cut away a half [of the swelling], as one would not wish his Blood to flow away from the other half... (meaning uncertain). 

4. WHEN AFTERWARDS THOU RECOGNIZEST THAT IT HAS BOUND ITSELF [the wound has consolidated], THEN PREPARE FOR HIM: Oil [and] Wax — cook [together] and apply as a plaster. Do not use too much. Treat in the same manner every swelling that breaks open. If [the discharge] flows out in abundance, have a linen bandage tied around the back of his head. Considered in the light of more recent research, it seems a pity for the sake of the patient that the linen bandage was not thought of in the first instance, with the remainder of the remedy conveniently forgotten.

Lastly, in the event of ulceration forming and extending into the Ear:

WHAT TO DO TO TREAT ULCERS WHICH EXTEND INTO THE EAR
There is an internal swelling. There is discharge [pus and matter] from the ulcers and from the accumulated dirt in his Ears, with fluid resembling the Water-of-the-fermented-meswxa-drink [a fermented beverage used as a comparator for the appearance or odour of the discharge]. Go around the Ulcers with the Knife [i.e., excise] as far as the Disease extends within. Prepare for him Oil and Honey; place inside it Charpie [lint/tow] of Flax taken from a cloth. Apply it as a plaster so that he recovers.

THE PAPYRUS EBERS
The nose is treated even more briefly than the ears, Coryza [rhinitis/common cold] being the only condition mentioned. It could be treated by pouring Date-juice into the nostrils, or by rubbing the Nose with Peppermint-in-Dates; or, much more vigorously — not to mention picturesquely — by a combination of Magic, Medicine, and Surgery:

TO CHARM AWAY CORYZA
Spit it out, thou Slime, Son of Slime! Grasp the bones, touch the skull, smear [the head] with tallow, make seven openings in the patient's head, serve the god Ra, give thanks to the god Thoth. Then I have brought thy remedy for thee, thy drink for the [passage incomplete].

entities_mentioned.name: Milky-Juice-of-the-Sycamore-Tree
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a haemostatic and binding agent applied to tumours/wounds; the milky latex of Ficus sycomorus was a well-attested ancient Egyptian medicinal substance.

entities_mentioned.name: Oil (unnamed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely sesame or moringa oil (Sesamum indicum or Moringa peregrina)
entities_mentioned.notes: Generic term in the papyrus; exact species unclear without further context.

entities_mentioned.name: Wax
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with oil, cooked, and used as a plaster for swellings.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with oil as a wound dressing for ear ulcers; well-documented antimicrobial agent in ancient Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Charpie of Flax-of-a-cloth
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to unravelled or scraped linen fibres used as wound packing [charpie]; inserted into ear ulcers with oil and honey.

entities_mentioned.name: Linen band
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied around the back of the head as a pressure dressing to control excessive wound discharge.

entities_mentioned.name: Date-juice
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Expressed juice of Phoenix dactylifera (date palm)
entities_mentioned.notes: Poured into the nostrils as a treatment for coryza.

entities_mentioned.name: Peppermint-in-Dates
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha piperita or related Mentha species combined with Phoenix dactylifera
entities_mentioned.notes: Rubbed on the nose for coryza; exact Mentha species used in ancient Egypt debated.

entities_mentioned.name: Tallow
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rendered animal fat, likely bovine or ovine
entities_mentioned.notes: Applied to the skull in the magical-surgical charm against coryza.

entities_mentioned.name: meswxa-drink
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a descriptive comparator for the colour or consistency of ear discharge; exact composition unknown. Possibly a fermented grain or date beer variant.

results.translator_notes: This chunk is a translated and editorially annotated excerpt from a published English rendering of the Ebers Papyrus (likely Bryan's 1930 or Joachim's 1890 translation), not a direct translation from the original hieratic. The translator/editor has interjected modern commentary (e.g., the remark about the linen bandage). The term 'meswxa' is transliterated from the Egyptian and its exact identification remains uncertain. The 'seven openings in the head' in the coryza charm most likely refers to a ritual/symbolic number rather than a literal surgical trepanation. The text breaks off mid-sentence at the end of the segment ('thy drink for the'), indicating this is a chunk boundary. The god 'Su' likely refers to Shu, Egyptian god of air, whose 'shooting-forth' metaphorically describes expulsion of disease. OCR artefact 'I10' has been silently corrected to 'THE PAPYRUS EBERS' section heading continuation.

results.title_inferred: Diseases of the Ear, Nose, and Mouth; Dental Remedies; Introduction to the Nervous System — Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text: into the mouth of the Ancient Egyptian by the physicians of the day, it is remarkable to note how lightly that cavity (with its contents) escaped serious damage. The diseased tongue is dismissed DISEASES OF EAR, NOSE, AND MOUTH 111 with a few simple remedies. At first a Milk-gargle is deemed sufficient: BEGINNING OF THE REMEDIES TO DRIVE OUT DISEASES OF THE TONGUE; Milk Gargle and put on the earth (i.e. spit out). If the disease persisted, the Milk is reinforced by Goose-grease and the gargle turned into a chewing-remedy: ANOTHER amaa-erain Goose-grease Milk Chew. Another chewing-remedy was composed of Cow's Milk, Fresh Bread and Bullock's Fat; while still another could be prepared from Goose-Grease, Honey, Incense, Caraway-seeds, Fresh Lead-earth and Water, a specific instruction to chew it Nine Times being added. As for the teeth, all that these appeared to need from time to time was 'strengthening,' which, after all, was not to be wondered at. Powdered-Fruit-of-the-Dom-palm with Honey effected this, we learn: it was rubbed on the teeth. If it did not, then Incense, Verdigris, and Fresh Lead-earth was tried; while extra-stubborn cases could try a still grittier preparation in the shape of Pebbles-powdered-in-Honey. The 'strengthening' process could also be carried out by the chewing method; Crocus-in-Sweet-Beer was one of the remedies recommended. Gumboils (even the Ancients suffered from these painful afflictions!) were also found to yield to a chewing-remedy composed of Cow's Milk, Fresh Dates, and uah-grain, provided, however, it was kept moist and chewed Nine Times. And they also had 'Blisters-in-the-Teeth,' whatever they may mean. These were dealt with by a plaster which included Incense, Crocus, Cyperus, Onions, the Wood-of-Aloes, and Water, not to mention a variety of herbs of whose name and nature we are still in ignorance. Chapter XVIII THE NERVOUS SYSTEM THE proper interpretation to give to the word 'met' has exercised
results.translation: Among the remedies placed into the mouths of ancient Egyptians by the physicians of the time, it is remarkable to note how little serious damage was done to the oral cavity and its contents. Diseases of the tongue are addressed with only a few simple remedies. At first, a milk gargle was deemed sufficient — the heading reads: BEGINNING OF THE REMEDIES TO DRIVE OUT DISEASES OF THE TONGUE: Milk. Gargle and spit out onto the ground. If the disease persisted, the milk was reinforced with goose-grease and the gargle transformed into a chewing-remedy: ANOTHER [amaa-erain, Egyptian term untranslated]: Goose-grease, Milk. Chew. Another chewing-remedy was composed of Cow's Milk, Fresh Bread, and Bullock's Fat; yet another could be prepared from Goose-Grease, Honey, Incense, Caraway-seeds, Fresh Lead-earth, and Water, with specific instruction to chew it nine times. As for the teeth, all they appeared to require from time to time was 'strengthening,' which is scarcely surprising given the diet. Powdered fruit of the Doum-palm mixed with Honey accomplished this, we are told; it was rubbed onto the teeth. If that failed, Incense, Verdigris, and Fresh Lead-earth were tried; while particularly stubborn cases could attempt an even grittier preparation in the form of Pebbles-powdered-in-Honey. The 'strengthening' process could also be performed by the chewing method; Crocus-in-Sweet-Beer was one of the remedies recommended. Gumboils — even the ancients suffered from these painful afflictions — were likewise found to yield to a chewing-remedy composed of Cow's Milk, Fresh Dates, and uah-grain [an unidentified Egyptian grain], provided it was kept moist and chewed nine times. There are also references to 'Blisters-in-the-Teeth,' the precise meaning of which remains unclear. These were treated with a plaster that included Incense, Crocus, Cyperus, Onions, the Wood-of-Aloes, and Water, as well as a variety of herbs whose names and natures remain unknown to us. Chapter XVIII: THE NERVOUS SYSTEM. The proper interpretation to be given to the word 'met' has long exercised [scholars — text continues].

results.title_inferred: Remedies for the Met, the Nervous System, and the AAA Disease in the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text: the Body' he set down several remedies. One, an internal remedy, was compounded of Figs, Grapes, and Onions, Bread-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus, Blossoms-of-the-Cucumber, Incense, Wine, and Sweet Beer. It was taken for four days. Irregularities of the met were cured by the patient chewing uwah-grain while at the same time he clapped on to his buttocks a poultice of Goose's eggs— still another illustration of the Scribe's belief in the doctrine of like-curing-like. As to the possibility of the human body assimilating the foregoing remedies there appears to have been a doubt in the mind of the Scribe. At any rate he very properly provided, or attempted to provide, against that eventuality, with what success it is not very difficult to estimate even at this distance of time: THE NERVOUS SYSTEM A REMEDY TO ALLOW THE MET TO TAKE UP THE REMEDY Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son Let it stand in a New Vessel until the Cream separates. Then smear the Patient. Chapter XIX THE AAA DISEASE Of all the difficulties that beset the translators of the Papyrus Ebers the most difficult centred around the diagnosis of the conditions which were continually cropping up in the text under the names of the AAA disease, the uxedu-disease, and the uha-disease. Sometimes these conditions were described and treated separately. At other times they complicated each other sadly. Three facts led at first to the AAA disease being diagnosed as Bilharzia. These were firstly that throughout the papyrus the AAA disease is almost invariably qualified by the adjective 'deadly.' Secondly the disease was specifically related to 'worms'; for example on Plate 19 of the Papyrus there appears a 'remedy to kill worms in the body that have been caused by the AAA disease.' And thirdly that the symbol AAA was always followed by a Phallus as the 'determinative sign.' This diagnosis of Bilharzia, however, is discarded by Joachim, who holds that the AAA disease is in reality the common Egyptian scourge, Chlorosis.
results.translation:
Regarding the body, the Scribe set down several remedies. One internal remedy was compounded of Figs, Grapes, and Onions, Bread-of-the-Zizyphus-Lotus [bread made from the fruit of Ziziphus lotus], Blossoms-of-the-Cucumber, Incense, Wine, and Sweet Beer. It was to be taken over four days. Irregularities of the 'met' [the Egyptian concept of bodily channels or vessels] were treated by having the patient chew 'uwah-grain' while simultaneously applying a poultice of Goose's eggs to his buttocks — yet another illustration of the Scribe's adherence to the doctrine of like-curing-like [similia similibus curantur]. As to whether the human body could actually assimilate these remedies, the Scribe himself appears to have harbored some doubt. In any case, he duly provided — or attempted to provide — against that contingency, with what success it is not difficult to judge even at this remove in time.

THE NERVOUS SYSTEM

A REMEDY TO ALLOW THE MET TO ABSORB THE REMEDY:
Milk of a Woman Who Has Borne a Son — let it stand in a New Vessel until the Cream separates. Then smear the Patient.

Chapter XIX: THE AAA DISEASE

Of all the difficulties confronting the translators of the Papyrus Ebers, the most formidable centered on the diagnosis of conditions that recur throughout the text under the designations of the 'AAA disease,' the 'uxedu-disease,' and the 'uha-disease.' These conditions are sometimes described and treated separately, and at other times they are confusingly interrelated. Three facts initially led scholars to identify the AAA disease with Bilharzia [schistosomiasis]: first, throughout the papyrus the AAA disease is almost invariably qualified by the adjective 'deadly'; second, the disease was specifically associated with 'worms' — for instance, Plate 19 of the Papyrus contains a 'remedy to kill worms in the body that have been caused by the AAA disease'; and third, the hieroglyphic symbol AAA was always followed by the Phallus as a 'determinative sign' [a hieroglyph indicating semantic category]. This diagnosis of Bilharzia is, however, rejected by Joachim, who argues that the AAA disease is in reality Chlorosis [iron-deficiency anemia], the common Egyptian affliction.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a secondary scholarly commentary on the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE) rather than a direct translation of the hieratic original. The 'met' is a key technical term in Egyptian physiology with no direct modern anatomical equivalent. The 'AAA disease' debate between Bilharzia and Chlorosis reflects early 20th-century Egyptological scholarship; modern scholars (e.g., Nunn) have continued to debate this identification. The 'ny' preceding 'REMEDY' in the original OCR appears to be a corrupted section header ('THE NERVOUS SYSTEM') and has been silently corrected. The abbreviation 'CuHaprer' is an OCR error for 'Chapter' and has been corrected. The doctrine of 'like-curing-like' referenced here foreshadows homoeopathic principles but is here attributed to sympathetic magic rather than Hahnemann's system.

results.title_inferred: Diagnosis and Remedies for the AAA Disease in the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text: the authority Griesinger) afflicted one out of every four of the population of Egypt. The second he waves aside on the ground that the 'worms' mentioned could apply equally to the Hookworm as to the Bilharzia parasite. Then as to the third point he insists that the use of the Phallus as the 'determinative sign' following the symbol AAA must be viewed in the light of two other considerations: firstly that the Phallus is also the 'determinative sign' placed after AA, the symbol by which the much-mentioned Ass is designated in the Papyrus (a fact which very considerably diminishes the importance of its significance in the former case); and secondly that the use of the male generative organ in such a way is as easily applicable to Chlorosis Egyptica as to Bilharzia since delayed puberty, impotence in males, and sterility in females, were frequent sequelae of the Chlorosis. As to the uha-disease and the uxedu-disease, Joachim holds that they are merely manifestations of the AAA disease. The uxedu-disease, he declares, can be ascribed to the scourge in its early stages, although he is careful to point out that there are certain objections to that view which he cannot combat with satisfaction to himself. He feels on surer ground with the uha-disease, however, and unhesitatingly associates it with Chlorosis Aegyptica. THE PAPYRUS EBERS. But whatever the diagnosis, and the balance of evidence is in favour of Joachim's contention, the multitudinous remedies proposed for the AAA disease, whether by itself or complicated by the uha-disease or the uxedu-disease, brand it as a scourge of the first magnitude. Scores of 'remedies' are set down for combating its ravages, their number providing active proof (if such were needed) of the old adage that where there are many cures there is no cure. Added to which it can hardly be maintained that any one of the remedies proposed inspires a modern practitioner with any feeling of confidence in their success. To take a sample at random—
