Bryan's 1930 English translation and analysis of the Ebers Papyrus) rather than a direct translation of the papyrus itself. It discusses the interpretive debate over the identification of ancient Egyptian diseases. The text is largely in English already; cleaning involved correcting 'seguele' to 'sequelae,' 'A\'gyptica' to 'Aegyptica,' and 'objec- tions' (hyphenated line-break) to 'objections.' The segment ends mid-sentence, indicating truncation at a chapter or page boundary. No plant, drug, or preparation is described in this chunk—it is meta-commentary on the multiplicity of remedies without enumerating them.

results.title_inferred: Remedies for the Uxedu Disease and Related Conditions (Ebers Papyrus)
results.original_text: taken, several ointments, and various suppositories. One mixture is in the nature of an infusion: ANOTHER FOR THE UXEDU Bark-of-the-Pomegranate-tree-soaked-in-Beer Keep moist in a jug of Water. Strain a sufficient amount each morning and let the Patient drink. There is no evidence that this remedy benefited the patient, but at least it could be drunk. This feat hardly seems possible with the next mixture in which the Flesh-of-a-Live-Cow was combined with Freshly-baked Bread, Incense, Lettuce, and Sweet Beer, and ordered to be 'drunk' for four days. Another mixture of Ass's Milk with Dough, Wine, and Bitter Beer, was also ordered to be 'drunk' for four days. Sometimes the uxedu caused spots to appear on the skin. A mixture of Onions, Turpentine, and Cow's Milk, drunk for four days, knocked the spots off. When the uxedu-disease was complicated by the uha-disease the Scribe was rather at a loss. He counselled a salve of Sea-salt, Honey, and Cow's Bile; but at a later stage, having doubts as to the potency of Cow's Bile in this connection, he proceeded to repeat the salve, substituting however Cow's Brain for Cow's Bile. Even now he was not satisfied that the remedy would stand the test and seemingly in despair he resorted to what many a harried modern practitioner is driven to do to-day—he recommended a palliative. In this case it was the old familiar household remedy, Castor Oil, that he employed; but instead of administering it in the old familiar household way, namely a tablespoonful internally overnight, he ordered the oil to be rubbed in. Albeit, he took a roundabout way of saying so: ANOTHER TO DRIVE OUT THE UXEDU Oil-expressed-from-the-Seeds-of-the-Castor-Oil-bush Smear therewith any person who has the uha-swelling.
results.translation: The text describes several ointments and suppositories, as well as a series of mixtures prescribed for the 'uxedu' disease [an ancient Egyptian pathological category, exact identification uncertain]. One preparation takes the form of an infusion: ANOTHER REMEDY FOR THE UXEDU — Bark of the Pomegranate tree soaked in Beer, kept moist in a jug of Water. Each morning a sufficient quantity is to be strained off and given to the patient to drink. There is no evidence that this remedy benefited the patient, but at least it was drinkable. The same can hardly be said of the next mixture, in which the Flesh of a Live Cow was combined with Freshly-baked Bread, Incense, Lettuce, and Sweet Beer, and prescribed to be 'drunk' over four days. Another mixture consisting of Ass's Milk with Dough, Wine, and Bitter Beer was likewise prescribed to be 'drunk' over four days; it appears somewhat more liquid than the former preparation, though barely. When the uxedu produced spots on the skin, a mixture of Onions, Turpentine, and Cow's Milk, drunk over four days, was prescribed to eliminate the spots — a preparation that might also have served as a topical application. When the uxedu-disease was complicated by the uha-disease [another uncertain Egyptian pathological category, possibly involving swelling or abscess], the scribe appeared uncertain in his approach. He first prescribed a salve of Sea-salt, Honey, and Cow's Bile; then, having doubts about the efficacy of Cow's Bile in this context, he revised the formula, substituting Cow's Brain for Cow's Bile. Still unsatisfied that the remedy would prove effective, he fell back — much as many a pressed modern practitioner might — upon a palliative. The familiar household remedy of Castor Oil was employed; however, rather than administering it internally in the usual manner (a spoonful taken overnight), he prescribed that the oil be rubbed on externally. He expressed this instruction in an indirect manner: ANOTHER REMEDY TO DRIVE OUT THE UXEDU — Oil expressed from the Seeds of the Castor-Oil bush: Smear it upon any person who has the uha-swelling.

entities_mentioned.name: Uha disease / uha-swelling

entities_mentioned.name: Lettuce

entities_mentioned.name: Ass's Milk

entities_mentioned.name: Onions

results.translator_notes: This segment is a secondary English source — a modern (early 20th century) scholarly commentary and translation of the Ebers Papyrus, not a direct transcription of the original hieratic Egyptian text. The voice alternates between paraphrase of the ancient prescriptions and editorial commentary by the modern author. The chapter headings ('THE AAA DISEASE', 'ANOTHER FOR THE UXEDU', etc.) appear to represent translated rubrics from the original papyrus. The term 'uxedu' is a transliteration of an Egyptian disease name; its precise pathological identity remains debated in Egyptological and medical-historical scholarship. The 'uha' condition similarly lacks a secure modern identification. The translator's commentary ('There is no evidence that this remedy benefited the patient...') is authorial editorializing, not part of the ancient text. Page numbers 121–122 refer to the modern book edition, not the papyrus. The original OCR text contained 'olor Col' which appears to be an OCR artifact and has been silently removed as unintelligible interpolation.

results.title_inferred: The 'Wood-like Uha-Disease' Salves and the Heart & Circulatory System in the Ebers Papyrus
results.original_text: desperate when he came to deal with the 'wood-like uha-disease' occurring by itself. Salves were all that he could offer against it. They made up in picturesqueness what they most assuredly lacked in utility. An ointment of Hippopotamus Oil and Honey would perhaps be more sticky than picturesque. Another of Soot-from-the-bet'a-Pot with Honey and Sweet Myrrh would seem to be not only sticky but dirty. Another made of Ass's Head, Corn, and Oil, can only be called asinine, surely. A fourth, wherein Stone-from-Memphis was ground up with Seeds and Grain in the Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son was without a doubt gritty. It had to be persisted in for seven days but does not seem to have been very successful since it was followed successively by other salves which had to be rubbed in for eight days, nine days, and ten days, respectively. Finally there remained a salve calculated to arouse feelings of homicide in every poetic breast; a salve which sought to combine Seeds-from-the-Blossoms-of-the-Earth and Fresh Cream with the Hoof-of-an-Ass! CHAPTER XX THE HEART AND CIRCULATORY SYSTEM To the student the most interesting section of the Papyrus is undoubtedly the chapter dealing with the Heart, or rather with the vascular system. At first sight one is in danger of assuming that the knowledge of the Heart and of its action enjoyed by these Ancients was much greater than it was in reality. But as one reads on it is clearly established that they made the fatal mistake of regarding the Heart not as a pump but as a mere well. Perhaps had they made a closer examination of the Heart itself they would have discovered the existence of the all-important valves, when (it is not too much to believe) they would in a flash have grasped the real function of the organ. For they had arrived at all save that. The section well merits setting down here at length. Unfortunately it cannot be set forth in its entirety for much of it is untranslatable, and indeed a great part of it remains so.
results.translation: The ancient physician was at a loss when confronted with the so-called 'wood-like uha-disease' [Egyptian: uha, possibly a stiffening or sclerotic condition] occurring in isolation. Salves were the only remedies he could prescribe against it. These preparations possessed a certain vivid character that thoroughly compensated for their evident lack of therapeutic utility. An ointment compounded of hippopotamus fat and honey would have been more adhesive than picturesque. A second preparation, consisting of soot collected from the 'bet'a-pot' [a type of Egyptian cooking or heating vessel] combined with honey and sweet myrrh [Commiphora sp.], would appear to have been not merely adhesive but unclean. A third, composed of an ass's head, grain [corn], and oil, can only be described as foolish. A fourth, in which 'Stone-from-Memphis' [likely a calcareous or alabaster mineral from the Memphis region] was ground together with seeds and grain in the milk of a woman who had given birth to a son, was undoubtedly gritty in texture. This last preparation was to be applied persistently for seven days; however, it does not appear to have been particularly successful, since it was followed in succession by further salves requiring application for eight days, nine days, and ten days respectively. Finally, there remained one salve apt to provoke murderous feelings in every poetically-inclined physician: a preparation combining seeds from the blossoms of the earth [unidentified plant material] and fresh cream with the hoof of an ass. CHAPTER XX: THE HEART AND CIRCULATORY SYSTEM. For the student, the most interesting section of the Papyrus is undoubtedly the chapter treating the Heart—or rather, the vascular system as a whole. At first reading, one risks the erroneous assumption that the knowledge of the Heart and its function possessed by these ancient Egyptians was considerably more advanced than it actually was. Upon closer reading, however, it becomes clear that they committed the fundamental error of conceiving the Heart not as a pump but merely as a reservoir or well. Had they examined the Heart itself more closely, they might have discovered the existence of its all-important valves, and one may reasonably suppose that, upon doing so, they would have immediately grasped the true function of the organ—for in every other respect they had approached a correct understanding. This section deserves to be transcribed here at length. Unfortunately, it cannot be presented in its entirety, as much of it remains untranslatable, and indeed a considerable portion of the text has not yet been rendered into any modern language.

entities_mentioned.name: uha-disease (wood-like)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: possibly a sclerotic, fibrotic, or rheumatic condition causing stiffness, or a skin/tissue hardening disorder
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian term 'uha' is not conclusively identified; 'wood-like' qualifier suggests rigidity or induration of tissue.

entities_mentioned.name: Hippopotamus Oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a base for topical salves in ancient Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Common excipient and mild antimicrobial in ancient Egyptian pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Soot-from-the-bet'a-Pot
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: carbon soot from a specific type of Egyptian vessel (bet'a); vessel type unidentified
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'bet'a' refers to an Egyptian pot or furnace; soot used as a topical ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: Sweet Myrrh
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora myrrha or related Commiphora species
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely attested aromatic resin in Egyptian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Ass's Head
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a materia medica ingredient; likely rendered or processed in some manner.

entities_mentioned.name: Corn (Grain)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: emmer wheat (Triticum dicoccum) or barley (Hordeum vulgare), most likely
entities_mentioned.notes: Egyptian 'corn' in this context refers to cereal grain, not Zea mays (maize), which is a New World plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Stone-from-Memphis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: possibly limestone, alabaster (calcite), or natron from the Memphis region of Egypt
entities_mentioned.notes: Memphis was associated with fine limestone and alabaster quarrying; exact mineral unspecified.

entities_mentioned.name: Milk-of-a-Woman-who-has-borne-a-Son
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: human breast milk from a mother of a male child
entities_mentioned.notes: A common ingredient in ancient Egyptian prescriptions, believed to carry special ritual or medicinal properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Seeds-from-the-Blossoms-of-the-Earth
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: unidentified; possibly a generic term for wild-gathered plant seeds
entities_mentioned.notes: The phrase is poetic and may refer to a specific but now-unidentified Egyptian plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Fresh Cream
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: fresh dairy cream, likely from cattle
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a topical emollient base.

entities_mentioned.name: Hoof-of-an-Ass
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely ground or burned and used as an ingredient in the salve.

results.translator_notes: This segment is secondary scholarly commentary (early 20th century, likely by John F. Nunn or a contemporaneous Egyptologist) on the Ebers Papyrus, rather than a direct translation of the Papyrus itself. The author's tone is openly ironic and editorial. The 'uha-disease' treated with these salves corresponds to Ebers Papyrus prescriptions for what the text describes as a hardening or stiffening condition. The ailments and preparations discussed span both the 'aaa disease' section and the transition into the vascular/heart section of the Papyrus. The seven-, eight-, nine-, and ten-day application schedules reflect the stepped therapeutic protocols documented in the Papyrus. The chapter on the Heart and circulatory system referenced here corresponds to the well-known Ebers Papyrus passages (Eb. 854–856 and related) describing the 'metu' (vessels) and their connection to the heart.

results.title_inferred: Diseases of the Heart and Circulatory System: Egyptian Medical Diagnostics
results.original_text: Heart absolutely deny him their service. When their clothes weigh them down, behold it is the dehert-disease. When his dehert-disease breaks out, behold it is dangerous. (Uncertain.) When the Heart is sick, behold there is an Oppression of the Heart, or it is an Overflow of Blood which arises in the Heart. It falls down and approaches his diaphragm, while his Heart feels an aversion. When the mast-disease is in the Heart, behold there is a contraction of the Chest. His Heart has in its place Blood from the Anus which protrudes a little because of the disease. It is a Fever of the Heart. If his Heart quietens down a little under the disease he will eat, but only fastidiously. When the Heart consumes itself, behold it is an Accumulation of Blood in the Heart. When there is mast-disease of the Heart because of the uxedu, behold his Heart is small in the inside of his Belly. The uxedu falls upon his Heart. He is aat-sick. He is mast-sick. When there is Weakness as the result of old age, behold it is the uxedu at his Heart. When there is a rising of the Heart, behold it lifts itself at his left breast. It lifts itself on its fat. It flies forth from its place. Its layers of fat are on his Left Side so that they collect in his shoulder. When the dehert-disease of his Heart appears frequently, behold his Heart swims and sinks downwards and is not in its place. When his Heart is in its right place the Fat of the Heart is on the left side. It neither rises upwards nor falls downwards. It remains soundly in its place. When his Heart trembles and there is much fat under his Left breast, behold it is his Heart which causes a little of the sinking because his disease is spreading. When his Abdomen palpitates, it is caused by a swelling therein. When the Mouth is like Fire and becomes fatigued and the Heart is weary, behold it is because a Fire forces itself towards his Heart. His Heart burns with Fire like a person on whom
results.translation: The Heart [vessels/channels] utterly deny him their service. When [a patient's] clothes weigh him down [i.e., he feels burdened by the weight of his garments], behold it is the dehert-disease [a named Egyptian pathological category, possibly related to cardiac or thoracic heaviness]. When his dehert-disease erupts, behold it is dangerous. (Note: passage is uncertain in the source.) When the Heart is sick, behold there is an Oppression of the Heart [angina-like symptom], or it is an Overflow of Blood arising in the Heart. [The blood] falls downward and approaches his diaphragm, while his Heart feels an aversion [i.e., revulsion or discomfort]. When the mast-disease [another named Egyptian condition] is in the Heart, behold there is a contraction of the Chest. His Heart has in its place Blood from the Anus which protrudes slightly because of the disease. It is a Fever of the Heart. If his Heart quietens somewhat under the disease, he will eat, but only fastidiously [i.e., with little appetite or selectiveness]. When the Heart consumes itself, behold it is an Accumulation of Blood in the Heart. When there is mast-disease of the Heart caused by the uxedu [a pathological substance or humor in Egyptian medicine, possibly putrefaction or a morbid fluid], behold his Heart is small within his Belly. The uxedu falls upon his Heart. He is aat-sick [afflicted with the aat-condition]. He is mast-sick [afflicted with the mast-condition]. When there is Weakness resulting from old age, behold it is the uxedu [acting] at his Heart. When there is a rising of the Heart, behold it lifts itself at his left breast. It lifts itself upon its fat [pericardial fat]. It flies forth from its place. Its layers of fat are on his Left Side so that they accumulate in his shoulder. When the dehert-disease of his Heart appears frequently, behold his Heart swims and sinks downwards and is not in its place. When his Heart is in its right place, the Fat of the Heart is on the left side. It neither rises upward nor falls downward. It remains soundly in its place. When his Heart trembles and there is much fat beneath his left breast, behold it is his Heart causing some degree of sinking because his disease is spreading. When his Abdomen palpitates, it is caused by a swelling therein. When the Mouth is like Fire and becomes fatigued and the Heart is weary, behold it is because a Fire forces itself toward his Heart. His Heart burns with Fire like a person upon whom [text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: dehert-disease
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly cardiac or thoracic heaviness; may correspond to symptoms of congestive heart failure or pericarditis
entities_mentioned.notes: An untranslated Egyptian disease category characterized by heaviness, recurrence, and cardiac involvement. The term 'dehert' is a transliteration of an Egyptian word with no certain modern equivalent.

entities_mentioned.name: mast-disease
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a condition involving chest constriction and cardiac symptoms; some scholars associate it with inflammation or fluid accumulation
entities_mentioned.notes: Another named Egyptian pathological category. Associated with chest contraction, fever of the heart, and poor appetite. May relate to the term meaning 'illness' or a specific organ-related condition.

entities_mentioned.name: uxedu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A putrefactive or morbid humor/substance in Egyptian medicine; possibly analogous to a concept of toxins or rotting matter affecting organs
entities_mentioned.notes: The uxedu appears to be a pathological agent or humor that falls upon the heart and causes weakness, smallness of the heart, and aat- and mast-sickness. It may correspond loosely to concepts of putrefaction or systemic toxic decay.

entities_mentioned.name: aat-sickness
entities_mentioned.notes: A named Egyptian disease state, possibly referring to generalized debility or a specific organ-related ailment. Insufficient context for modern identification.

entities_mentioned.name: Oppression of the Heart
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly angina pectoris or cardiac distress
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a condition of the sick heart, possibly reflecting symptomatic descriptions of ischemic or pressure-related cardiac pain.

entities_mentioned.name: Overflow of Blood in the Heart
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly cardiac congestion, pericardial effusion, or hemorrhagic cardiac event
entities_mentioned.notes: Blood is described as arising in the heart, falling toward the diaphragm, causing aversion in the heart.

entities_mentioned.name: Fever of the Heart
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly endocarditis, myocarditis, or febrile cardiac condition
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with mast-disease and abnormal blood presence.

entities_mentioned.name: Accumulation of Blood in the Heart
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as occurring when 'the Heart consumes itself.'

entities_mentioned.name: Fat of the Heart
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an anatomical marker of correct cardiac position; displacement of this fat is associated with disease states.

entities_mentioned.name: Fire of the Heart / Burning of the Heart
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly pyrexia, esophageal or cardiac burning, or febrile illness; could include gastroesophageal reflux or endocarditis
entities_mentioned.notes: Described in conjunction with a fatigued mouth and weary heart; a fire is said to force itself toward the heart.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a continuation of the Papyrus Ebers cardiac diagnostic section (Ebers Papyrus, chapters on the heart and vessels). The source text is already an English translation of the original Egyptian hieratic, likely from the Joachim or Grundriss tradition, and has been re-cleaned here for archival purposes. The parenthetical '(Uncertain.)' appears in the source and likely reflects the original translator's note on a corrupt or ambiguous passage. Technical Egyptian medical terms (dehert, mast, uxedu, aat) have been retained in transliteration as they have no secure modern equivalents. The anatomical and physiological concepts reflect ancient Egyptian cardiocentrism and the metu [vessel] theory of physiology, in which the heart is the center of bodily function and fluid distribution. The chunk ends mid-sentence, indicating continuation in the next chunk.

results.title_inferred: Heart Vessels, Symptoms, and Remedies from the Papyrus Ebers
results.original_text:
who has eaten the Bull-fruit of the Sycamore-tree. When the Heart is miserable and is beside itself, behold it is the Breath of the heb-wxer Priest that causes it through the hollow of his hand. It penetrates right down to the Rectum in such a manner that the Heart comes forth and loses its way under the disease. When dryness befalls his Heart, behold it is the dryness of Fire that befalls it. He sighs often and his Heart is eaten up with anger; this is because his Heart is full of Blood, which in turn is due to drinking Warm Water and eating Bad Food. When his Heart is afflicted and has tasted sadness, behold his Heart is closed in and darkness is in his body because of anger which is eating up his Heart. When his Flesh is quite withered like the stilled Heart of a Person who has Found-the-Way (died), behold it is his Flesh which has become immovable under it, like the immovable flesh of a Person who has gone Into-the-Wide. When it is Fate that he shall go Up-on-High, behold it is his Heart which determines that he shall go Up-on-High.

The section concludes with various remedies for affections of the heart, some of which are mixed up with a little 'anatomy':

Man has Twelve Heart-vessels which spread out to all his Limbs. There are Two Vessels in the region of his Breast which cause inflammation in the anus. Use against this: Fresh Dates, Leaves-of-the-Castor-Oil-Plant, Fruit-of-the-Sycamore — mix into one in Water, strain, and let it be taken for four days. There are Two Vessels to his Thigh. When he suffers from his Thigh and both Thighs are a-tremble, say thou for it: 'This Vessel that runs to the region of his Thigh has conceived the disease.' Use against it: Fresh Milk, Wormwood, Natron — Cook into one and make the Patient drink for four days. There are Two Vessels to his Arm. When he suffers in his Arm and his fingers tremble, then thou sayest to him: 'They are swollen glands.' Prepare against it:
results.translation:
...[The text continues from a prior passage describing a person] who has eaten the Bull-fruit of the Sycamore-tree. When the Heart is miserable and beside itself, it is the breath [exhalation] of the heb-wxer [a ritual Egyptian priestly title] Priest that causes it, acting through the hollow of his hand. It penetrates all the way down to the Rectum, in such a manner that the Heart comes forth and loses its way under the disease. When dryness befalls his Heart, it is the dryness of Fire that befalls it. He sighs often and his Heart is consumed with anger; this is because his Heart is full of Blood, which in turn results from drinking Warm Water and eating Bad Food. When his Heart is afflicted and has tasted sadness, his Heart is closed in and darkness is within his body, due to anger which is consuming his Heart. When his Flesh is entirely withered, like the stilled Heart of a Person who has Found-the-Way [i.e., died], it is his Flesh which has become immovable beneath it, like the immovable flesh of a Person who has gone Into-the-Wide [i.e., into the beyond]. When it is Fate that he shall go Up-on-High [i.e., die], it is his Heart which determines that he shall go Up-on-High.

The section concludes with various remedies for affections of the heart, some of which are interspersed with rudimentary 'anatomy':

A man has Twelve Heart-vessels [metu] which spread out to all his Limbs. There are Two Vessels in the region of his Breast which cause inflammation in the anus. Use against this: Fresh Dates, Leaves-of-the-Castor-Oil-Plant [Ricinus communis], Fruit-of-the-Sycamore [Ficus sycomorus] — combine into one preparation in Water, strain, and let it be administered for four days. There are Two Vessels leading to his Thigh. When he suffers in his Thigh and both Thighs are trembling, pronounce over it: 'This Vessel that runs to the region of his Thigh has contracted the disease.' Use against it: Fresh Milk, Wormwood [Artemisia sp.], Natron [natural sodium carbonate/bicarbonate salt] — cook together into one preparation and have the Patient drink it for four days. There are Two Vessels leading to his Arm. When he suffers in his Arm and his fingers tremble, say to him: 'They are swollen glands.' Prepare against it: [text continues in subsequent passage]

entities_mentioned.name: Bull-fruit of the Sycamore-tree
entities_mentioned.notes: The sycomore fig (Ficus sycomorus) was common in ancient Egypt; 'Bull-fruit' may refer to a particular large or male variety.

entities_mentioned.name: heb-wxer Priest
entities_mentioned.notes: A ritual Egyptian priestly designation; the transliteration 'heb-wxer' is tentative and may vary across editions. The priest's breath or exhalation is implicated as a cause of cardiac distress, possibly referring to magical or demonic causation.

entities_mentioned.name: Fresh Dates
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly used in Egyptian medical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Leaves-of-the-Castor-Oil-Plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Castor plant leaves used medicinally in Egyptian papyri; identified with high confidence by Egyptological consensus.

entities_mentioned.name: Fruit-of-the-Sycamore
entities_mentioned.notes: Sycomore fig fruit, a staple medicinal ingredient in the Ebers Papyrus.

entities_mentioned.name: Wormwood
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia absinthium or related Artemisia sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Egyptian term translated as 'Wormwood' is often identified as Artemisia, though exact species is debated.

entities_mentioned.name: Natron
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Naturally occurring sodium carbonate/bicarbonate (sodium sesquicarbonate, trona)
entities_mentioned.notes: Natron (Egyptian: ntrj) was a naturally occurring salt used widely in ancient Egyptian medicine, preservation, and ritual.

entities_mentioned.name: Twelve Heart-vessels (metu)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Egyptian concept of 'metu' (often translated as vessels or channels) does not map directly onto modern anatomical vessels; these twelve vessels were thought to distribute vital substances throughout the body.

entities_mentioned.name: Fresh Milk
entities_mentioned.notes: A common vehicle for medicinal preparations in ancient Egyptian texts.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a translation of the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE), one of the oldest extant medical texts. The English here is itself a secondary translation (likely from the German of Georg Ebers or a subsequent English rendering); the translator of this edition uses euphemistic circumlocutions for death ('Found-the-Way,' 'Into-the-Wide,' 'Up-on-High'). The 'anatomy' of twelve vessels is the Egyptian metu system, a proto-physiological framework unrelated to modern vascular anatomy. The text breaks off mid-prescription for the arm remedy; a subsequent chunk presumably continues. The 'heb-wxer' priestly designation is uncertain in transliteration and may reflect OCR or editorial variation. Conditions treated include cardiac distress (with aetiologies attributed to anger, bad food, warm water, and magical causes), rectal inflammation linked to breast vessels, thigh trembling, and arm/finger trembling with swollen glands.
